title: Rare Epithelial Ovarian Cancers: Low Grade Serous and Mucinous Carcinomas
journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Medicine
summary: Endometriosis is strongly associated with the development of two histological subtypes of ovarian epithelial cancer: endometrioid carcinoma and clear-cell carcinoma. While mucinous and low-grade serous carcinomas represent less common forms of epithelial ovarian cancer, each comprising fewer than 10% of all cases, they remain clinically significant rare histotypes. The endometriosis-associated malignancies (endometrioid and clear-cell cancers) are distinguished from the more prevalent high-grade serous ovarian carcinomas, suggesting distinct etiological pathways in ovarian cancer development. These rare epithelial subtypes present distinct clinical challenges due to their lower prevalence, which may complicate diagnosis and treatment approaches compared to more common ovarian cancer types. Understanding the specific associations between endometriosis and particular histological subtypes is important for risk stratification and potentially developing targeted surveillance strategies in patients with endometriosis.

title: Ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinomas with infiltrative “adenofibroma-like” morphology and aberrant β-catenin expression: tumors that co-express CDX2 and LEF1 with frequent neuroendocrine marker expression, diminished/lost PAX8 and possible association with endometrioid type II SCOUTs in the fallopian tube
journal: International journal of gynecological pathology : official journal of the International Society of Gynecological Pathologists
summary: This study describes a distinctive subgroup of ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinomas characterized by aberrant β-catenin expression (associated with CTNNB1 mutations) and an adenofibroma-like infiltrative growth pattern. All cases showed strong co-expression of β-catenin with the transcriptional regulators CDX2 and LEF1, markers typically associated with Wnt/β-catenin pathway activation. A notable feature was frequent neuroendocrine marker expression, particularly synaptophysin (5/6 cases) and chromogranin (3/6 cases), combined with diminished or lost PAX8 expression, distinguishing these tumors from typical endometrioid carcinomas. Two of five cases with available fallopian tube tissue contained multifocal type II stem cell outgrowths (SCOUTs) with aberrant β-catenin expression, suggesting a potential precursor lesion. The immunophenotype was consistent with endometrial origin, showing estrogen receptor positivity and absence of CK20, effectively excluding secondary adenocarcinomas from appendiceal or other gastrointestinal sites. These findings identify a molecularly and morphologically distinct endometriosis-associated endometrioid carcinoma subtype with potential origins in aberrant stem cell populations within the fallopian tube.

title: Mesonephric and mesonephric-like adenocarcinomas of gynecologic origin: A single-center experience with molecular characterization, treatment, and oncologic outcomes
summary: Mesonephric adenocarcinoma (MA) and mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) are rare gynecologic malignancies with distinct anatomic origins: MA occurs in the uterine cervix independent of human papillomavirus, while MLA arises in the uterine corpus, ovary, and adjacent tissues, with MLA linked to endometriosis-associated malignant transformation. Both tumor types are characterized by near-ubiquitous alterations in the MAPK signaling pathway, with KRAS mutations present in 77% of cases and additional mutations in NRAS, BRAF, and NF1, along with PIK3CA mutations in 30% of uterine and ovarian MLAs. Immunophenotypically, both MA and MLA express GATA3, thyroid transcription factor-1, calretinin, and CD10, while being largely estrogen and progesterone receptor negative. Platinum-doublet chemotherapy demonstrated the highest response rate at 77.8% in uterine MLA patients, whereas immunotherapy and targeted therapies such as temsirolimus showed limited efficacy, suggesting conventional chemotherapy remains the standard approach. The study proposes future investigation of dual MEK/RAF and FAK inhibitors (avutometinib plus defactinib) as targeted therapeutic options, mirroring successful strategies in other MAPK-driven malignancies such as low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma.

title: Molecular classification of ovarian high-grade serous/endometrioid carcinomas through multi-omics analysis: JGOG3025-TR2 study
summary: This multi-omics study identified four distinct molecular subtypes of high-grade serous/endometrioid ovarian carcinomas, with significant implications for prognosis and treatment selection. A novel subtype designated C4, characterized by endometrial differentiation (HGEC-type), exhibited a homologous recombination proficient status and demonstrated favorable clinical outcomes despite its malignant classification. In contrast, C1 tumors with CCNE1 amplification showed poor survival, while C2 and C3 subtypes harbored frequent BRCA1/2 alterations associated with homologous recombination deficiency and enhanced sensitivity to platinum-based and PARP inhibitor therapies. The HGEC-type tumors (C4) displayed distinctive features including absence of BRCA mutations, low HRD scores, reduced genomic complexity, and an endometrium-like DNA methylation profile, distinguishing them from classical high-grade serous carcinomas. These findings suggest that molecular profiling can stratify endometriosis-associated ovarian carcinomas into clinically meaningful subtypes with different therapeutic vulnerabilities and prognostic trajectories, enabling more personalized treatment approaches.

title: STK11 (LKB1) Immunohistochemistry is a Sensitive and Specific Marker for STK11 Adnexal Tumors
summary: STK11 adnexal tumors are rare, aggressive malignancies arising from para-adnexal soft tissues that are strongly associated with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, a condition caused by germline STK11 loss-of-function mutations. These tumors are characterized by complete loss of cytoplasmic STK11 protein expression, making STK11 immunohistochemistry a highly sensitive and specific diagnostic marker; STK11 alterations are identified in 92% of sequenced tumors. STK11 normally functions as a tumor suppressor that activates AMPK, which regulates energy homeostasis through phosphorylation of mTORC1 and HIF-1α, controlling cell metabolism, growth, and apoptosis. Clinically, STK11 adnexal tumors follow an aggressive course with frequent metastases (50%), high recurrence rates (82%), and 18% mortality, often requiring multimodal treatment including surgery, chemotherapy, aromatase inhibitors, or everolimus. The tumors display a variable immunophenotype with frequent positivity for hormone receptors (estrogen, progesterone, androgen), calretinin, inhibin, and desmin, while remaining negative for mesothelioma markers and TTF-1, allowing them to be distinguished from morphologic mimics. Accurate recognition of these tumors is critical given their aggressive behavior and the need for appropriate clinical surveillance and management strategies in affected patients.

title: Establishment and characterization of ovarian clear cell carcinoma patient-derived xenografts
summary:
# Summary

This study established and characterized patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), an endometriosis-associated malignancy, to better understand its molecular drivers and therapeutic vulnerabilities. The OCCC PDX models recapitulated the frequent mutations found in primary tumors, particularly in ARID1A and PIK3CA—genes involved in chromatin remodeling and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling—as well as mutations in ATM, PTEN, and BRCA1. Beyond point mutations, the tumors exhibited significant copy number alterations across multiple chromosomes and aberrant DNA methylation patterns associated with PRC2/histone methylation gene sets, affecting critical regulatory genes including GATA2, HNF1A, and WT1. Additional signaling pathways implicated in OCCC pathogenesis included MAPK, ERBB, and TGFβ/SMAD signaling. The PDX models demonstrated chemoresistance to platinum-based therapies (carboplatin/paclitaxel), consistent with poor clinical outcomes, and high expression of APOBEC3B was identified as a potential biomarker for platinum sensitivity. These comprehensive PDX models provide valuable preclinical platforms for studying OCCC biology and testing novel therapeutic strategies targeting the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and other dysregulated pathways in this aggressive cancer.

title: Pembrolizumab in Patients With Advanced Clear Cell Gynecological Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Clear cell gynecological cancers (CCGCs) are a distinct subgroup of malignant tumors strongly associated with endometriosis and characterized by recurrent mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, and PTEN. These tumors possess an immune-rich microenvironment driven by mechanisms including mismatch repair deficiency, ARID1A alterations, and elevated IL-6, with high expression of PD-L1 and PD-L2 checkpoint ligands. The PEACOCC trial demonstrated that pembrolizumab achieved a 42% disease response rate in patients with previously treated advanced CCGC, with durable overall survival improvement, establishing PD-1 inhibitors as effective immunotherapies for this aggressive cancer type. While mismatch repair deficiency is an important predictor of immune checkpoint inhibitor sensitivity, neither PD-L1 positivity, ARID1A loss, nor p53 aberrant expression were associated with pembrolizumab response in this study. Combination immunotherapies with anti-angiogenic agents and alternative checkpoint inhibitors including durvalumab, sintilimab, and nivolumab have also shown clinical activity in advanced clear cell ovarian and endometrial cancers, though pembrolizumab and combination PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade remain the leading approaches for endometriosis-associated malignant transformation.

title: Mesonephric-like adenocarcinomas of the uterine corpus: report of a case series and review of the literature indicating poor prognosis for this subtype of endometrial adenocarcinoma
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (ML-AC) represents a recently recognized and aggressive subtype of endometrial adenocarcinoma characterized by mixed morphology with co-expression of both mesonephric and Müllerian markers. Molecularly, ML-AC is strongly associated with KRAS mutations at codon 12 (predominantly G12V) and POLE mutations, with retained mismatch repair protein expression suggesting a copy number-low phenotype; notably, PD-L1 is consistently absent across all tested cases. Clinically, ML-AC presents with vaginal bleeding as the leading symptom but demonstrates aggressive behavior with high rates of recurrent disease and substantial risk for distant metastatic spread, with the lungs being the most common site of distant involvement. These findings collectively indicate that ML-AC carries a poor prognosis compared to other endometrial adenocarcinoma subtypes, warranting distinct clinical recognition and potentially modified treatment approaches tailored to its unique molecular and biological characteristics.

title: Infiltration of CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells and expression of PD-1 and PD-L1 in ovarian clear cell carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) frequently arises from endometriosis and is characterized by high frequencies of ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations, which drive abnormal cell cycle progression and influence survival outcomes. The tumor microenvironment exhibits a paradoxical immune profile in which CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells accumulate preferentially in adjacent nonneoplastic regions rather than within tumor tissue, and counterintuitively, high CD8+ T-cell density in these adjacent areas correlates with worse progression-free survival. This unfavorable immune phenotype is driven partly by PD-L1 expression on tumor and immune cells, which inhibits anti-tumor CD8+ T-cell function, though PD-1/PD-L1 expression itself does not directly predict response to checkpoint inhibitor therapy. VEGF inhibition can remodel the OCCC stromal environment and restore T-cell function to enhance immunotherapy efficacy, and clinical trials of PD-1/PD-L1 pathway blockers (including pembrolizumab and durvalumab) are underway for recurrent OCCC. These findings suggest that CD8+ T-cell localization and stromal modulation, rather than simple checkpoint marker expression, are critical determinants of prognosis and treatment response in endometriosis-associated OCCC.

title: Female urethral diverticular carcinoma: a case report and review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

Female urethral diverticular carcinoma (UDC) is a rare malignancy arising from urethral diverticula, typically presenting with hematuria, dysuria, and voiding dysfunction, with poorly differentiated urothelial adenocarcinoma being the most common histological type. The condition develops through carcinogenic transformation of glandular epithelium following chronic infections, recurrent periurethral infections, vaginal delivery trauma, or previous urethral surgery. Diagnosis relies on multimodal imaging including MRI, ultrasonography, CT scanning, and cystourethroscopy, combined with urine cytology and definitive pathological confirmation through immunohistochemical markers (CK7, CK8&18, EMA, GATA3). Treatment primarily involves surgical urethrectomy, with advanced cases requiring multimodal approaches combining radiotherapy and platinum-based chemotherapy, while emerging targeted therapies include HER2 inhibitors and NK cell-based immunotherapy. Prognosis is influenced by multiple factors including age, clinical stage, tumor location, volume, and histological type, with adenocarcinoma demonstrating superior five-year cancer-free survival rates (54%) compared to transitional cell carcinoma (43%) and squamous cell carcinoma (35%).

title: Female adnexal tumor of probable Wolffian origin (FATWO): a case report and literature review
journal: International Journal of Clinical and Experimental Pathology
summary: Female adnexal tumor of probable Wolffian origin (FATWO) is a rare neoplasm characterized by a distinctive immunophenotypic profile, including high expression of Vimentin, CD10, and hormone receptors, along with variable sex cord-stromal marker expression. The tumor cells were notably negative for GATA-3 and thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF1), helping to distinguish FATWO from other ovarian and adnexal neoplasms. A CTNNB1 mutation (c.98C>G, p.S33C) was identified in the tumor, suggesting involvement of Wnt/β-catenin pathway alterations in FATWO pathogenesis. The standard surgical management approach involved comprehensive tumor resection combined with hysterectomy and bilateral adnexectomy to achieve complete disease control. This case highlights the importance of recognizing FATWO's unique immunophenotypic and molecular features for accurate diagnosis and appropriate surgical staging in patients with adnexal masses.

title: Molecular Biomarkers in Borderline Ovarian Tumors: Towards Personalized Treatment and Prognostic Assessment
summary:
# Summary

This review examines molecular biomarkers in borderline ovarian tumors (BOTs) with implications for endometriosis-associated malignancies. BRAF mutations, found in 23-48% of serous BOTs and mucinous BOTs, activate the MAPK signaling pathway and are associated with earlier-stage disease, favorable prognosis, and low recurrence risk, likely through induction of cellular senescence. In contrast, BOTs lacking BRAF mutations show higher progression risk to aggressive low-grade serous ovarian carcinomas. The tumor suppressor p16INK4a exhibits progressive overexpression from benign to borderline to malignant tumors, with elevated stromal expression correlating with malignancy and tumor aggressiveness, and potentially predicting responsiveness to PARP inhibitors and immunotherapy. Serum biomarkers CA 125 and calprotectin display elevated levels in BOTs compared to benign tumors but lower levels than invasive cancers, with rising CA 125 being an early sign of malignant transformation. KRAS mutations, present in most serous BOTs alongside or independent of BRAF mutations, activate MAP-kinase and downregulate PI3K-AKT pathways to promote cellular proliferation, providing complementary oncogenic mechanisms in ovarian tumorigenesis.

title: Primary low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma of the ovary with mesenteric metastasis: An unusual case report
summary: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LGESS) is a rare malignant tumor that frequently originates from endometriosis, particularly in perimenopausal women, and presents with abnormal vaginal bleeding and pelvic pain. The tumor cells exhibit a characteristic immunohistochemical profile, being diffusely positive for CD10, estrogen receptor (ER), and progesterone receptor (PR), while lacking expression of cyclin D1, BCOR, pan-cytokeratin, smooth muscle antibody, and desmin. Cytogenetically, LGESS is frequently associated with JAZF1-SUZ12 gene fusions and PHF1 abnormalities, which serve as important diagnostic markers. Emerging evidence suggests that interferon-induced transmembrane protein-1 (IFITM1) represents a new immunohistochemical marker for LGESS diagnosis. The hormone-responsive nature of these tumors, evidenced by ER and PR positivity, supports the use of progesterone hormone therapy as a treatment option following surgical resection. This case report highlights an unusual presentation of primary ovarian LGESS with mesenteric metastasis, expanding understanding of the clinical spectrum and metastatic potential of this endometriosis-associated malignancy.

title: Sister Mary Joseph’s nodule as the first presenting sign of advanced fallopian tube carcinoma: A case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of advanced fallopian tube carcinoma manifesting as Sister Mary Joseph's nodule, a metastatic umbilical lesion indicative of extensive intra-abdominal malignancy. The patient was diagnosed with stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of the left fallopian tube with pelvic lymph node metastasis and a pathogenic BRCA2 mutation, presenting clinically with abdominal pain, abdominal mass, and vaginal bleeding. Immunohistochemical analysis of the umbilical mass confirmed metastatic fallopian tube adenocarcinoma through positive expression of cytokeratin 7, paired box 8, and other müllerian markers. The patient was treated with debulking surgery followed by chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin, and subsequently received PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy targeting the BRCA2 mutation. Sister Mary Joseph's nodule represents an ominous prognostic sign in fallopian tube cancer, with fewer than ten cases reported in the literature, and treatment typically involves multimodal therapy combining surgical debulking with platinum-based chemotherapy and targeted approaches.

title: Primary Carcinomas of the Episiotomy Scar Site: A Systematic Literature Review
summary: This systematic review documents a rare but clinically significant phenomenon: primary malignant tumors arising at episiotomy scar sites, with endometriosis-related histotypes comprising 77% of reported cases. Clear cell carcinoma and endometrioid carcinoma were the predominant tumor types, with evidence of direct malignant transformation from endometriosis occurring 3 to 27 years after initial diagnosis. Pregnancy appears to accelerate malignant transformation of endometriotic lesions in the perineal region, and patients typically present with perineal pain, dyspareunia, abnormal genital bleeding, or pruritus. Surgical resection forms the cornerstone of treatment, often supplemented by chemotherapy and radiotherapy, with specific regimens including paclitaxel, carboplatin, cisplatin, and mFOLFOX6 in various combinations. The findings underscore an unexpected link between obstetrical trauma, endometriosis, and oncologic outcomes, highlighting the need for heightened clinical surveillance in women with episiotomy scars and known endometriosis.

title: Dedifferentiated liposarcoma with extensive cystic change causing significant diagnostic challenges: report of two cases and review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

Dedifferentiated liposarcoma (DDLPS) is a rare, aggressive malignancy arising from well-differentiated liposarcoma, characterized by high rates of recurrence, metastatic potential, and dismal oncological outcomes. The disease is molecularly defined by amplifications of MDM2 and CDK4 genes located at chromosome 12q14-q15, which were confirmed in both reported cases through immunohistochemistry and molecular testing. This report presents two unusual cases of DDLPS with extensive cystic degeneration—a rare presentation typically associated with necrosis and hemosiderin deposition indicating hypoxic conditions within the tumors. Both cases expressed GLUT1, a hypoxia-inducible factor downstream target, supporting the role of hypoxic microenvironments in tumor pathology. Notably, the first case occurred in a patient with a history of endometriosis, while the second case demonstrated heterologous osteosarcomatous differentiation with SATB2 positivity and STAT6 amplification. These cases highlight the diagnostic challenges posed by atypical cystic configurations in DDLPS and underscore the importance of molecular confirmation through MDM2/CDK4 amplification analysis for accurate diagnosis.

title: A Bilateral Wolffian Adnexal Tumor with Malignant Behavior: A Rare Case Report with Literature Review
summary: This case report describes a rare bilateral female adnexal tumor of Wolffian origin (FATWO), a neoplasm arising from mesonephric duct remnants typically found in the broad ligament, mesosalpynx, fallopian tubes, ovaries, and omentum. Although FATWOs generally exhibit low malignant potential, this case exemplifies aggressive behavior with lymphovascular invasion, omental involvement, and evidence of tumor progression, which occurs in approximately 11% of cases within two years of diagnosis. The neoplastic cells expressed characteristic markers including calretinin, inhibin, WT1, and cytokeratins (CK7, CK19) while lacking PAX-8, EMA, and CD10, findings that help distinguish FATWO from morphologically similar endometrioid carcinomas and mesonephric carcinomas. Malignant behavior in FATWOs, observed in approximately one-fifth of cases, is associated with pathogenic mutations in STK11, APC, and MDB4 genes, along with KMT2D variants, and is indicated by histologic features including necrosis, capsular invasion, and high mitotic rates. The patient presented with abdominal pain and a mass, with imaging revealing concurrent deep infiltrating endometriosis, highlighting the clinical complexity of adnexal pathology. This case underscores the importance of recognizing FATWO's malignant potential and the need for careful long-term monitoring, as metastasis most commonly occurs to the liver and lung.

title: Ovarian endometrioid carcinoma with a sex cord-like pattern: a morphological, immunohistochemical, and molecular analysis
summary: Sex cord-like endometrioid carcinoma (SCLEC) is a morphologically heterogeneous ovarian malignancy characterized by sertoliform and granulosa-like architectural patterns that can mimic sex cord tumors, yet typically exhibits classic endometrioid features including squamous, mucinous, and ciliated differentiation. Immunohistochemically, SCLEC consistently expresses epithelial markers (cytokeratin-7 and EMA) and hormone receptors (estrogen and progesterone receptors), with frequent CD10 positivity that may raise diagnostic confusion with mesonephric-like carcinoma. Molecularly, SCLEC is predominantly characterized by CTNNB1 exon 3 mutations with resulting nuclear β-catenin accumulation, which may drive the altered sex cord-like morphology and is associated with eosinophilic cytoplasmic changes and nuclear enlargement. The majority of cases demonstrate a "no specific molecular profile" (NSMP) classification, with one case showing p53-abnormality, and despite their unusual architecture, these tumors behave as low-grade malignancies with excellent prognosis when confined to the ovary. This morphological, immunophenotypic, and molecular analysis establishes SCLEC as a distinct variant of ovarian endometrioid carcinoma requiring careful pathological distinction from sex cord tumors and other diagnostic mimics.

title: Rectal Endometriosis-Associated Adenocarcinoma: A Rare Entity Demanding Clinical Vigilance
summary: Endometriosis, though typically benign, possesses the capacity for malignant transformation into various histological subtypes including endometrioid adenocarcinoma and clear cell carcinoma, with rectal involvement representing a rare but clinically significant manifestation. While endometriosis most commonly affects the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and uterosacral ligaments, extra-pelvic sites like the rectum can develop endometriosis-associated adenocarcinoma, where benign endometriotic foci coexist contiguously with malignant tissue and enable bowel wall invasion. Previous pelvic surgical interventions such as hysterectomy, myomectomy, or uterine polypectomy contribute to rectal endometriosis-associated carcinoma development, potentially through facilitated dissemination of endometrial tissue. Endometriosis-associated adenocarcinomas express estrogen and progesterone receptors along with PAX8, while characteristically lacking CDX2 and SATB2 expression, and may harbor mutations in KRAS, PIK3CA, and PTEN with high microsatellite instability. Accurate diagnosis requires immunohistochemical staining to distinguish the tumor's true origin, as morphological assessment alone can be misleading. Treatment typically combines cytoreductive surgery with adjuvant chemotherapy regimens such as paclitaxel and carboplatin to optimize outcomes in this rare malignancy.

title: Establishment of a Novel In Vitro and In Vivo Model to Understand Molecular Carcinogenesis of Endometriosis-Related Ovarian Neoplasms
summary: This study establishes both in vitro and in vivo models to elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying the transformation of endometriosis into ovarian clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas. The research demonstrates that ARID1A mutation is a critical initiating event in endometriosis-related ovarian neoplasm development, with ARID1A loss combined with overexpression of KRAS (or AKT activation) and c-Myc being necessary and sufficient for malignant transformation in immortalized endometriotic epithelial cells. These triple-mutant cells exhibit significantly enhanced proliferation, invasion, migration, and colony formation in vitro, and consistently generate tumors in xenograft models. The resulting tumors activate both RAS/ERK and PIK3CA/AKT signaling pathways and express markers consistent with either clear cell carcinoma (HNF-1β expression) or endometrioid carcinoma (estrogen receptor 1 expression). Notably, the tumor immune microenvironment influences histological phenotype, as B-cell signaling contributes to the histological diversity observed between clear cell and endometrioid subtypes, suggesting that both genetic alterations and immune contexture shape endometriosis-associated malignant transformation.

title: Isolated abdominal wall metastasis 42 years after curative surgery for ovarian cancer: A case report
summary: This case report documents an exceptional instance of isolated abdominal wall metastasis from low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma presenting 42 years after the patient's initial curative surgery—a remarkably late recurrence that underscores the importance of long-term surveillance in ovarian cancer patients. The metastatic tumor, confirmed through immunohistochemistry showing positivity for CK7, PAX8, estrogen receptor, and wild-type p53, presented as a 10-centimeter mass within the rectus abdominis muscle. While laparoscopic approaches significantly increase the risk of port-site metastases compared to open surgery, this case arose following laparotomy, suggesting that meticulous surgical technique during both laparoscopic and open procedures remains critical for prevention. The standard treatment approach employed surgical excision combined with synthetic mesh reconstruction, supported by adjuvant chemotherapy, which represents the conventional management for localized metastatic disease. This case highlights that despite the rarity of abdominal wall metastases in ovarian cancer, clinicians must maintain clinical vigilance even decades after primary treatment, as late recurrences can occur and are amenable to aggressive surgical intervention. The extended disease-free interval before recurrence emphasizes the indolent nature of low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma and the necessity for indefinite follow-up in this patient population.

title: A Case of Postmenopausal Uterine Adenosarcoma With Concomitant Diabetes Mellitus
summary: Uterine adenosarcoma is a rare mixed epithelial-mesenchymal tumor of the endometrium, typically comprising benign epithelial components with a low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma component, and occurs predominantly in postmenopausal women with a median age of 54 years. Multiple risk factors contribute to its development, including tamoxifen use, prolonged estrogen replacement therapy, pelvic radiation exposure, obesity, and type 2 diabetes mellitus, with metabolic dysregulation playing a critical role through abnormal estrogen metabolism, chronic inflammation, and enhanced glucose uptake. The disease presents clinically with abnormal vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, and palpable masses, with characteristic pathological findings including periglandular cuffing and immunohistochemical positivity for estrogen and progesterone receptors, smooth muscle markers, and CD10. Prognosis depends on myometrial invasion depth, sarcomatous overgrowth, lymphovascular invasion, and tumor size, with myometrial invasion associated with significantly higher recurrence rates (36% versus 7%) and increased risk of lymph node metastasis and distant spread to lungs and liver. Primary treatment consists of hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, though combination chemotherapy regimens including doxorubicin, ifosfamide, gemcitabine, and docetaxel, along with hormonal therapy for receptor-positive cases, show potential benefit. Accurate diagnosis through hysteroscopic tumor excision is essential, particularly in postmenopausal diabetic women presenting with persistent abnormal bleeding and polypoid uterine lesions.

title: Case Report: Primary solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the ovary with “cholesteroma-like” denaturation
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare primary solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (SPN) of the ovary with distinctive "cholesteroma-like" degeneration, expanding recognition of ovarian SPNs beyond their typical pancreatic origin. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed the characteristic SPN immunoprofile, with neoplastic cells expressing β-catenin (nuclear), CD99 (dot-like paranuclear pattern), CD56, vimentin, CD10, synaptophysin, and cytokeratin, while demonstrating absence of E-cadherin, TFE3, CD34, inhibin, and progesterone receptors. The pathogenesis of SPNs is driven by CTNNB1 gene mutations (predominantly exon 3 mutations including S37Y and p.S33C variants) that impede ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of β-catenin, leading to abnormal accumulation and aberrant activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway. The ovarian origin is explained by embryological proximity between the ovarian ridge and pancreas, with possible derivation from ectopic pancreatic tissue. The patient presented with elevated CA125 levels (43.54 U/mL), and nuclear β-catenin expression serves as a distinctive diagnostic marker for SPNs, effectively differentiating them from morphologically similar tumors including sex cord-stromal tumors, yolk sac tumors, and papillary thyroid carcinoma.

title: Disseminated Peritoneal Leiomyomatosis: Two Rare Cases With Diagnostic Mimics and a Novel Central Nervous System Disease Association
summary:
# Summary

Disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis (DPL) is a rare benign smooth muscle tumor arising on the peritoneal surface, predominantly affecting premenopausal women with a history of prior abdominal surgery, particularly myomectomy or laparoscopic morcellation. The pathogenesis involves prolonged hormonal exposure stimulating peritoneal mesenchymal stem cell metaplasia and smooth muscle cell differentiation, supported by the presence of estrogen and progesterone receptors in lesional tissue, as well as possible iatrogenic implantation of smooth muscle cells during surgical procedures. DPL frequently coexists with Müllerian lesions including endometriosis and endosalpingiosis, and this report notably describes the first association between DPL and central nervous system malignancies (medulloblastoma and recurrent meningiomas), suggesting possible shared genetic predisposition syndromes such as Gorlin syndrome. Diagnostic challenges arise due to morphologic mimicry with mesothelioma, gastrointestinal stromal tumors, and pseudomyxoma peritonei, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemical markers including positive desmin, smooth muscle actin, and h-caldesmon, and negative PAX8, WT1, and calretinin. Treatment approaches include surgical excision combined with hormonal therapy (leuprolide, letrozole) and, in select cases, intraperitoneal chemotherapy, though optimal management protocols remain undefined for this rare entity.

title: Two case reports of ovarian clear cell carcinoma: Atypical MRI findings with a renal corticomedullary contrast-like appearance
summary: Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (CCC) is a histological subtype of epithelial ovarian cancer with distinct clinical features and a strong association with background ovarian endometriosis in 20-50% of cases. These tumors are characterized histopathologically by cuboidal, hobnail, and flattened cells lining cysts and glands, with cancer cell proliferation in the peripheral area accompanied by stromal fibrosis and edematous stroma in the central region. CCC demonstrates relative insensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy compared to serous or endometrioid carcinomas, though treatment typically involves paclitaxel and carboplatin combination therapy. An atypical MRI finding in these cases includes a renal corticomedullary-like contrast appearance, with hyperintense cystic components on T1-weighted imaging being more frequent in CCC than in high-grade serous carcinomas, and apparent diffusion coefficient values correlating with cancer cell proliferation patterns. Endometriosis-derived CCCs typically present as unilocular cystic lesions with polypoid mural nodules and tend to exhibit papillary growth patterns, while adenofibroma-derived CCCs often demonstrate tubulocystic patterns. CCC comprises approximately 10% of epithelial ovarian cancers in Western countries but accounts for a higher proportion in Japan, with patients often presenting in their sixth decade with atypical genital bleeding, lower abdominal pain, and elevated serum CA-125 levels.

title: Primary Peritoneal Clear Cell Carcinoma Presenting with Nonspecific Gastrointestinal Symptoms in a 39-Year-Old Woman: A Case Report
summary: Primary peritoneal clear cell carcinoma (PPCCC) originates from the Müllerian system and arises in the abdominal cavity, with endometriosis and polypoid endometriosis serving as significant risk factors for malignant transformation in susceptible individuals, including those with BRCA-1 mutations. This malignancy typically presents with nonspecific gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal bloating, constipation, early satiety, nausea, and vomiting, often accompanied by normocytic anemia and elevated tumor markers CA-125 and CA 19-9. Diagnosis is confirmed through immunohistochemical staining showing positivity for PAX-8, ER, P53, P16, and Napsin A, with negativity for PR and WT-1, and molecular testing may reveal PDL-1 expression relevant to immunotherapy. Current treatment combines surgical debulking followed by platinum-based chemotherapy with paclitaxel/carboplatin and bevacizumab (an anti-vascular endothelial growth factor agent), with radiation therapy used for inoperable or recurrent disease and emerging evidence supporting immunotherapy targeting PD-1 pathways. The presence of residual primary tumor following surgery is associated with increased mortality, emphasizing the importance of complete cytoreduction in improving patient outcomes.

title: Primary Low-Grade Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma of the Ovary Arising From Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LGESS) arising from ovarian endometriosis in a 52-year-old woman, highlighting endometriosis as a significant risk factor for malignant transformation. The tumor exhibited characteristic immunohistochemical features including positivity for estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PgR), and CD10, along with a JAZF1-SUZ12 genetic rearrangement typical of LGESS. The underlying mechanism of malignant transformation involves chronic inflammation and excessive estrogen stimulation within endometriotic tissue, creating a pro-tumorigenic microenvironment. Treatment combined optimal surgical debulking with complete resection of disseminated lesions and postoperative hormonal therapy using medroxyprogesterone acetate, with alternative endocrine-dependent approaches including aromatase inhibitors and GnRH agonists. Prognostic factors included tumor size, dissemination, and vascular invasion, though the absence of lymphatic invasion in this case was notable. The authors emphasize that long-term follow-up and close monitoring of ovarian endometriosis patients is essential given the malignant potential, and that combination surgery with hormonal therapy is critical for achieving long-term remission.

title: Synchronous Endometrial and Ovarian Adenocarcinomas in a 43-Year-Old Patient Following Infertility Treatment: A Case Report
summary: This case report documents synchronous endometrial and ovarian adenocarcinomas in a 43-year-old woman with a history of infertility and infertility treatment, highlighting the malignant transformation risk associated with reproductive disorders and interventions. Multiple metabolic and reproductive factors—including polycystic ovary syndrome, anovulatory cycles, obesity, and insulin resistance—contribute to endometrial cancer development, while infertility itself and assisted reproductive treatments are independently associated with increased cancer risk in both endometrial and ovarian tissues. Doppler ultrasound parameters, particularly reduced resistance index and pulsatility index, along with elevated biomarkers CA 125 and HE4, proved valuable for cancer detection. The tumors exhibited characteristic endometrioid adenocarcinoma immunophenotypes (cytokeratin 7 positive, estrogen receptor positive) and were likely independent primary tumors arising from ovarian endometriosis rather than metastatic disease. While progestin-based therapies effectively treat endometrial hyperplasia through progesterone receptor interaction (particularly levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices), comprehensive endometrial evaluation using fractional curettage rather than limited sampling is critical for detecting precancerous lesions that carry higher cancer progression risk.

title: The Coincidence of Ovarian Endometrioma with Paratubal Leydig Cell Nodules: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Article Summary

This case report documents the rare coincidental presentation of ovarian endometrioma with paratubal Leydig cell nodules in a single patient. Leydig cell nodules are testosterone-producing clusters normally distributed throughout the ovary, mesovarium, and mesosalpinx that frequently associate with various gynecological abnormalities including endometriosis, salpingitis, and salpingitis isthmica nodosa. These tumors characteristically manifest endocrine manifestations of hyperandrogenism such as hirsutism and voice thickening, with malignant Leydig cell tumors expressing diagnostic markers including inhibin, calretinin, androgen receptors, and multiple estrogen receptor subtypes (ERα, ERβ1, and ERβ2), while lacking estrogen receptor expression in their non-neoplastic counterparts. The endometriotic component showed characteristic estrogen receptor and CD10-positive endometrial stromal cells, consistent with standard endometriosis histology. Leydig cell development may be stimulated by elevated gonadotropin levels during pregnancy or hormonal changes in postmenopausal women, potentially arising from mesonephric duct cell migration during embryogenesis or differentiation from undifferentiated stromal and fibroblast populations. Imaging features of Leydig cell tumors include hypoechoic appearance with marked hypervascularization on ultrasound, with 18F-FDG PET/CT useful for localization when conventional imaging proves inconclusive.

title: Obstructive Jaundice Induced by Hilar Mucinous Cystic Neoplasm of the Liver: A Rare Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the liver (MCN-L) is a rare benign tumor primarily affecting middle-aged women that originates in the intrahepatic biliary system and carries a 3–5% risk of malignant transformation to cystadenocarcinoma. When located in the hilar region, MCN-L frequently causes bile duct obstruction and compression, leading to obstructive jaundice characterized by elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST, GGT, ALP) and bilirubin levels. The tumor exhibits distinctive histopathological features including mucinous columnar epithelial lining underlain by ovarian-like mesenchymal stroma expressing hormone receptors (ER, PR) and cytokeratin markers (CK, CK7, CK19), with activated Hedgehog and Wnt signaling pathways in the mesenchyme. Diagnostic evaluation involves imaging modalities, serum and cyst fluid CA19-9 and CEA elevation, and pathological examination, though cyst aspiration carries risks of tumor cell dissemination and biliary leakage. Radical surgical resection, particularly hepatectomy with complete cyst wall removal, is the definitive treatment that reduces malignancy and recurrence risks and provides favorable long-term prognosis. For patients unsuitable for surgery, endoscopic ultrasound-guided biliary drainage (EUS-BD) or percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) can provide temporary symptom relief, though close postoperative surveillance remains essential for early detection of recurrence.

title: Cytologic Histotyping of Gynecologic Malignancies in Peritoneal Fluids Is Reliable When Compared to Its Corresponding Surgical Specimen
summary:
# Summary

This study validates the reliability of cytologic subtyping of gynecologic malignancies in peritoneal fluid specimens compared to surgical histology, demonstrating 91.2% concordance overall with near-perfect agreement. Gynecologic malignancies account for 55.8% of malignant peritoneal fluid cases, with epithelial ovarian carcinomas showing the highest concordance rate (87.1%), while high-grade serous carcinoma was correctly subtyped in 91.1% of cases using morphologic features including complex branching, pleomorphism, and brisk mitotic activity. Immunohistochemical markers—particularly WT-1, p53, p16, HNF-1beta, ARID1a, and progesterone receptors—significantly improved diagnostic accuracy, with concordance enhanced by adequate specimen volume and immunostain usage. Clinical treatment decisions depend on accurate subtyping, as high-grade serous carcinomas receive neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by cytoreductive surgery and PARP inhibitor therapy for BRCA1/2-positive patients, while low-grade serous carcinomas proceed directly to primary surgical resection. Diagnostic challenges arise from low cellularity, low specimen volume, heterogeneous marker expression, and fragmented samples, emphasizing the importance of cytologic evaluation as an initial diagnostic tool before surgical intervention or chemotherapy initiation.

title: A Rare Case of Endometrial Adenosarcoma and Its Management: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Endometrial adenosarcoma is a rare uterine malignancy composed of epithelial and stromal components that predominantly affects postmenopausal women and presents with abnormal vaginal bleeding in approximately 65% of cases. Risk factors include prior pelvic radiation, prolonged estrogen exposure, and tamoxifen use, while adenosarcomas show genetic heterogeneity involving mutations in MDM2/CDK4, PI3K/AKT/PTEN, Wnt/β-catenin, ATRX, FGFR2, KMT2C, TP53, TERT, and MYLB1 pathways. The stromal component characteristically expresses estrogen and progesterone receptors, along with CD10 and mesenchymal markers (CD34, desmin, smooth muscle actin), while showing weak p53 expression and EGFR negativity. Prognosis depends on tumor stage, myometrial invasion depth, presence of sarcomatous overgrowth, and lymph node involvement, as these tumors have significant potential for recurrence and metastasis despite typically indolent behavior. Treatment involves surgical intervention with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy using regimens such as paclitaxel-carboplatin or doxorubicin-based combinations, while emerging hormonal therapies with aromatase inhibitors and selective estrogen receptor modulators are being explored. MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging and hysteroscopy are critical diagnostic tools for early detection, with PET and CT scans used to identify metastases and recurrence.

title: Triple synchronous primary cancers comprising large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lower uterine segment and endometrioid carcinomas of the uterine corpus and the right ovary-a rare combination: A case report
summary: This case report documents a rare presentation of triple synchronous primary malignancies: large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC) of the lower uterine segment combined with endometrioid carcinomas of the uterine corpus and right ovary. Endometriosis and atypical endometrial hyperplasia are recognized precursor lesions, with borderline endometrioid tumors and endometriotic cysts specifically associated with the ovarian malignancy. The LCNEC demonstrated characteristic neuroendocrine differentiation confirmed by immunohistochemical positivity for chromogranin, synaptophysin, and CD56, while elevated CA-125 levels (296.2 U/mL) supported the malignant diagnosis. Molecular profiling revealed mutations in PIK3CA and PTEN genes within the neuroendocrine component, with evidence that endometrial NECs may originate from conventional endometrial neoplasia including endometrioid adenocarcinomas. Treatment involved surgical resection followed by chemotherapy with irinotecan and cisplatin, the current standard approach for advanced neuroendocrine carcinomas; emerging options include immune checkpoint inhibition for mismatch repair-deficient tumors and molecular-targeted therapies. While early-stage synchronous endometrioid cancers typically carry favorable prognosis, endometrial neuroendocrine carcinomas are highly aggressive with poor overall survival rates of 12-22 months.

title: Papillary and ductal patterns of mesonephric‐like adenocarcinomas are often overlooked: a retrospective revaluation of over 1000 endometrial carcinomas
summary:
# Summary

Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) is a rare endometrial malignancy often misdiagnosed as low-grade endometrioid carcinoma that arises from Müllerian tissue and is associated with endometriosis. This study screened over 1000 endometrial carcinomas using immunohistochemistry and identified 16 potential MLA cases characterized by GATA3 and/or TTF1 positivity combined with absent or focal estrogen receptor expression; dual GATA3/TTF1 staining was highly specific for MLA. MLAs exhibit diverse and mixed morphological patterns including papillary, ductal, cribriform, and labyrinthine architectures, with four of five molecularly profiled cases harboring pathogenic KRAS mutations, which present opportunities for targeted therapy with agents such as Sotorasib, lenvatinib, and pembrolizumab. Clinically, MLAs demonstrate aggressive behavior with 80% recurrence rates, predominantly metastasizing to the lung, and present at advanced FIGO stages despite their low-to-moderate cytologic atypia, conferring poor prognosis compared to standard low-grade endometrioid carcinomas in the "no specific molecular profile" category.

title: Cellular origins of mucinous ovarian carcinoma
summary: Mucinous ovarian carcinoma (MOC) is a rare epithelial ovarian cancer comprising 3-4% of cases with distinct cellular origins and pathological characteristics. MOC can arise from primary ovarian sources through mucinous metaplasia of Müllerian tumors or Brenner tumors, but many ovarian mucinous tumors are actually gastrointestinal metastases from the colon, appendix, pancreas, or stomach, which can be distinguished using CK7 and SATB2 markers with 95% accuracy. Molecularly, MOC harbors frequent co-occurring KRAS and TP53 mutations (35-54%), as well as CDKN2A deletions and ERBB2 amplifications that are absent in pancreatic adenocarcinomas, making MOC genetically distinct from other mucinous malignancies. ERBB2 amplifications are associated with an expansile growth pattern and better clinical outcomes, and MOC shows similarity to gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinomas histologically. MOC demonstrates intrinsic resistance to standard ovarian cancer chemotherapy, exhibits low or absent estrogen and progesterone receptor expression, and is associated with environmental risk factors including smoking and increased body mass index, while most patients present with early-stage disease with excellent prognosis for borderline tumors.

title: Three Separate Cases of Polypoid Endometriosis Coincided with Abscess, Angioleiomyoma or Stromal Sarcoma
summary:
# Summary

This case series describes three rare presentations of polypoid endometriosis, a variant characterized by polypoidal growth of ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, each coinciding with distinct pathological complications. The first case involved cervical polypoid endometriosis with multiple abscesses presenting with dysmenorrhea and cyclic menstrual fever, elevated CA-125 levels, and imaging features including the black rim sign on MRI; hysterectomy confirmed the diagnosis after conservative treatment with ZOLADEX failed. The second case presented polypoid endometriosis coexisting with a rare uterine angioleiomyoma, a vascular smooth muscle tumor, characterized by rich vascularity and requiring immunohistochemical differentiation through positive SMA, desmin, and CD10 staining. The third case demonstrated aggressive endometrial stromal sarcoma arising from widespread endometrial stromal nodules disseminated throughout the bilateral ovaries, fallopian tubes, mesentery, and intestine, with a Ki-67 index of 30% indicating low-grade malignant potential. These cases highlight that polypoid endometriosis can associate with serious complications including infectious, benign vascular, and malignant neoplastic processes, requiring immunohistochemical staining for accurate differential diagnosis and aggressive surgical management. MRI imaging characteristics such as enhancement patterns can help distinguish benign from malignant nodules and guide appropriate clinical treatment decisions.

title: Synchronous triple squamous cell carcinomas of the stomach, skin and gingiva with liver, lung, spleen, kidney, bone and brain metastases: A case report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of synchronous triple squamous cell carcinomas affecting the stomach, skin, and gingiva with extensive metastatic disease to multiple organs including the liver, lungs, brain, and bone. Multiple risk factors contributed to tumor development, including heavy smoking (~60 cigarettes/day), chronic alcohol consumption, and betel nut chewing, which are well-established carcinogenic exposures for squamous cell carcinomas across these anatomical sites. Diagnosis was supported by elevated biomarkers (cytokeratin 19 fragment, neuron-specific enolase, and SCC antigen) and characteristic immunohistochemical markers (panCK, P63, P40, and P53 overexpression), which confirmed squamous cell differentiation across all three tumors. The patient was treated with platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin and carboplatin) combined with anti-PD-1 antibodies (camrelizumab) and taxane-based chemotherapy (nab-paclitaxel and docetaxel), reflecting emerging immunotherapy approaches for advanced squamous cell carcinomas. PD-L1 expression was identified as an important prognostic biomarker predictive of therapeutic response to anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibodies. This extraordinarily rare case highlights the aggressive behavior of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma and the potential benefit of multimodal treatment combining immunotherapy with conventional chemotherapy regimens.

title: Laparoscopic treatment of a rare primary peritoneal serous borderline tumor: Case report and narrative review of the literature
summary: Primary peritoneal serous borderline tumors (PPSBT) are rare epithelial proliferations representing less than 1% of all serous tumors, characterized by low malignant potential and a propensity for peritoneal dissemination that often leads to misdiagnosis as primary ovarian carcinomas. These tumors predominantly affect women of reproductive age and are frequently asymptomatic or present with nonspecific symptoms such as abdominal and pelvic pain, infertility, and amenorrhea, with approximately one-third of cases discovered incidentally during surgeries for other conditions including endometriosis. Pathogenesis is theorized to involve endosalpingiosis, a condition characterized by ectopic benign tubal-type epithelium, along with suspected associations involving nulliparity, infertility history, hormonal factors, and possibly BRCA mutations. Histopathologically, PPSBT presents distinctive features including psammoma bodies and immunohistochemical expression of epithelial markers (cytokeratin AE1/AE3, cytokeratin 7, WT1, CA125, Ber-EP4, pancytokeratin) and hormone receptors (estrogen and progesterone), with negative calretinin and D2.40 staining. While laparotomy has historically been the standard surgical approach, minimally invasive laparoscopic excision combined with comprehensive surgical staging including hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, omentectomy, and peritoneal biopsies effectively treats these tumors. Patients with PPSBT demonstrate favorable prognosis with high survival rates, low recurrence rates, and excellent outcomes even with conservative, fertility-sparing surgical approaches.

title: A Rare Case of Non-Hodgkin B-Cell Lymphoma Following Invasive Lobular Carcinoma of the Breast: A Case Report
summary: This case report documents a rare occurrence of follicular B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma developing in the spleen of a patient with a history of infiltrating lobular carcinoma of the breast, representing only the third such documented case. The patient's initial breast tumor was hormone receptor-positive (ER 65%, PR 80%) and HER2-negative, treated with endocrine therapy including a GnRH agonist (Decapeptyl) and aromatase inhibitor (exemestane), along with the CDK4/6 inhibitor Palbociclib. Following recurrence, the tumor demonstrated markedly reduced hormone receptor expression (ER <1%, PR absent), requiring chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel, followed by capecitabine. The secondary splenic lymphoma was characterized by CD20, CD79a, CD10, Bcl-2, and Bcl-6 positivity with elevated Ki-67 proliferation index (30–35%), consistent with follicular lymphoma. The case highlights that prolonged endocrine therapy and prior cancer treatment represent significant risk factors for secondary hematologic malignancies, though the precise etiology—whether treatment-induced or coincidental secondary malignancy—remains uncertain in this exceptionally rare presentation.

title: Postoperative diagnosis of uterine florid cystic endosalpingiosis: a case report emphasizing diagnostic challenges and multimodal imaging correlation
summary: Endosalpingiosis is a rare benign condition characterized by ectopic fallopian tube-like epithelium that frequently coexists with endometriosis and commonly follows pelvic surgery, particularly salpingostomy for ectopic pregnancy. Patients typically present with chronic pelvic pain, and diagnosis is complicated by imaging mimicry of other cystic uterine pathologies, though multimodal imaging—including ultrasound showing thin-walled anechoic cysts, contrast-enhanced ultrasound demonstrating enhancement patterns, and MRI revealing T2-weighted hyperintensity with T1-weighted hypointensity—can help differentiate endosalpingiosis from conditions like cystic adenomyosis and uterine fibroids. Histopathologically, endosalpingiosis is confirmed by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with immunohistochemical markers including PAX-8, WT-1, estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and CK7 positivity, while CK20 and CDX2 are absent. A notable finding is the strong hormone receptor expression (approximately 95% for both estrogen and progesterone receptors with low proliferation), suggesting potential hormone dependency similar to endometriosis and opening the possibility of fertility-sparing hormonal therapy as an alternative to traditional surgical management with hysterectomy or salpingo-oophorectomy. Current clinical practice relies primarily on surgical intervention, but the documented hormone responsiveness warrants investigation of hormonal therapeutic approaches to preserve reproductive capacity.

title: Leiomyoma with Bizarre Nuclei and Hereditary Leiomyomatosis and Renal Cell Carcinoma
journal: CRSLS : MIS Case Reports from SLS
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 22-year-old woman with leiomyoma with bizarre nuclei (LBN) caused by a pathogenic fumarate hydratase (FH) gene variant, linking her presentation to hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell carcinoma (HLRCC) syndrome. Loss of FH expression in the tumor leads to accumulation of fumarate and elevated S-(2-succino)-cysteine, which promotes tumor cell proliferation and apoptosis resistance while compromising genomic integrity. FH deficiency is significantly enriched in LBN (37.3%) compared to typical leiomyomas (1-2%), and FH-deficient leiomyomas are strongly associated with HLRCC, an autosomal dominant condition that also manifests as cutaneous leiomyomata, symptomatic uterine fibroids at early age, and aggressive type 2 papillary renal cell carcinomas. The patient's discovery of LBN should prompt genetic counseling and long-term renal screening, as FH-mutated individuals face significantly elevated cancer risk. This case underscores that LBN in young women may serve as a sentinel presentation of occult HLRCC syndrome requiring comprehensive clinical surveillance.

title: Beautiful and regular vascularization in a borderline ovarian seromucinous tumor detected by microvascular imaging
summary: Seromucinous borderline tumors (SMBTs) of the ovary are endometriosis-related neoplasms, with endometriosis identified in 30-70% of cases and ectopic endometrial foci confirmed histologically. This case report demonstrates that three-dimensional microvascular imaging effectively visualizes the characteristic regular vascularization patterns within papillary projections of SMBTs, improving diagnostic differentiation of these tumors. The tumor expressed typical immunohistochemical markers including estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, PAX-8, P16, MUC6, and CA-125, while lacking WT-1 and Villin expression, with a low Ki-67 proliferation index (5%) consistent with borderline pathology. Elevated serum tumor markers (CA-125 at 339.8 U/ml and CA-199 >2000 U/ml) accompanied the imaging findings. The combination of advanced ultrasound microvascular imaging with immunohistochemical profiling provides enhanced diagnostic capability for identifying endometriosis-associated SMBT, which may have clinical implications for early detection and management of this rare ovarian malignancy.

title: Exploring the Genetic and Clinical Landscape of Dedifferentiated Endometrioid Carcinoma
summary: Dedifferentiated endometrioid carcinoma (DDEC) is a rare malignancy with poor prognosis characterized by frequent genomic alterations and loss of epithelial differentiation. The disease exhibits high rates of mismatch repair deficiency (66.7% of cases), p53 mutations, and additional mutations in PIK3CA, CTNNB1, PTEN, and ARID1A, with p53 and mismatch repair deficiency playing central roles in the dedifferentiation process. DDEC presents with aggressive clinical features including advanced-stage disease, increased muscle invasion, and lymphovascular space invasion, with p53 mutations and loss of the SWI/SNF complex associated with particularly poor outcomes. Molecularly, the undifferentiated component loses epithelial markers (PAX8, cytokeratin, E-cadherin) present in well-differentiated regions, while E-cadherin-negative tumors paradoxically demonstrate better prognosis. Current standard treatment relies on platinum/taxane-based chemotherapy, but emerging evidence supports immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors like pembrolizumab in mismatch repair-deficient cases, alongside PARP inhibitors for homologous recombination-deficient tumors and investigational approaches targeting the p53 pathway and ARID1A deficiency.

title: New Evidence About Malignant Transformation of Endometriosis—A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis carries a 0.5–1% risk of malignant transformation into endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers, with clear cell carcinoma and endometrioid carcinoma being the most common subtypes. Multiple genetic and epigenetic alterations drive this transformation, including ARID1A mutations that activate the PI3K/Akt and mTOR pathways, along with microsatellite instability, RUNX3 and RASSF2 hypermethylation, and loss of mismatch repair proteins. Clinical risk factors include advanced age (40–60 years), large ovarian endometriomas (>9 cm), postmenopausal status, and prolonged estrogen exposure, while atypical endometriosis represents a premalignant intermediate lesion with elevated p53 expression and intermediate Ki-67 proliferation indices. Iron accumulation within endometriotic cysts drives oxidative stress that may promote malignant transformation, and dysregulation of specific microRNAs, particularly miR-125b-1-3p and BANCR, correlates with progression to cancer and chemotherapy resistance. Together, these molecular and clinical features identify endometriosis as a disease requiring surveillance, particularly in older women with large lesions and evidence of atypical changes.

title: Hormonal therapy in low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma; what is the current standard of care?
summary: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LG-ESS) is an indolent malignancy characterized by a generally favorable five-year survival exceeding 90%, though late recurrences occur in up to 60% of patients, particularly in cases with extrauterine disease associated with endometriosis. Surgical resection via total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy remains the primary treatment standard and is associated with significantly improved recurrence-free and overall survival. The tumors typically express estrogen and progesterone receptors, along with CD10, WT-1, and vimentin on immunohistochemistry, and frequently harbor JAZF1/SUZ12 gene fusion; negative estrogen receptor, smooth muscle actin, or desmin status predicts worse recurrence rates and overall survival. Adjuvant hormonal therapy with aromatase inhibitors or megestrol acetate substantially improves outcomes compared to observation alone, with megestrol acetate demonstrating superior recurrence-free survival (median 13.48 years) compared to letrozole (6.06 years), and both agents remaining effective at first recurrence. Notably, chemotherapy use at recurrence is associated with poorer outcomes, whereas selective estrogen receptor modulators such as tamoxifen are ineffective and associated with shorter survival, underscoring hormonal therapy as the standard of care for both adjuvant treatment and recurrent disease management.

title: High grade endometrioid carcinoma arising from deep infiltrating endometriosis extending deeply into the pelvis: a case report
summary: This case report describes a rare instance of malignant transformation in deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), where high-grade endometrioid adenocarcinoma developed from DIE extending into the rectovaginal septum and invaded multiple pelvic organs including the bladder, rectum, and ureters. While malignant transformation of endometriosis occurs in approximately 0.7-1.0% of endometriosis patients, such extensive invasion originating from DIE is exceptionally uncommon. The proposed mechanism involves oxidative stress from excessive hemoglobin, heme, and iron accumulation in endometriotic lesions, which may dysregulate genes including TP53, KRAS, PTEN, PIK3CA, and ARID1A. The patient presented with elevated tumor markers (carcinoembryonic antigen and CA125) and imaging revealed left hydroureter and hydronephrosis secondary to ureteral invasion. Treatment with concurrent chemoradiation therapy using cisplatin and 5-fluorouracil achieved complete response, demonstrating that chemoradiation rather than surgical resection may be the preferred approach for locally advanced DIE-originating carcinomas.

title: Primary Atrial Leiomyosarcoma in a Patient with a Prior History of Uterine Leiomyoma and Ovarian Tumour
summary: This case report describes a 39-year-old female with a history of endometriosis, uterine leiomyoma, and borderline ovarian tumor who presented with progressive dyspnea and chest pressure due to a large left atrial leiomyosarcoma. Primary cardiac leiomyosarcomas are exceptionally rare malignancies arising from smooth muscle cells, characteristically causing mass effect symptoms including dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, and syncope. Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed the diagnosis through positive smooth muscle markers (smooth muscle actin and desmin) and negative neural, endothelial, epithelial, and hormone receptor markers, excluding metastatic disease. Surgical resection remains the cornerstone of treatment and improves long-term survival, though the patient's tumor exhibited high-risk features including high mitotic activity and necrosis. Prognosis is influenced by tumor size, location, completeness of resection, and presence of metastasis, with adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation therapy considered for recurrence prevention given the tumor's high propensity to recur. This case highlights the association between prior benign smooth muscle tumors and development of rare cardiac malignancies, particularly in patients with endometriosis.

title: A locally aggressive pelvic MEIS1::NCOA1 fusion sarcoma in a young adult female: a case report and review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a MEIS1::NCOA1 fusion-positive low-grade spindle cell sarcoma discovered in the pelvis of a 19-year-old female, a tumor type characterized by monomorphic spindle cells arranged in short fascicles with an infiltrative growth pattern and variable immunophenotype including estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and CD10 expression. Notably, this case presented with unusually strong and diffuse estrogen/progesterone receptor positivity, suggesting a gynecologic origin and indicating hormonal sensitivity of the tumor. MEIS1::NCOA1/2 fusion sarcomas are typically associated with low malignant potential but frequently experience local recurrences, with rare instances of lung metastasis occurring primarily when accompanied by additional genetic alterations such as MDM2/CDK4 amplifications or CTNNB1 mutations. Management includes surgical approaches such as total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy combined with endocrine therapy targeting the tumor's hormone sensitivity. The case underscores the importance of molecular testing via next-generation sequencing to identify these fusion-defined sarcomas, as such diagnosis guides both prognostic assessment and therapeutic options focused on reducing local recurrence risk.

title: Molecular Interplay Between PTEN, ARID1A, PD-L1, and MMR in Asian Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma: Implications for Immunotherapy Response and Patient Stratification
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates the molecular landscape of Asian ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) by examining alterations in PTEN, ARID1A, PD-L1, and mismatch repair (MMR) proteins in 69 cases. PTEN loss occurred in 78.3% of cases and emerged as a key driver, activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway to promote chemotherapy resistance and upregulate PD-L1 expression, ultimately correlating with worse progression-free survival in early-stage disease. ARID1A mutations were present in 48.8% of cases and impaired DNA damage repair, with concurrent PTEN and ARID1A loss found in approximately 46.5% of early-stage tumors, indicating these represent fundamental early pathogenic events. The study revealed that 100% of PD-L1-positive tumors demonstrated PTEN loss, and MMR deficiency (10.1% of cases) was strongly associated with both ARID1A loss and elevated tumor mutational burden, suggesting potential sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitors. These molecular alterations provide a rational basis for stratifying patients and implementing combination therapies targeting the PI3K/AKT pathway alongside immune checkpoint blockade in recurrent OCCC, particularly in Asian populations where PTEN loss rates substantially exceed previously reported frequencies.

title: Colonic endometriosis: from subtotal bowel obstruction to malignant transformation - a case series and literature review
summary: This case series examines colonic endometriosis with emphasis on its rare but serious complication of malignant transformation. The authors present two cases of deeply infiltrating endometriosis in the sigmoid colon, with the first causing subtotal bowel obstruction presenting with positive fecal occult blood tests and elevated calprotectin, and the second progressing to endometrioid adenocarcinoma (EAC) discovered during endoscopic evaluation for occult gastrointestinal bleeding. Colonic endometriosis typically produces nonspecific symptoms including dyschezia, abdominal pain, and hematochezia, and in severe cases can cause acute complications such as intussusception, perforation, or bowel obstruction. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates that endometrial glands within colonic lesions express PAX8 and CD10 but lack CK20, contrasting with native colorectal mucosa, allowing differentiation from primary colorectal carcinoma. Both benign deeply infiltrating endometriosis and its malignant transformation show excellent prognosis when treated with complete surgical resection (R0), with early-stage EAC achieving greater than 90% five-year survival rates and adjuvant hormonal therapy reducing recurrence risk. The authors emphasize that while GI endometriosis remains rare and typically benign, vigilant clinical monitoring and histopathological examination are essential to detect the potential for malignant transformation.

title: A Case Report to Reflect on the Origins of MMRd Mesonephric-like Ovarian Adenocarcinoma: Can It Be Defined as a Mϋllerian Neoplasm?
summary:
# Summary

Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) is a rare ovarian neoplasm whose embryological origin remains debated, with evidence suggesting derivation either from Wolffian remnants in the ovarian hilum or from Müllerian epithelium undergoing mesonephric trans-differentiation. This case report documents an MLA with benign endometriosis foci nearby, supporting a potential Müllerian origin and association with endometriosis-related ovarian cancers. The tumor harbored multiple mutations including KRAS and PIK3CA (common in MLA), alongside novel findings of FBXW7 and NF1 mutations in MLA, plus additional alterations in ATM, MSH2, MSH6, PPP2R1A, and MST1, with loss of mismatch repair protein expression characteristic of MMRd tumors. Immunohistochemically, the tumor expressed PAX8, TTF1, and CD10, while showing PD-L1 positivity and lacking GATA3 and WT1 expression. The molecular profile and high intratumoral inflammatory burden typical of MMRd endometrial cancers suggest MLA may share biological features with Müllerian neoplasms, potentially making it amenable to immunotherapy-based treatment strategies.

title: Isolated low-grade serous carcinoma arising in inguinal lymph nodes in the setting of endosalpingiosis: A case report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of low-grade serous carcinoma (LGSC) arising in an inguinal lymph node in association with endosalpingiosis, a benign condition histologically similar to endometriosis but composed of tubal-like epithelium without the inflammatory response characteristic of endometriosis. LGSC is an indolent tumor characterized by slow progression, low mitotic index, and micropapillary architecture, with MAPK pathway mutations (KRAS and BRAF) present in up to 50% of cases and p53 wild-type staining that distinguishes it from the more aggressive high-grade serous carcinoma. The immunohistochemical profile showed positivity for WT-1, PAX-8, p53 (wild-type), P16, and estrogen receptor, with negative HER2 and progesterone receptor expression, and a low proliferative index (<5%) on Ki-67. The patient received neoadjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel, which reduced CA 125 levels, followed by surgical resection and hormonal adjuvant therapy. This case highlights the diagnostic challenges of isolated LGSC without a detectable primary site and emphasizes how misclassification of serous carcinomas can lead to inappropriate treatment strategies and delayed definitive management.

title: Distinct endometriosis involvement confers divergent oncologic outcomes in ovarian clear cell carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is strongly associated with ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) and ovarian endometrioid carcinoma through chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation, with the estrogen-dependent nature of endometriosis driving altered receptor expression patterns including markedly elevated estrogen receptor beta and reduced progesterone receptor levels in endometrial tissues. The nature of endometriosis involvement in OCCC critically influences clinical outcomes: patients with OCCC arising from endometriosis (the Arising group) demonstrated superior prognosis compared to those with coexisting endometriosis or no endometriosis, with the Arising group achieving a 5-year overall survival of 92.4%, lower lymph node metastasis rates (2.8%), and substantially reduced recurrence rates (8.4%) relative to the Without group (27.69%). The improved outcomes in the Arising group were associated with earlier FIGO stage disease at diagnosis, complete tumor resection, and unique atypical endometriosis features, whereas FIGO stage emerged as the only independent prognostic factor for overall survival in multivariate analysis. OCCC diagnosis is reliably confirmed through a distinctive immunohistochemical profile including high expression of PAX-8, HNF-1β, Napsin A, and CK7 alongside characteristic negativity for CK20, WT1, and hormone receptors, supporting accurate differentiation from other malignancies and endometrial origins.

title: Laparoscopic resection of a giant post-mesenteric ovarian seromucinous cystadenoma: a case report and review
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian seromucinous tumors are rare epithelial neoplasms characterized by mixed serous and mucinous components with papillary structures, frequently associated with endometriosis and exhibiting a distinctive immunophenotype including CK7, PAX-8, and MUC5AC positivity with ER negativity. Large tumors exceeding 10 centimeters commonly present with progressive abdominal distension, epigastric discomfort, and morning cramping, with elevated CA-125 serving as a supporting diagnostic marker. These tumors show loss of ARID1A expression, similar to endometrioid and clear cell ovarian malignancies, and may display variable epithelial differentiation including transitional, squamous, and endometrioid components. Single-port laparoscopic surgery represents an effective minimally invasive approach for managing giant seromucinous cystadenomas, offering significant advantages including reduced tissue trauma, minimal scarring, and diminished postoperative pain compared to traditional open surgery. Specialized surgical techniques such as suture ring application and controlled suction during laparoscopic resection prevent cystic fluid leakage and mitigate operative complications while facilitating expedited recovery in this challenging patient population.

title: Symptomatic Tricuspid Valve Obstruction Due to Intravenous Leiomyomatosis
summary: Intravenous leiomyomatosis (IVL) is a rare benign neoplasm typically arising from uterine fibroids in premenopausal women that can extend intravascularly through the inferior vena cava into the right atrium. This case describes a patient presenting with chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, syncope, and lower extremity edema caused by a 2.4 × 3.1 cm IVL mass crossing the tricuspid annulus, resulting in tricuspid regurgitation and obstruction. The tumor cells express estrogen and progesterone receptors, smooth muscle actin, and desmin, confirming smooth muscle origin. Untreated IVL with cardiac extension carries serious complications including right-sided heart failure, pulmonary embolism, and sudden cardiac death. Symptomatic tricuspid valve obstruction can be temporarily managed with fluid boluses to maintain preload and prevent hemodynamic compromise. Definitive treatment requires surgical resection, making early recognition of IVL critical in premenopausal women presenting with cardiac symptoms and a history of uterine fibroids.

title: Comprehensive analysis of calcification frequency and patterns in ovarian tumours using non-contrast CT
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive non-contrast CT analysis identifies calcification patterns as a reliable diagnostic tool for distinguishing ovarian tumor histotypes and malignancy potential. Benign epithelial tumors, particularly mucinous and Brenner tumors, demonstrate significantly higher calcification frequencies than borderline tumors and carcinomas, with germ cell tumors also showing elevated calcification rates while metastatic tumors characteristically lack calcification. Specific calcification morphologies correlate with tumor type: linear patterns predominate in mucinous tumors and germ cell tumors, while amorphous patterns are more common in serous and Brenner tumors, with punctate and coarse calcifications appearing predominantly in epithelial and germ cell malignancies. Notably, clear cell carcinomas associated with endometriosis can present with calcifications, and stromal calcification patterns specifically indicate fibroma-rich tumors.
