These findings indicate that calcification identification and pattern recognition on non-contrast CT provides valuable diagnostic utility for tumor classification and potentially predicting malignant potential, with distribution patterns (diffuse versus localized) further refining histological assessment.

title: A novel method for endometrial cancer patient stratification considering ARID1A protein expression and activity with effective use of multi-omics data
summary:
# Summary

This study presents a novel stratification method for endometrial cancer patients based on ARID1A protein expression and functional activity, integrating multi-omics data to improve patient classification and treatment selection. ARID1A, a core subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, is frequently mutated in endometrial cancers (46.29% in uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma) and binds AT-rich DNA sequences to regulate gene expression and facilitate DNA damage repair. ARID1A mutations—ranging from frameshift to missense variants—cause functional disruption and chromosomal instability, with haploinsufficiency effects reducing protein expression levels. By integrating proteomics and transcriptomics data alongside protein-protein interaction networks, the authors identified distinct patient groups with different functional ARID1A states and revealed immune-related differences that are undetectable by transcriptomics alone. This stratification approach identified ARID1A-deficient patients as candidates for emerging therapies including immune checkpoint inhibitors and novel agents like NXP800, which has received FDA fast-track designation for platinum-resistant, ARID1A-mutated ovarian carcinoma. The study demonstrates that proteomics-informed functional state analysis enables discovery of therapeutic vulnerabilities and immune alterations relevant to personalized treatment of endometriosis-associated malignancies.

title: Immunohistochemical analysis of 147 cases of low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma: refining the immunohistochemical profile of LG-ESS on a large, molecularly confirmed series
summary:
# Summary

This large immunohistochemical study of 147 molecularly confirmed low-grade endometrial stromal sarcomas (LG-ESS) refined the diagnostic and prognostic biomarker profile of this rare endometriosis-associated malignancy. CD10 and IFITM1 emerged as the primary diagnostic markers, detected in 86% and 69% of cases respectively, with significantly higher expression in fusion-positive tumors. Smooth muscle markers (α-SMA, desmin, calponin, and transgelin) were frequently expressed in LG-ESS, though h-caldesmon and smoothelin were notably rare, helping distinguish LG-ESS from benign leiomyomas and smooth muscle tumors. Hormone receptors ER and PR were highly prevalent (83% and 92%), supporting the therapeutic efficacy of hormone-based treatments including high-dose progestins, aromatase inhibitors, and GnRH analogues, which reduce recurrence risk in early-stage disease. Most cases exhibited the characteristic fusion genes (JAZF1::SUZ12, JAZF1::PHF1, EPC1::PHF1, and MEAF6::PHF1) with preserved SMARCB1/SMARCA4 expression, while negative markers (S-100, HMB45, CD117, ALK, ROS1) helped exclude differential diagnoses. These findings establish a comprehensive immunohistochemical signature that improves diagnostic accuracy and supports hormone-based treatment strategies for this frequently misdiagnosed tumor.

title: Primary serous borderline tumor arising from the vaginal stump
summary: This case report describes a primary serous borderline tumor arising from the vaginal stump in a postmenopausal woman with a history of hysterectomy and bilateral ovarian endometriosis. The article establishes that endometriosis is a significant risk factor for malignant transformation, with strong associations demonstrated between endometriosis and multiple cancer histologies including endometrioid carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, and high-grade serous carcinoma. The immunohistochemical analysis of the serous borderline tumor revealed expression of WT-1, P53, and intact mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, MSH6), providing molecular characterization of this rare neoplasm. Additionally, the article reviews that vaginal cancer arising from endometriosis most commonly presents as endometrioid adenocarcinoma or endometrial stromal sarcoma, while primary vaginal carcinomas in the general population are predominantly squamous in histology. High-risk human papillomavirus infection is identified as an alternative etiology for vaginal malignancies, specifically associated with squamous carcinoma and vaginal intraepithelial neoplasia. This case highlights the clinical importance of recognizing endometriosis-associated malignant transformation in post-hysterectomy patients and the distinct pathogenic mechanisms underlying different vaginal cancer subtypes.

title: Clinico-Morphological Features and Immunohistochemical Profile of a Rare Case of Three Synchronous Primary Malignancies in the Female Genital Tract
summary: This case report documents three synchronous primary malignancies in the female genital tract, featuring an immunohistochemical analysis of tumor markers and their prognostic significance. The study demonstrates that p53 overexpression was detected in two of the three tumors and is associated with unfavorable prognosis, chemoresistance, and increased myometrial invasion, though p53 mutations are notably rare in clear-cell ovarian carcinomas. Clear-cell carcinomas were identified and characterized by the absence of estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and WT1 expression, but strong positivity for Napsin-A and AMACR, which serve as reliable immunohistochemical markers for diagnosis. The immunophenotypic profile revealed p63 and p16 expression consistent with squamous components in adenosquamous carcinoma, while loss of ER and PR expression predicted unfavorable prognosis and increased recurrence risk. The patient received carboplatin and paclitaxel chemotherapy but subsequently died from complications including thrombocytopenia leading to massive intestinal hemorrhage, with elevated preoperative CA-125 levels reflecting the aggressive nature of synchronous malignancies.

title: Ovarian squamous cell carcinoma: clinicopathological features, prognosis and immunotherapy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a rare malignancy most commonly arising from malignant transformation of mature cystic teratomas (54.8% of cases), with endometriosis accounting for a smaller but significant proportion (14.3%) of cases. At the molecular level, ovarian SCC exhibits high-frequency mutations in TP53, CDKN2A, and PIK3CA genes, with PIK3CA mutations specifically activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway to promote tumor growth, and overall tumor mutation burden reaching 13.4–25.7 mutations/Mb—substantially higher than other ovarian malignancies. Prognosis is significantly influenced by disease stage and patient age, with stage IA patients showing superior outcomes and older age (≥50 years) associated with better overall survival, whereas endometriosis-associated SCC carries very poor prognosis compared to teratoma-derived tumors. Current treatment approaches include cytoreductive surgery followed by chemotherapy, with paclitaxel and platinum-based regimens preferred, and emerging evidence suggests immune checkpoint inhibitors (sintilimab, tislelizumab, toripalimab) and PI3K pathway inhibitors (sirolimus) may benefit patients with high tumor mutation burden. Notably, fertility-sparing surgery can be considered in younger patients with early-stage teratoma-derived SCC, and stage IA patients demonstrated excellent outcomes with some achieving successful pregnancy after treatment.

title: A diagnostic challenge of primary serous borderline tumor involving the peritoneum: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Primary peritoneal serous borderline tumors (PPSBTs) are rare epithelial neoplasms arising from Müllerian-derived peritoneal mesothelium or peritoneal inclusions, characterized by non-invasive implants confined to the peritoneum. Diagnosis requires integrated microscopic examination and immunohistochemical staining, with tumor cells typically expressing PAX8 and WT1 while remaining negative for mesothelial markers calretinin and D2-40, in contrast to florid mesothelial hyperplasia which shows the opposite pattern. PPSBTs demonstrate higher Ki-67 and HER-2/neu expression compared to primary ovarian tumors and carry an excellent prognosis with approximately 95% survival rates, though long-term follow-up is essential due to the risk of recurrence and potential progression to low-grade serous carcinoma. Complete surgical excision with preservation of reproductive organs in young patients represents the standard treatment approach. The key diagnostic challenge lies in distinguishing PPSBTs from benign mesothelial proliferations and other peritoneal malignancies through careful pathological evaluation and appropriate immunophenotyping.

title: Vaginal Adenocarcinoma: A Review of a Rare Gynecologic Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Vaginal adenocarcinoma is a rare gynecologic malignancy with distinct histologic subtypes driven by different etiologic factors. Clear cell adenocarcinoma, which typically affects younger women (peak incidence ages 17–21), is strongly associated with in utero diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure, whereas mesonephric adenocarcinoma is DES-independent and characterized by KRAS mutations (60% of cases), TP53 mutations, and expression of mesonephric markers including GATA3, TTF1, and PAX2. Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, smoking, and HIV-related immunosuppression represent additional risk factors for vaginal adenocarcinoma, particularly for HPV-associated variants showing p16 overexpression, though endometrioid adenocarcinoma may originate from endometriosis independent of HPV. Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma can be distinguished from endometrial and cervical origins through immunohistochemical profiling, with high expression of CDX2, CK20, SATB2, and CEA, contrasting markedly with endometrial tumors (PAX8+, CK7+) and serous papillary tumors (WT1+, PAX8+). Clinical presentation includes abnormal vaginal bleeding, vaginal discharge, palpable mass, and pelvic pain, with diagnosis aided by MRI for local staging and colposcopy or examination under anesthesia for tissue confirmation. Treatment typically combines surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy tailored to stage and histology, with emerging immunotherapy approaches such as pembrolizumab offering additional options for advanced disease.

title: Pathophysiology of Endometriosis: Insights from Immunohistochemical Analysis of Ectopic and Eutopic Tissues
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex gynecological disorder characterized by chronic pain and infertility, driven by profound alterations in the immunological and molecular landscape of ectopic endometrial tissues. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals elevated levels of inflammatory mediators including leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF), mast cells, cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), and corticotropin-releasing hormone in ectopic tissues, which collectively drive neuroinflammation and pain sensation through transient receptor potential channels (TRPA1 and TRPV1). Adhesion molecule abnormalities, particularly altered expression of integrin subtypes (α4β1 and α5β1) and reduced CD44 and E-cadherin, contribute to aberrant cellular interactions and impaired implantation. The ectopic microenvironment exhibits reduced apoptosis and increased survival signaling, evidenced by elevated BCL-2 and reduced p53 expression, alongside diminished estrogen and progesterone receptor expression that disrupts normal steroid hormone responses. Transcriptional reprogramming, including elevated Oct-4 expression in ectopic tissues, suggests enhanced stemness of endometriotic lesions. These molecular alterations establish a permissive environment for lesion establishment, pain generation, and notably, a shift toward malignant transformation in atypical ovarian endometriosis.

title: The difficult diagnosis of a patient presenting with a Krukenberg tumor and Lynch syndrome: a case report
summary: This case report describes a rare concurrent presentation of Krukenberg tumor and Lynch syndrome in a 35-year-old female patient, representing the first documented association between these two conditions. Krukenberg tumors are ovarian metastases typically originating from primary gastrointestinal malignancies, most commonly colorectal adenocarcinoma, and account for approximately 1% of ovarian tumors, though they complicate up to 4.6% of colorectal cancers in premenopausal women. The patient presented with bowel obstruction and was found to have microsatellite instability with loss of MSH2 expression, molecular features diagnostic of Lynch syndrome, a hereditary cancer predisposition syndrome predominantly associated with colorectal, endometrial, and urinary tract malignancies. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed SATB2 positivity, confirming colorectal origin of the metastatic lesion. The presence of microsatellite instability in an ovarian tumor should alert clinicians to the possibility of underlying Lynch syndrome, as this finding is rarely observed in ovarian neoplasms. The patient was treated with pembrolizumab, an anti-PD-1 immunotherapy, based on the microsatellite instability status.

title: Pathogenesis of peritoneal high‐grade serous carcinoma after risk‐reducing surgery: a systematic review
journal: The Journal of Pathology: Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

Germline BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants substantially increase the risk for high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), which most commonly originates in the fallopian tube epithelium, particularly in precursor lesions called serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) located at the distal fimbriated end. STIC shares identical TP53 mutations with concurrent HGSC, establishing a clonal relationship and supporting STIC as a direct precursor to peritoneal HGSC, with detection of STIC at risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy increasing the 10-year HGSC risk to 27.5%. Additionally, serous endometrial intraepithelial carcinoma (SEIC) similarly demonstrates clonal relationships with extra-uterine HGSC through shared TP53 mutations, suggesting a secondary endometrial origin pathway. Following tumor shedding from the fallopian tube, precursor cells survive in ultra-low attachment conditions within the peritoneal cavity through mechanisms involving follicular fluid factors such as hepatocyte growth factor, while omental adipocytes and their secreted adipokines further support metastatic dissemination. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy achieves a 96% reduction in HGSC risk when performed within current guideline age ranges, though incomplete fallopian tube removal and residual distal fimbriae may account for the small persistent peritoneal cancer risk observed in these high-risk carriers.

title: Treatment of Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma: A Case of Successful Management With Targeted Therapies
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma is strongly associated with endometriosis and carries a poor prognosis in advanced stages, with tumors frequently harboring mutations in PIK3CA, ARID1A, and ARID1B genes. This case demonstrates successful management of platinum-resistant clear cell carcinoma through targeted and immunotherapeutic approaches: the patient received carboplatin and paclitaxel initially, followed by alpelisib targeting PIK3CA mutations, which achieved partial response with resolution of ascites. Subsequently, nivolumab and ipilimumab checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1 and CTLA-4 pathways were administered, exploiting clear cell carcinoma's elevated microsatellite instability, tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, and PDL1 expression, which confer superior immunotherapy responsiveness compared to other epithelial ovarian cancers. Although the combination immunotherapy significantly improved progression-free survival, the patient developed immune-related adverse events including pituitary hypophysitis and adrenal insufficiency. This case illustrates that personalized, mutation-directed therapy combined with checkpoint inhibition offers viable alternatives for platinum-resistant clear cell carcinoma, though careful monitoring for serious immune complications is essential.

title: Low-grade Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma Presenting as a Sigmoid Mass
summary: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LG-ESS) is a rare uterine mesenchymal tumor characterized by slow growth, indolent behavior, and strong immunopositivity for CD10, estrogen receptor, and progesterone receptor. Although LG-ESS typically originates in the uterus with myometrial invasion, it can metastasize to extrauterine sites including the pelvis, peritoneal cavity, vagina, and rarely the colon, as illustrated by this case of metastatic disease presenting as a solitary sigmoid tumor. The tumor's dependence on estrogen and progesterone receptors makes it sensitive to hormone therapy, with progesterone serving as an important adjuvant treatment option, while tamoxifen and estrogen are contraindicated as they stimulate disseminated stromal cells. Standard management involves surgical resection of the primary tumor and cytoreduction of extrauterine lesions, with postoperative radiation therapy reducing local recurrence risk, though without improving overall survival. The diagnosis is supported by immunochemical markers that distinguish LG-ESS from related entities like gastrointestinal stromal tumors and can be associated with underlying endometriosis at metastatic sites. Despite its indolent nature, LG-ESS requires vigilant surveillance as metastasis and recurrence can occur after extended disease-free intervals.

title: Benign metastasizing leiomyoma with retroperitoneal lymph node involvement in a patient with a history of renal cell carcinoma
journal: Indian Journal of Urology : IJU : Journal of the Urological Society of India
summary: Benign metastasizing leiomyoma (BML) is a rare condition where smooth muscle tissue originating from uterine fibroids metastasizes to distant sites, most commonly the lungs and lymph nodes, despite exhibiting benign histological features. BML arises through two proposed mechanisms: coelomic metaplasia of mesenchymal tissue or peritoneal seeding of uterine fibroid tissue, and typically occurs in premenopausal women with a history of uterine surgery, particularly hysterectomy. Immunohistochemical analysis of BML tumors characteristically shows positivity for smooth muscle markers (smooth muscle actin, desmin), hormone receptors (estrogen and progesterone receptors), and other markers including Wilms tumor protein 1 and cathepsin K, while remaining negative for melanoma and sarcoma-associated antigens. Estrogen and progestin promote BML growth, suggesting that hormonal management through gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogs, selective estrogen or progesterone receptor modulators, aromatase inhibitors, and oophorectomy may provide therapeutic benefit. This case describes an unusual presentation of BML in the retroperitoneum in a patient with concurrent clear cell renal carcinoma and prior endometriosis, highlighting the importance of considering BML in the differential diagnosis of metastatic lesions in women with uterine pathology.

title: Synchronous endometrial/cervical and ovarian/fallopian tube carcinoma: a genome‐wide mutation analysis
journal: The Journal of Pathology: Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

This genome-wide mutation analysis of synchronous endometrial/cervical and ovarian/fallopian tube carcinomas reveals that the majority of cases arise from clonally related tumors spreading bidirectionally through trans-tubal dissemination rather than independent primary tumors. Whole-exome sequencing detected shared mutations in 58.3% of synchronous endometrial-ovarian carcinoma cases and all cervical-ovarian cases, with pathogenic POLE mutations and bilateral ovarian involvement significantly associated with metastatic disease. The study demonstrates that low-grade endometrioid carcinomas with superficial myometrial invasion have favorable outcomes (100% five-year survival at stage IA3), while TP53 mutations were exclusively found in advanced-stage disease and correlated with poorer survival. Notably, endometriosis and atypical hyperplasia were found only in cases classified as dual primary tumors, and dedifferentiation may occur during the dissemination process, explaining histological discordance between paired tumors. The FIGO 2023 classification effectively stratifies patient prognosis, with trans-tubal spread emerging as a more accurate descriptor of dissemination than metastasis given the generally favorable clinical outcomes.

title: A Case of Myoepithelioma-Like Tumor of the Vulvar Region after Removal of an Inguinal Tumor
summary: This case report describes a myoepithelioma-like tumor of the vulvar region (MELTVR), a rare mesenchymal neoplasm that typically arises in the groin or vulvar areas and is characterized by intra-tumoral tissue diversity with mucoid stroma. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that tumor cells expressed epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PgR), and alpha-smooth muscle actin, while being negative for markers typically seen in conventional myoepitheliomas such as cytokeratin, p63, desmin, S100, GFAP, and SOX10, with reduced INI1 protein expression. The consistent positivity for ER and EMA, along with PgR positivity, provides diagnostic markers that distinguish MELTVR from other soft tissue tumors. The case is clinically relevant as Nuck's cysts, which are frequently associated with endometriosis, have been linked to MELTVR development. Surgical wide excision with complete resection represents the standard treatment approach, as evidenced by all twelve documented MELTVR cases undergoing surgical intervention. This case contributes to understanding of endometriosis-associated malignant transformation in the vulvovaginal region.

title: Gastric ectopic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma coexisted with endometriosis mimick advanced disease: a case report and literature review
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of gastric ectopic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma coexisting with endometriosis, highlighting the diagnostic and therapeutic challenges of malignant transformation in ectopic pancreatic tissue. Ectopic pancreas most commonly occurs in the upper gastrointestinal tract, particularly the stomach (35%), duodenum (34%), and proximal jejunum (13%), yet malignant transformation remains extremely rare despite well-documented precursor lesions such as acinar-ductal metaplasia and pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia. The patient's tumor expressed immunologically significant markers including PD-L1 and HER2, alongside elevated CA199 serum levels, while concurrent endometriosis at the rectosigmoid junction expressed estrogen and progesterone receptors with increased 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose uptake related to inflammation-induced glucose metabolism. Notably, the tumor regressed following combined treatment with nivolumab (an anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) and XELOX chemotherapy, suggesting that malignant ectopic pancreatic tumors may have improved immunotherapy responsiveness compared to primary pancreatic cancers, which are typically considered immune-privileged due to infiltrating regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells. Diagnosis required endoscopic ultrasonography and endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration, emphasizing the importance of advanced imaging and tissue confirmation when evaluating gastric lesions, particularly in cases where inflammatory conditions like endometriosis may complicate clinical presentation and imaging interpretation.

title: Rectal Clear Cell Carcinoma Arising from Endometriosis: Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, particularly when located in the rectovaginal region, carries a rare but significant risk of malignant transformation into endometriosis-associated neoplasms, predominantly clear cell carcinoma and endometrioid adenocarcinoma. This case report documents an exceptionally uncommon presentation of rectal clear cell carcinoma arising from rectovaginal endometriosis in a 43-year-old woman, with malignant transformation occurring five years after initial ovarian endometrioma surgery. The malignant transformation is driven by multiple mechanisms including hormone imbalance, chronic inflammation, reactive oxygen species accumulation, and somatic mutations in tumor suppressor genes (ARID1A, PTEN, p53) and oncogenic pathways (PIK3CA, KRAS), with increased risk during perimenopause and postmenopause. Clear cell carcinomas developing from intestinal endometriosis display a characteristic Müllerian immunophenotype (positive for CK7, CK20, CDX2, PAX8, and HNF-1β; negative for WT1, estrogen and progestin receptors) that aids in diagnosis and distinguishes them from primary colorectal malignancies. While endometriosis-associated intestinal tumors remain exceptionally rare compared to the high prevalence of endometriosis overall, their potential occurrence necessitates vigilant clinical monitoring, particularly for deep endometriosis involving the bowel, and consideration of prophylactic surgical treatment when feasible.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Usual- and Gastric-Type Cervical Adenocarcinoma in a Japanese Population Reveals Distinct Clinicopathological and Molecular Features with Prognostic and Therapeutic Insights
summary:
# Summary

This comparative analysis reveals that gastric-type cervical adenocarcinoma (GCA) and usual-type cervical adenocarcinoma (UCA) represent distinctly different malignancies with contrasting molecular pathogenesis and clinical outcomes. UCA is HPV-driven, characterized by strong p16 expression and RB1 pathway inactivation via HPV E7, whereas GCA develops independently of HPV and harbors mutations in TP53, ARID1A, KRAS, and BRAF that drive alternative oncogenic pathways. Clinically, GCA displays significantly more aggressive behavior, with higher rates of lymph node involvement, advanced FIGO staging, vaginal invasion, and recurrence compared to UCA, translating to substantially worse progression-free and overall survival outcomes. High ARID1B expression in GCA—likely compensating for ARID1A loss within the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex—and elevated PD-L1 expression correlate with poor prognosis and distant metastasis. The distinct molecular profiles suggest different therapeutic strategies, with GCA showing resistance to conventional treatments but potential responsiveness to immunotherapy, particularly in cases with high PD-L1 expression combined with elevated CD8+ tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes.

title: Magnetic resonance imaging diagnosis of leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata: Case report and literature review
summary: Leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata (LPD) is a rare benign smooth muscle proliferative disorder frequently associated with uterine leiomyomas and endometriosis, characterized by multiple smooth muscle cell hyperplasia distributed across peritoneal surfaces including the rectouterine pouch, mesentery, and greater omentum. The condition develops secondarily following laparoscopic myomectomy or hysterectomy, with contributing risk factors including excessive estrogen secretion, sex hormone elevation, and genetic predisposition. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals positive staining for smooth muscle markers (smooth muscle actin and desmin) and proliferation markers (Ki-67 at 30% and P16 at 40%), while being negative for melanoma-associated markers, helping distinguish LPD from other peritoneal malignancies. Magnetic resonance imaging provides superior diagnostic value by revealing the characteristic distribution pattern of solid and mixed cystic-solid masses while distinguishing LPD from ovarian cancer and peritoneal metastases, notably by demonstrating the absence of ascites unlike malignant tumors. Although malignant transformation occurs in approximately 2-5% of LPD cases, elevated CA-125 levels and imaging combined with histopathological confirmation remain essential for accurate diagnosis and surveillance.

title: Diagnostic and surgical management of giant broad ligament myoma with cystic degeneration: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Giant broad ligament leiomyomas frequently undergo cystic degeneration and can present with diverse symptoms including abdominal distension, pain, urinary frequency, and constipation, though some remain asymptomatic. The diagnostic challenge is compounded by elevated CA-125 levels, which correlate with fibroid size and can mimic malignancy, particularly ovarian cancer, a phenomenon associated with pseudo-Meigs syndrome. Immunohistochemical analysis of leiomyomas characteristically shows positivity for smooth muscle markers (SMA, caldesmon, desmin), hormone receptors (ER, PR), and vimentin, while remaining negative for gastrointestinal stromal tumor markers (CD117, DOG-1) and neural markers, with low Ki-67 proliferation indices. The broad ligament's rich vascular supply and anatomical proximity to vital structures create substantial surgical risk, including potential complications such as ureteral injury, pelvic hematoma, peritoneal hemorrhage, and incisional hernia. Surgical excision remains the definitive treatment, with preoperative imaging via MRI and transvaginal ultrasonography essential for accurate diagnosis and surgical planning.

title: A Case of Intramural Adenosarcoma With Sarcomatous Overgrowth Associated With Adenomyosis and Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an intramural adenosarcoma with sarcomatous overgrowth found in a 59-year-old woman and highlights its strong association with adenomyosis and endometriosis, conditions that may undergo malignant transformation. The adenosarcoma displayed characteristic immunohistochemical features including positivity for estrogen receptor (ER), CD10, and notably elevated p53 expression, while being negative for cyclin D1, desmin, and other mesenchymal markers. The presence of sarcomatous overgrowth, along with lymphovascular invasion, necrosis, and rhabdomyoblastic differentiation, served as poor prognostic indicators associated with worse progression-free and overall survival. The patient presented with typical adenosarcoma symptoms including abnormal vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, vaginal discharge, and a palpable pelvic mass, and was treated with sequential chemotherapy including doxorubicin, cisplatin, ifosfamide, and eribulin. The case underscores the critical importance of considering adenosarcoma as a rare but severe malignant complication in patients with endometriosis and adenomyosis, with adenosarcoma arising from endometriosis occurring in approximately 0.3% of endometriosis cases.

title: Evaluation of cell-free DNA from papanicolaou smears and peripheral blood to detect endometrial cancer
summary: This study demonstrates that cell-free DNA (cfDNA) from both Pap smears and peripheral blood effectively detects endometrial cancer through somatic mutations, with Pap smear cfDNA showing superior sensitivity (77.4%) compared to plasma (42.9%). Advanced disease features—including higher histopathological grades, lymph node metastases, and lymphovascular space involvement—strongly correlate with elevated plasma cfDNA concentrations and increased mutation detection rates. The most frequently mutated genes in endometrial cancer tissue include PTEN (48.8%), PIK3CA (46.4%), and CTNNB1 (28.6%), with these mutations also readily detectable in cfDNA from both body fluid sources. Notably, tumor-derived DNA was detected in all tissue samples from endometrial cancer patients but also in 84.6% of endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia samples, suggesting potential utility for disease stratification. Type II endometrial carcinomas showed distinctive TP53 mutations, highlighting mutation patterns' clinical relevance. These findings support multi-gene panel liquid biopsy approaches using Pap smears and plasma cfDNA as sensitive, noninvasive tools for early endometrial cancer detection and risk stratification.

title: Pulmonary Benign Metastasizing Leiomyoma: A Retrospective Analysis of Seven Cases Including a Rare Coexistence with In Situ Mucinous Adenocarcinoma
summary: This retrospective analysis of seven pulmonary benign metastasizing leiomyoma (PBML) cases demonstrates that this rare condition invariably arises from a prior history of uterine leiomyoma and exhibits characteristic immunohistochemical features including positive smooth muscle actin, desmin, and caldesmon expression, with high prevalence of estrogen and progesterone receptor positivity despite low proliferative activity (Ki-67 ≤3%). The pathogenesis likely involves estrogen-driven proliferation through hematogenous or lymphatic dissemination, similar to endometriosis-associated malignant transformation mechanisms wherein inflammatory mediators promote angiogenesis, cellular proliferation, and tumor progression. A particularly notable finding was one case exhibiting rare coexistence of in situ mucinous adenocarcinoma within the PBML lesion, characterized by CDX2, villin, and mucin expression, raising concerns about malignant potential in this typically indolent condition. While most PBML cases remain asymptomatic, approximately one-third to one-half of patients develop respiratory symptoms including cough, sputum production, dyspnea, or hemoptysis, with imaging showing minimal fluorodeoxyglucose uptake but increased fibroblast-activating protein inhibitor uptake suggestive of activated fibroblasts. Diagnostic confirmation requires video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery or computed tomography-guided biopsy, and GnRH analog therapy may provide adjunctive treatment, though long-term follow-up is essential given documented risk of distant metastasis to pelvic and vascular structures.

title: The -124C>T Mutation of the TERT Promoter Indicates Favorable Prognosis in Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma: A Single Institutional Study in China
summary: This study identifies the -124C>T TERT promoter mutation as a favorable prognostic marker in ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), an endometriosis-associated malignancy characterized by chemoresistance and poor outcomes. The -124C>T mutation was independently associated with longer overall and progression-free survival, lower preoperative CA125 levels, and higher single nucleotide polymorphism frequency in OCCC patients. In contrast, TP53 mutations emerged as a strong negative prognostic factor, correlating with advanced disease, bilateral tumors, elevated CA125, and worse survival outcomes. ARID1A deficiency, detected in 54% of patients, indirectly promotes poor prognosis through increased HDAC6 expression and higher p53 mutation incidence, whereas low HDAC6 expression itself associated with worse progression-free survival. The study reveals complex mutational patterns including mutual exclusivity between PIK3CA exon 20 mutations and TERT promoter alterations, as well as between ARID1A and TP53 mutations, suggesting distinct molecular subtypes of OCCC with different clinical trajectories. These molecular findings provide a basis for risk stratification and personalized treatment approaches in endometriosis-associated ovarian clear cell carcinoma.

title: Optimizing treatment for platinum‐resistant ovarian clear cell carcinoma: Efficacy of gemcitabine and combination therapy with bevacizumab
summary: Platinum-resistant ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) is a chemotherapy-refractory malignancy characterized by hypoxia-inducible factor activation, which drives increased glycolysis and pentose phosphate pathway activity. Gemcitabine emerges as an effective treatment option, particularly in ARID1A-deficient tumors, where the frequent loss-of-function mutations of this chromatin remodeler sensitize cells to gemcitabine-induced apoptosis through enhanced DNA replication inhibition. The hypoxic microenvironment paradoxically increases deoxycytidine kinase activity, facilitating gemcitabine activation and nucleotide incorporation into tumor cell DNA. Combining gemcitabine with bevacizumab significantly improves progression-free survival and time to treatment failure compared to chemotherapy alone, with a trend toward prolonged overall survival. ARID1A loss, occurring either through genetic mutation or epigenetic silencing via DNA methylation, represents a predictive biomarker for gemcitabine efficacy and drug sensitivity. These findings suggest that treatment optimization for platinum-resistant OCCC should incorporate ARID1A status assessment to guide gemcitabine-based therapy and consider bevacizumab combination approaches to enhance clinical outcomes.

title: Advances in Systemic Therapy for Ovarian Cancer Over the Past Decade: A Clinical and Molecular Perspective
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

This review examines molecular advances in ovarian cancer systemic therapy, focusing on distinct genetic subtypes and their therapeutic implications. High-grade serous ovarian cancer, characterized by near-universal TP53 mutations and frequent BRCA1/2 alterations, represents the most common and aggressive form, with homologous recombination deficiency driving sensitivity to PARP inhibitors and platinum-based chemotherapy. In contrast, low-grade serous tumors harbor KRAS, BRAF, and NRAS mutations instead of TP53 alterations and respond poorly to conventional chemotherapy, requiring alternative approaches such as MEK inhibitors like trametinib. Endometriosis-associated malignancies, including endometrioid and clear cell subtypes, involve distinct genetic pathways with PTEN/PI3K/AKT alterations in endometrioid tumors and ARID1A/PIK3CA mutations in clear cell carcinomas. The review emphasizes that BRCA-mutated patients experience improved progression-free survival with PARP inhibitors, exemplifying how molecular profiling enables personalized treatment strategies in epithelial ovarian cancer management.

title: Adenosarcoma of uterus characterized by differentiation of rhabdomyosarcoma and chondrosarcoma: A case report and review of literature
summary:
# Summary

Müllerian adenosarcoma is a rare uterine malignancy that typically presents in postmenopausal women with postmenopausal bleeding, pelvic pain, urinary frequency, and urinary urgency. This case report describes a tumor with heterologous differentiation containing rhabdomyosarcoma and chondrosarcoma elements, which significantly worsened the prognosis compared to adenosarcoma's generally low malignant potential. The presence of sarcomatous overgrowth (≥25% pure sarcoma), myoinvasion, and heterologous elements substantially increased malignant potential and recurrence risk, with sarcomatous overgrowth cases showing recurrence rates of 70–82%. Standard treatment involves surgical resection with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, supplemented by adjuvant chemotherapy regimens (gemcitabine/docetaxel, doxorubicin-based combinations, or carboplatin/paclitaxel) for tumors with sarcomatous overgrowth. While stage I adenosarcomas without sarcomatous overgrowth have favorable 5-year survival, the presence of adverse pathologic features and pulmonary metastasis significantly compromises patient outcomes, as demonstrated in this fatal case.

title: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma: a case report and literature review
summary: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LG-ESS) is a rare malignant tumor originating from endometrial stromal cells that commonly presents with dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and pelvic pain. Risk factors for LG-ESS development include obesity, diabetes, early menarche, long-term tamoxifen or estrogen supplementation, and pelvic irradiation, with recent evidence suggesting that laparoscopic power morcellation and uterine artery embolization may accelerate tumor progression by stimulating angiogenesis and altering sex hormone levels. The tumor exhibits high expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors (ER and PR), along with the pathognomonic JAZF1-SUZ12 gene fusion associated with t(7;17) translocation, and can metastasize beyond the uterus to the ovaries, peritoneum, bowel, bladder, vagina, and lungs, particularly from malignant transformation of ectopic endometriotic lesions. Standard treatment consists of total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and tumor debulking, avoiding morcellation to prevent dissemination. Adjuvant anti-estrogen therapy using aromatase inhibitors such as letrozole or progestins is recommended given the hormone sensitivity of these tumors, while MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging and ADC values aids in preoperative diagnosis.

title: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT): About three case reports
summary: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary, hypercalcemic type (SCCOHT) is a rare and highly aggressive ovarian malignancy affecting young women, characterized by inactivating mutations in the SMARCA4 gene that encodes a critical subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex. Loss of SMARCA4 (BRG1) protein expression, detected through immunohistochemistry, serves as a diagnostic hallmark, though the tumor can mimic other ovarian malignancies on imaging including dysgerminoma and mucinous cystadenocarcinoma, complicating preoperative diagnosis. SCCOHT demonstrates rapid progression and typically presents at advanced stages with poor prognosis; disease-free survival remains below 10% for advanced cases, partly due to chemoresistance associated with SWI/SNF dysfunction. Current treatment relies on intensive multimodal therapy combining radical surgery, platinum-based chemotherapy, whole-abdomen radiotherapy, and high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation, which improves outcomes particularly for stage I disease. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting SMARCA4-mutant tumors include CDK4/6 inhibitors, EZH2 inhibitors, anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy, and multi-targeted approaches combining immune checkpoint inhibitors with antiangiogenic and tyrosine kinase inhibitors to overcome conventional therapy resistance.

title: The treatment of intravascular leiomyomatosis with different clinical manifestations: Two case reports
summary: Intravascular leiomyomatosis (IVL) is a rare benign smooth muscle tumor arising from uterine fibroids that presents with diverse clinical manifestations including abnormal uterine bleeding, lower abdominal discomfort, pelvic masses, lower extremity edema, and cardiac symptoms. The condition is strongly associated with elevated estradiol levels and elevated CA125 markers, suggesting an estrogen-dependent pathophysiology often occurring alongside adenomyosis and endometrial abnormalities. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals consistent expression of desmin, estrogen receptors, CD34, and D2-40, confirming the smooth muscle and vascular origin of these lesions. Complete surgical resection, typically via total hysterectomy, remains the definitive treatment and primary therapeutic approach for IVL. Estrogen deprivation therapy, including gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists and the Qiu empirical formula for reducing estradiol levels, may provide adjunctive symptom management and reduce recurrence risk in select cases. The pathogenesis likely involves local uterine and vascular injury that facilitates abnormal smooth muscle cell invasion into blood vessels, particularly in the setting of estrogen-stimulated growth.

title: Assessment of Stromal Calretinin Expression in Normal and Pathological Endometrium of Patients With Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
summary:
# Summary

Calretinin, a calcium-binding protein encoded by the CALB2 gene, is expressed in endometrial stromal cells and plays important roles in calcium homeostasis, cell signaling, and apoptosis. Expression of calretinin fluctuates with hormonal phases, showing highest levels in secretory endometrium and declining during the menstrual phase, with vitamin D3 and progesterone regulating its expression. In pathological endometrial conditions associated with abnormal uterine bleeding—including adenomyosis, endometrial polyps, endometrial hyperplasia, and endometrioid adenocarcinoma—calretinin expression is substantially reduced or completely absent. This loss of calretinin correlates with abnormal stromal architecture and contributes to uncontrolled endometrial proliferation and tumor development. The findings establish calretinin as a potential diagnostic marker differentiating normal from pathological endometrium, with significantly lower expression (r=−0.43) in diseased states, suggesting its critical role in maintaining normal endometrial growth, differentiation, and remodeling.

title: Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy Is Superior to Salpingectomy Alone in Preventing Non-Tubal Tumor Development in a Mouse Model of High-Grade Serous Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), the leading cause of ovarian cancer mortality, typically arises from fallopian tube epithelium and can develop through progression from serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma. This study used a genetically engineered mouse model (with Brca1, Trp53, Rb1, and Nf1 mutations) to compare the efficacy of bilateral salpingectomy versus bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy in preventing HGSC development. While bilateral salpingectomy significantly reduced HGSC incidence, only bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy completely prevented tumor development, suggesting that residual ovarian endosalpingiosis (ectopic tubal-type epithelium) remaining after salpingectomy can serve as a source for non-tubal HGSC formation. Endosalpingiosis expresses tubal differentiation markers (PAX8, tubulin, OVGP1, WT1) and can progress through serous intraepithelial carcinoma to invasive carcinoma, with increased Ki-67 proliferation marking this transition. These findings support the superiority of bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy over salpingectomy alone for HGSC prevention in BRCA1/2 pathogenic variant carriers and highlight endosalpingiosis as a critical precursor lesion in the development of secondary Müllerian malignancies.

title: Enhanced prognostic stratification in early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer: multi-histological analysis of neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio with particular emphasis on clear cell histology
summary: This study demonstrates that the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), a measure of systemic inflammation and immune dysregulation, independently predicts poor progression-free and overall survival in early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer, with particularly strong prognostic power in clear cell carcinoma patients. Elevated NLR and advanced disease stage both significantly correlated with diminished survival outcomes, with NLR showing hazard ratios exceeding 5 for progression-free survival across the cohort. The analysis revealed distinctive immunological signatures across histological subtypes, with clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas showing markedly higher prevalence in Asian populations, yet NLR demonstrated prognostic relevance only in clear cell cases and not in endometrioid carcinoma, indicating heterogeneous tumor immune microenvironments. These findings highlight how the tumor immune microenvironment critically influences oncological progression and suggest that systemic inflammatory biomarkers like NLR serve as surrogate measures of host immunological responses. The histology-stratified approach identified clear cell carcinoma as particularly sensitive to NLR-based risk stratification, offering enhanced prognostic discrimination in early-stage ovarian cancer management.

title: Primary Endometrioid Adenocarcinoma in the Right Fallopian Tube Mimicking Female Adnexal Tumor of Probable Wolffian Origin: A Diagnostic Pitfall
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a primary endometrioid adenocarcinoma of the fallopian tube that closely mimicked female adnexal tumor of probable Wolffian origin (FATPWO), illustrating an important diagnostic challenge in adnexal pathology. The tumor exhibited overlapping histologic features with FATPWO, including tubular and solid growth patterns, and shared several immunohistochemical characteristics including positivity for AE1/AE3, CAM5.2, and estrogen receptor, while being negative for PAX8 and CK7. However, molecular testing revealed absence of characteristic mutations found in some FATPWO cases (such as KMT2D and STK11), and the tumor lacked STK11 mutations entirely, supporting an endometrioid rather than Wolffian origin. This case highlights that PAX8 and CK7 negativity alone cannot reliably distinguish endometrioid carcinoma from FATPWO, as PAX8 negativity can occur in low-grade endometrioid tumors of the fallopian tube. Accurate diagnosis requires integration of morphologic, immunohistochemical, and molecular findings, and awareness that endometrioid adenocarcinoma can arise in association with endometriosis at various sites beyond the endometrium, including the fallopian tube.

title: Atypical (Symplastic) Leiomyoma Arising in Pararectal Endometriosis Associated With Metastatic Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma: A Case Report
summary: This case report documents an exceptionally rare presentation of concurrent malignant and benign pathologies arising from endometriosis: a pararectal atypical leiomyoma and a primary ovarian clear cell carcinoma with metastases. Endometriosis underwent malignant transformation to clear cell carcinoma, the strongest malignancy association in endometriosis, while simultaneously facilitating smooth muscle metaplasia that resulted in the atypical leiomyoma. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that the endometrial tissue and adjacent endometriosis expressed PAX-8 and estrogen receptor, while the clear cell carcinoma showed strong nuclear staining for HNF1-beta and PAX-8 with scattered estrogen receptor expression. The case also involved a concurrent benign ovarian fibroma expressing WT1, vimentin, and CD56. This unusual presentation illustrates the malignant potential of endometriosis through multiple pathogenic pathways and reinforces the clinical importance of recognizing that endometriosis can generate diverse neoplastic outcomes, including leiomyosarcoma, endometrioid carcinoma, serous carcinoma, and most prominently, clear cell carcinoma.

title: EGFR-TKIs治疗失败的肺腺癌子宫内膜转移1例并文献复习
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a female patient with EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma harboring an EGFR L858R mutation and TP53 K132Q mutation who developed treatment-resistant disease despite EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy with amenitinib and bevacizumab. The patient presented with abnormal vaginal bleeding and was subsequently diagnosed with endometrial metastasis confirmed by immunohistochemistry showing TTF-1, CK7, and Napsin-A positivity with negative estrogen and progesterone receptors, confirming a lung primary rather than uterine origin. The TP53 missense mutation was associated with reduced progression-free survival and EGFR-TKI resistance. After development of pleural metastasis with malignant effusion, the patient received a multi-drug regimen combining pemetrexed, carboplatin, bevacizumab, and the immunotherapy agent sintilimab based on the ORIENT-31 trial, which demonstrated improved outcomes in EGFR-TKI resistant non-small cell lung cancer. The case highlights that EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinomas have enhanced tumor cell invasion capacity and can metastasize to unusual sites like the endometrium, potentially through EGFR pathway interactions with estrogen signaling and CXCR4/CXCL12 axis activation.

title: Premenstrual sub-occlusive episodes as initial presentation of metastatic breast cancer: a case report of a previously undescribed entity
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an unusual presentation of metastatic breast cancer with cyclical premenstrual gastrointestinal symptoms, representing a previously undescribed clinical entity. The patient presented with recurring premenstrual obstruction and abdominal pain caused by breast cancer metastasis to the terminal ileum and ileocecal valve, which are recognized but uncommon sites of spread. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed the tumor cells were positive for breast-specific markers (CK7, mammaglobin, GATA-3, E-cadherin) and hormone receptors (estrogen and progesterone receptor-positive), while negative for HER-2, CK20, and CDX2, confirming a breast origin rather than primary gastrointestinal malignancy. The hormone-sensitive nature of the metastatic lesions, combined with cyclical exposure to menstrual hormones, induced the characteristic cyclical symptoms, a phenomenon explained by retained estrogen and progesterone sensitivity of the cancer cells. The patient was treated with the CDK4/6 inhibitor palbociclib combined with the aromatase inhibitor letrozole, targeting the hormone-responsive metastatic disease. This case highlights that metastatic breast cancer can present with atypical gastrointestinal symptoms and emphasizes the importance of considering occult or primary breast lesions with early metastatic spread in the diagnostic differential.

title: Metastasis of uterine endometrioid carcinoma mimicking upper tract urothelial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents an unusual presentation of metastatic uterine endometrioid carcinoma occurring five years after hysterectomy, with the metastasis localized to the left ureter where it caused hydronephrosis and radiographically mimicked upper tract urothelial carcinoma (UTUC). Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed the diagnosis by demonstrating positive staining for estrogen receptor and PAX8 while showing negative GATA3, a marker pattern consistent with endometrioid carcinoma rather than urothelial origin. The case illustrates an important diagnostic challenge: metastases from primary cancers and benign conditions can simulate UTUC on imaging, potentially leading to unnecessary nephroureterectomy if histopathological confirmation is not obtained. The authors emphasize that computed tomography urography, ureteroscopy, and tissue biopsy are essential diagnostic modalities to differentiate true urothelial carcinoma from mimicking pathologies including endometrial cancer metastases. This report highlights the clinical importance of considering endometrial malignancy in the differential diagnosis of ureteral masses, particularly in patients with prior endometrial cancer history.

title: Malignant Brenner tumor with extensive clear cell features: A valuable diagnostic lesson learned from molecular tumor profiling and AI-based tumor differential tool
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a malignant Brenner tumor presenting with extensive clear cell features that mimicked ovarian clear cell carcinoma, highlighting the diagnostic value of molecular profiling and artificial intelligence tools. Immunohistochemistry revealed diffuse expression of urothelial markers GATA3 and p63 in both the clear cell component and conventional Brenner tumor areas, while the tumor tested negative for markers typical of clear cell carcinoma (Napsin A, WT-1, and Pax-8). Next-generation sequencing identified a characteristic molecular signature including CDKN2A/B loss, MDM2 amplification, and wild-type TP53 and TERT—a profile that was absent the PIK3CA and ARID1A mutations typically associated with endometriosis-related clear cell carcinomas. An artificial intelligence-based tumor differential tool predicted an 88% phenotypic match to urothelial origin, supporting the diagnosis of malignant Brenner tumor. The patient received platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel. This case demonstrates that integrated molecular and immunophenotypic analysis is essential for distinguishing malignant Brenner tumors from morphologically similar clear cell carcinomas and guides appropriate clinical management.

title: Gastrointestinal stromal tumor of the rectum: A case report with emphasis on MRI-pathologic correlation
summary:
# Summary

Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) are mesenchymal neoplasms arising from interstitial cells of Cajal that present clinically with symptoms such as incomplete defecation and can manifest as rectal masses. This case report illustrates the diagnostic importance of multimodal imaging and immunohistochemistry in identifying rectal GIST, as MRI demonstrated characteristic features including homogeneous intermediate signal on T1-weighted imaging, hyperintensity on T2-weighted imaging, and restricted diffusion on diffusion-weighted imaging. Histopathologic examination revealed spindle cell morphology with high mitotic activity and DOG-1 positivity, which are diagnostic hallmarks that distinguish GIST from mimics such as rectal leiomyomas (negative for DOG-1, showing minimal diffusion restriction), rectal adenocarcinomas (exhibiting glandular differentiation and CK20 positivity), and rectal schwannomas (S-100 positive, DOG-1 negative). Complete surgical resection achieved resolution of the patient's defecation symptoms, underscoring the importance of accurate preoperative diagnosis and timely intervention for rectal GISTs.

title: A case report and literature review: Diagnosis of pelvic retroperitoneal angiomyofibroblastoma assisted by next-generation sequencing
summary:
# Summary

Angiomyofibroblastoma (AMFB) is a rare, benign soft tissue tumor primarily arising in the lower reproductive tract of women of reproductive age, with fewer than 150 cases reported since its discovery in 1992. This case report demonstrates the diagnostic utility of next-generation sequencing (NGS) in distinguishing AMFB from histologically similar aggressive mesenchymal tumors, particularly aggressive angiomyxoma, by identifying the characteristic MTG1-CYP2E1 fusion transcript specific to AMFB and confirming the absence of HMGA2 expression. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals that AMFB characteristically expresses CD34, Desmin, S-100, estrogen receptor, and progesterone receptor while maintaining low Ki-67 proliferation indices, consistent with its benign behavior. The case of a pelvic retroperitoneal AMFB highlights the importance of comprehensive molecular and immunological profiling for accurate diagnosis, particularly when tumors occur in atypical locations adjacent to critical pelvic structures. Surgical resection using minimally invasive approaches, such as the Da Vinci robotic system, provides effective treatment while preserving pelvic floor function, nerves, and blood vessels. NGS represents a valuable alternative diagnostic approach that minimizes tissue waste and provides definitive molecular confirmation when morphological and immunohistochemical findings are inconclusive.

title: Endometriosis in para-aortic lymph nodes without any pelvic endometriosis: the first case report, sequencing analysis and literature review
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of endometriosis localized to para-aortic lymph nodes without concurrent pelvic involvement, occurring alongside cystic adenomyosis in a 36-year-old patient presenting with severe dysmenorrhea and an expanding abdominal mass. Sequencing analysis identified FOXA2 and SRRM2 as candidate driver genes for this atypical form of endometriosis, with lesions showing upregulation of genes and pathways associated with cell activation, adhesion, inflammation, and immune responses. The mechanism underlying lymph node dissemination likely involves enhanced lymphangiogenesis and increased lymphatic vessel density facilitated by VEGF-C signaling, enabling endometrial cell migration through lymphatic vessels according to Halban's benign metastasis theory. Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed endometriotic lesions through expression of CK7, PAX-8, WT-1, and CD10, while elevated serum markers (CA125, CA199, and prolactin) supported the diagnosis. The patient's molecular alterations suggest a shared pathogenic pathway with malignant transformation, as FOXA2 and SRRM2 have known associations with carcinogenesis in other cancer types, highlighting the potential oncogenic implications of extragonadal endometriosis in lymph nodes.

title: Pancreatic Solid Pseudopapillary Neoplasms—Clinicopathological Characteristics and Surgical Outcomes: A 10-Year Single-Centre Observational Study
summary: Solid pseudopapillary neoplasms (SPNs) are low-malignancy pancreatic tumors that predominantly affect young women in their third and fourth decades of life, with a strong female predominance (95.45%) and unclear pathogenesis possibly involving estrogen. These tumors most commonly locate in the pancreatic body and frequently undergo cystic degeneration, presenting clinically with abdominal pain, discomfort, and distention. Histopathologically, SPNs characteristically display perineural invasion (72.73%), pseudocapsule formation (59.09%), hemorrhage, vascular invasion, and variable expression of neuroendocrine and epithelial markers including beta-catenin, synaptophysin, CD10, and cytokeratin, while remaining negative for desmin and E-cadherin. Surgical resection is the primary curative treatment, with distal pancreatectomy for body/tail tumors and pancreaticoduodenectomy for head lesions, whereas chemotherapy and radiotherapy show minimal therapeutic benefit except in unresectable cases where radiotherapy may offer palliative value. Although SPNs possess malignant potential with capacity to metastasize to the liver and regional nodes, they generally carry a favorable prognosis with appropriate surgical management.

title: Immature Teratoma of the Ovary—A Narrative Review
summary: Immature teratomas are rare malignant ovarian germ cell tumors predominantly affecting adolescents and young women, particularly those ages 15-30 with retained childbearing desires. These tumors originate from primordial germ cells and characteristically contain immature embryonic tissue derived from two or all three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm), with immature neuroepithelium being key for diagnosis and grading. Surgery with a fertility-sparing approach remains the cornerstone of treatment and yields favorable pregnancy and live birth outcomes. Histological grade and stage are the primary prognostic factors, with grade 1 and stage I-II tumors carrying excellent prognosis (>95% survival), while higher grades and advanced stages show substantially reduced survival rates; notably, presence of yolk sac tumor components and age over 30 years further worsen outcomes. Alpha-fetoprotein and SALL4 serve as important diagnostic markers, while the SALL4/glypican-3 ratio helps differentiate immature teratomas from yolk sac tumor components. A rare but serious paraneoplastic complication is anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis caused by ectopic NMDA receptor expression on neuroectodermal elements, which presents with seizures, psychiatric symptoms, and autonomic dysfunction but resolves with tumor removal.

title: In Endometrial Cancers Originating from the Uterine Corpus, Does the Location of the Tumor Affect the Prognosis?
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer, a malignancy with globally increasing incidence, demonstrates significantly different prognostic outcomes based on primary tumor location within the uterine corpus. Tumors arising from the anterior and posterior walls exhibit substantially worse prognosis compared to fundal and lateral wall tumors, with anterior/posterior locations associated with dramatically higher rates of lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI, 43.5% versus 13.3%), recurrence (34.8% versus 0%), and mortality (34.8% versus 0%) over a 60-month follow-up period. This location-dependent prognostic difference is attributed to anatomical variations in myometrial thickness, lymphatic drainage patterns, and proximity to lymphovascular channels—anterior and posterior walls show greater myometrial thickness and more direct drainage to pelvic nodal basins, whereas fundal and lateral tumors have increased distance from cervical and parametrial lymphovascular structures. Regional variations in endometrial gland density and hormone receptor expression (estrogen and progesterone receptors) across different uterine regions may further contribute to differential tumor biology. These findings suggest that primary tumor location should be considered an important independent prognostic factor in endometrial cancer management, potentially warranting modified follow-up strategies and treatment intensification for anterior and posterior wall tumors.

title: Successful Complete Laparoscopic Resection of Benign Peritoneal Multicystic Mesothelioma Adherent to the Ascending Colon: A Case Report
summary: Benign peritoneal multicystic mesothelioma (BPMM) is a rare mesothelial tumor arising from the visceral peritoneum that predominantly affects women of reproductive age and localizes to the pelvis. Multiple risk factors contribute to BPMM development, including chronic inflammation, previous abdominal surgeries (particularly cervical cancer surgery), endometriosis, and recurrent peritonitis. Pathological diagnosis relies on immunohistochemical markers including positive staining for mesothelial proteins (cytokeratin 5/6, calretinin, Wilm's tumor-1, and D2-40) and hyaluronic acid detection, which help differentiate BPMM from cystic lymphangiomas that show distinct endothelial marker expression patterns. The standard treatment is complete surgical resection, with laparoscopic approaches offering feasible and safe complete tumor removal, potentially supplemented by hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy with cisplatin and doxorubicin. Despite its benign classification, BPMM presents significant clinical challenges due to its high local recurrence rate and potential for malignant transformation, warranting comprehensive surgical management and close follow-up.

title: Case Report: Uterine mesothelial cyst in a nulliparous woman—diagnostic and clinical challenges of a rare benign lesion
summary:
# Summary

Uterine mesothelial cysts (UMCs) are rare benign lesions whose development is promoted by chronic peritoneal inflammation, which drives mesothelial cell proliferation and migration. Endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and previous abdominal surgeries are established risk factors that induce hyperplastic and neoplastic changes in mesothelial cells, collectively enhancing their proliferation and migration. Intramural cystic lesions can distort the uterine cavity and compromise endometrial receptivity, potentially interfering with embryo implantation in nulliparous women. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities including ultrasound, CT, and MRI, with pathological analysis and immunohistochemical markers (calretinin, HBME-1, WT1, and cytokeratin 5/6) confirming mesothelial differentiation and excluding malignancy through negative estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and GATA3 staining. Laparoscopic cystectomy provides both diagnostic and therapeutic benefits with low morbidity, while conservative approaches such as ultrasound-guided aspiration offer only transient symptom relief due to rapid cyst reaccumulation. For patients not desiring fertility preservation, hysterectomy is recommended as the definitive treatment option.

title: Disseminated Peritoneal Leiomyomatosis of the Peritoneum Following Laparoscopic Supracervical Hysterectomy with Morcellation: A Case Report
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Mechanical morcellation during laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy carries significant risk of dispersing viable uterine cells throughout the abdominal cavity, leading to disseminated peritoneal leiomyomatosis (DPL), a rare but serious complication. The pathophysiology involves either direct dissemination of myometrial tissue fragments or metaplasia of peritoneal stem cells, with resulting lesions expressing estrogen and progesterone receptors and demonstrating immunohistochemical positivity for smooth muscle markers including desmin and smooth muscle actin. DPL manifests with multiple smooth muscle tumors distributed across peritoneal surfaces, commonly affecting the peritoneum, omentum, mesentery, and trocar sites, and frequently causes abdominal and pelvic pain that can be mistaken for malignancy. If malignancy is inadvertently morcellated, outcomes substantially worsen with doubled recurrence rates at one year and quadrupled mortality within the first year. While containment systems theoretically reduce tissue dissemination, the rarity of DPL limits evidence regarding their effectiveness. This case underscores the critical importance of histopathological examination in confirming the benign nature of DPL and careful consideration of morcellation risks during hysterectomy procedures.

title: Peritoneal Mesothelioma in a Patient with Long-Standing Crohn’s Disease: Cause or Coincidence?
summary: This case report presents a rare instance of peritoneal mesothelioma developing in a patient with long-standing Crohn's disease, suggesting that chronic serosal inflammation from non-asbestos sources may contribute to mesothelioma carcinogenesis. While asbestos exposure remains the most well-established risk factor for peritoneal mesothelioma, operating through induction of chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and DNA damage, the study highlights that other chronic inflammatory conditions—including Crohn's disease, endometriosis, and familial Mediterranean fever—can also increase mesothelioma risk through sustained peritoneal inflammation. In this patient, Crohn's disease-induced transmural intestinal inflammation extended to the serosal surface, where it appears to have promoted mesothelial cell proliferation and malignant transformation, with genetic factors such as BAP1 mutations potentially playing a secondary role. The epithelioid histological subtype identified in this case, characterized by calretinin and CK7 positivity with CK20 negativity, was associated with a more favorable prognosis. Treatment options for peritoneal mesothelioma include cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraoperative peritoneal chemotherapy perfusion and immunotherapy, either alone or in combination with conventional chemotherapy. This case underscores the importance of considering mesothelioma in the differential diagnosis of chronic inflammatory abdominal conditions and the potential oncogenic effects of long-standing serosal inflammation beyond occupational asbestos exposure.

title: Mucinous Carcinoma of Ovary in a 15-Year-Old Girl, A Rare Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Mucinous ovarian carcinoma is a rare malignancy characterized by distinct molecular alterations including KRAS mutations (64%), CDKN2A copy-number loss (76%), and TP53 mutations (64%), with TP53 particularly implicated in progression from borderline tumors to invasive carcinoma. ERBB2/HER2 amplifications occur in 18–26% of cases and frequently co-occur with TP53 mutations, distinguishing this tumor type from benign lesions. Immunohistochemically, mucinous carcinomas typically express CK7, CK20, CDX2, and CEA, while remaining negative for p16 and hormone receptors, with elevated Ki67 proliferation indices. Early-stage disease carries an excellent prognosis with approximately 90% five-year survival and is managed with surgery alone, whereas advanced-stage tumors are less chemosensitive and respond poorly to standard platinum-taxane regimens, though HER2-positive cases may benefit from trastuzumab-based therapy. The molecular and clinical heterogeneity of mucinous ovarian carcinoma necessitates tailored treatment approaches distinct from other epithelial ovarian cancer subtypes.

title: Primary retroperitoneal squamous cell carcinoma: A case report
summary:
# Endometriosis-Associated Retroperitoneal Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Primary retroperitoneal squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a rare malignancy associated with endometriosis, human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, and prior gynecological surgery, particularly hysterectomy. In HPV-positive cases, the viral oncoprotein E6 degrades p53, disrupting DNA repair and apoptosis, while E7 inhibits the retinoblastoma protein to promote G1/S phase cell cycle progression. Diagnostic evaluation involves elevated tumor markers including carcinoembryonic antigen and CA125, along with immunohistochemical positivity for Pan-Cytokeratin, CK5/6, p40, and Ki-67, with SCC antigen helping confirm diagnosis. Multimodal treatment combining surgical resection followed by chemotherapy (paclitaxel and carboplatin) and radiotherapy represents the standard approach, with concurrent chemoradiotherapy yielding improved clinical outcomes compared to sequential treatment. The underlying molecular pathology of endometriosis-associated SCC involves somatic mutations in PI3K, KRAS, and ARID1A genes in the endometriotic lesions themselves, establishing a clear malignant transformation pathway from benign endometriosis to invasive carcinoma.

title: Rare abdominal wall clear cell carcinoma in endometriosis: case report and literature review of diagnostic and therapeutic considerations
summary: Endometriosis is a well-established precursor lesion for malignant transformation, particularly into clear cell carcinoma, though such transformation in the abdominal wall is exceptionally rare. While clear cell carcinomas most commonly arise in the ovary where they are associated with endometriosis in up to 50% of cases, this report documents a highly aggressive clear cell carcinoma developing in abdominal wall endometriosis, likely seeded during prior caesarean section or other pelvic surgery. The tumor demonstrated aggressive biological behavior characterized by elevated Ki-67 proliferation index, weak estrogen receptor expression, absent progesterone receptor expression, and high propensity for recurrence and metastasis. Immunohistochemical profiling revealed Napsin positivity and wild-type p53, supporting the diagnosis of clear cell carcinoma. Multimodal treatment combining extensive surgical resection with abdominal wall reconstruction, platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin and paclitaxel), bevacizumab, and radiotherapy was employed to address the locally advanced malignancy. This case highlights the necessity for long-term surveillance of endometriosis patients and consideration of malignant transformation even in extragonadal sites, as well as the importance of surgical expertise in managing complex oncological reconstruction.

title: Metastatic endometrial stromal sarcoma with sex cord and neuroendocrine differentiation harboring a complex gene fusion
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a metastatic low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LGESS) with unusual sex cord and neuroendocrine differentiation, harboring a complex JAZF1::DLG5::PHF1 gene fusion identified by RNA-based next-generation sequencing. Endometrial stromal sarcomas are typically driven by recurrent gene fusions involving JAZF1, SUZ12, or PHF1; the JAZF1::SUZ12 fusion dysregulates the PRC2 complex and reduces H3K27me3 histone marks, while PHF1 fusions are known to induce sex cord-like differentiation. The tumor exhibited a rare triple fusion variant and displayed divergent histological features including sex cord-like areas positive for CD10 and SF-1, and neuroendocrine components expressing synaptophysin and chromogranin-A, alongside typical stromal elements expressing estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and smooth muscle actin. The case illustrates the propensity of LGESS to metastasize to distant sites such as the lungs after prolonged latency periods and highlights the importance of accurate molecular diagnosis. Management involved surgical resection combined with hormonal therapy, as LGESS tumors demonstrate responsiveness to hormone-based treatments.

title: Extraovarian fibrothecomas: Two case reports and comprehensive review of ovarian sex cord-stromal fibroma-thecoma tumors
summary: Extraovarian fibroma-thecoma group tumors are rare sex cord-stromal neoplasms that commonly present with clinical features mimicking malignancy, including ascites, elevated CA-125 levels, and pleural effusion (Meigs-like syndrome). These tumors typically arise in the pelvic cavity, broad ligament, omentum, or adjacent structures, and are frequently misdiagnosed as ovarian cancers due to overlapping clinical presentations. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals characteristic expression of vimentin, WT1, and progesterone receptor, with variable expression of estrogen receptor, CD56, inhibin, and calretinin. The diagnostic challenge is particularly acute because fibromas account for only 4% of ovarian tumors while thecomas constitute 0.5–1%, yet their extraovarian locations create confusion with primary malignancies. Recognition of the distinctive immunophenotype and characteristic association with benign Meigs-like syndrome rather than true malignancy is essential for appropriate clinical management and avoiding unnecessary aggressive treatment.

title: Evolution of an ovarian endometriotic cyst into clear cell carcinoma with squamous differentiation across two pregnancies: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) demonstrates a well-established malignant progression from benign endometriosis through atypical endometriosis to invasive carcinoma, as illustrated in this case of a patient whose endometriotic cyst transformed into OCCC with squamous differentiation during her second pregnancy. The malignant transformation involves hormonal dysregulation in which elevated estrogen promotes ovarian cancer development while progesterone exerts an inhibitory effect, though interestingly multiparity reduces ovarian cancer risk. Pathological diagnosis relies on characteristic morphological features including hobnail cells, clear cells, and tubular, papillary, cystic, and solid growth patterns, with diagnostic immunohistochemical markers including positive expression of NapsinA, P504S, hepatocyte nuclear factor 1 beta, CK7, EMA, WT1, and CD10, while markers such as SALL-4, α-inhibin, Vimentin, and thyroid transcription factor-1 are absent. The patient developed thromboembolic complications and significantly elevated tumor markers (CA-125 and CA724), prompting definitive surgical treatment including pregnancy termination, total hysterectomy, bilateral adnexectomy, and pelvic lymph node dissection. While pregnancy-associated ovarian cancers are exceptionally rare, they generally exhibit more favorable prognoses than non-pregnant cases, though OCCC carries a poorer prognosis compared to endometrioid carcinoma.

title: Clinical challenge of benign metastasizing leiomyoma with cardiac and hepatic involvement: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Benign metastasizing leiomyoma (BML) is a rare condition that develops in women with a history of uterine leiomyomas, typically following myomectomy or hysterectomy. This case report describes a 39-year-old female presenting with fatigue and exertional dyspnea, in whom imaging revealed a large mass involving the right atrium, inferior vena cava, and right hepatic vein.
