Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed the diagnosis through positive staining for actin, desmin, progesterone receptor, and Ki67, markers consistent with smooth muscle differentiation and benign proliferation. The patient's surgical history of myomectomy for multiple uterine fibroids, combined with the subsequent discovery of benign leiomyoma and endometriosis during hysterectomy, highlights the important clinical association between these gynecologic conditions and metastatic disease. This case demonstrates that BML should be considered in the differential diagnosis of atypical cardiac masses, particularly in patients with prior uterine fibroid interventions, as the condition can result in cardiopulmonary complications requiring clinical intervention.

title: Case Report: A patient with lynch syndrome with vaginal endometriosis-associated malignancy and synchronous colonic tubulovillous adenoma
summary: This case report presents a rare clinical scenario of a patient with Lynch syndrome presenting with synchronous vaginal endometriosis-associated malignancy and colonic tubulovillous adenoma. The patient harbored pathogenic MSH6 mutations (p.F1104Lfs*11 and c.3556+1G>A) that impair mismatch repair function, leading to microsatellite instability and increased cancer susceptibility across multiple organ systems. Immunohistochemistry confirmed loss of MSH6 protein expression in the vaginal endometrial adenocarcinoma, while MLH1, MSH2, and PMS2 remained intact. The patient was treated with surgical interventions including hysterectomy, bilateral adnexectomy, vaginal excision, and right hemicolectomy, followed by paclitaxel/carboplatin chemotherapy for the endometriosis-associated malignancy. This case underscores the critical importance of MMR protein testing in identifying Lynch syndrome in patients with endometriosis-associated cancers, as MSH6 mutation carriers face cumulative cancer risks of 12% for colorectal cancer and 16% for endometrial cancer by age 70. The synchronous presentation of extra-ovarian endometriosis-associated cancer with intestinal neoplasia highlights the need for comprehensive screening and surveillance in Lynch syndrome patients.

title: Diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers associated with histotype in advanced epithelial ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Advanced epithelial ovarian cancer comprises distinct histotypes with characteristic mutational profiles and clinical outcomes. High-grade serous carcinoma (70% of cases) is associated with TP53 and BRCA mutations and poor prognosis, while endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas (12% each) frequently arise from endometriosis and harbor PTEN, ARID1A, and PIK3CA mutations. Low-grade serous carcinoma shows KRAS/BRAF mutations with chemoresistance but superior 5-year survival (67%), whereas mucinous carcinoma displays KRAS mutations and the poorest outcomes (23% survival). Histotype-specific gene expression panels enable diagnostic classification, with genes like S100A1, HNF1A, and COL17A1 upregulated in high-grade serous carcinoma, and distinct markers for other subtypes including HNF1B and ARID1A for clear cell carcinoma. Prognostic biomarkers including OTOF, EEF1E1-BLOC1S5, and STAC3 associate with favorable survival, while SMOC1, GDPGP1, and EPRS1 predict poor outcomes. Therapeutic advances include poly ADP ribose polymerase inhibitors for BRCA-mutant tumors and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy for advanced disease.

title: Clinicopathological Characteristics and Oncologic Outcomes of Endometrioid Ovarian Carcinoma: A Retrospective Study from a Tertiary Cancer Centre
summary: Endometrioid ovarian carcinoma arises in the context of endometriosis and atypical endometriosis, particularly affecting younger patients who typically present with early-stage, low-grade disease and therefore experience favorable prognosis compared to other ovarian cancer subtypes. Molecularly, these tumors harbor mutations in CTNNB1, PIK3CA, ARID1A, and PPP2R1A, while MMR and POLE alterations are associated with improved outcomes and p53 alterations with worse prognosis. Advanced stage disease, residual tumor after surgery, high tumor grade, and elevated CA125 levels are independent predictors of both disease relapse and cancer-related mortality, with patients facing up to 13.86-fold increased risk of death in advanced stages. Up to one-third of patients have synchronous endometrial malignancy with high histologic and molecular concordance, necessitating careful pathological evaluation. Standard treatment includes surgical resection followed by adjuvant carboplatin and paclitaxel chemotherapy in approximately two-thirds of patients. Notably, the presence of endometriosis itself was associated with lower cancer-related mortality and earlier incidental detection, potentially improving outcomes through surveillance of these high-risk patients.

title: Epidemiological and Histopathological Characterization of Endometrial Carcinoma: A Retrospective Cohort from Romania
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study characterizes endometrial carcinoma by histopathological and epidemiological features, identifying key prognostic indicators and molecular associations. PTEN gene loss of function mutations represent an early molecular event in oncogenesis, particularly in endometrioid carcinomas, while the two main carcinoma types show distinct etiologies: Type I tumors arise from chronic estrogen exposure through predictable progression from hyperplasia, whereas Type II carcinomas develop in atrophied endometrium and represent more aggressive histological variants including serous and clear cell types. Lymphovascular invasion emerges as the strongest independent prognostic predictor, significantly correlating with tumor grade, myometrial invasion depth, lymph node involvement, and advanced FIGO stages, with substantial invasion (>5 vessels) strongly impacting overall and progression-free survival. Higher-grade tumors demonstrate increased propensity for lymphovascular invasion, deeper myometrial penetration, and lymph node metastasis, with grade 3 endometrioid tumors showing particularly poor prognosis despite diagnostic difficulty distinguishing them from non-endometrioid entities. The study emphasizes that classical histopathology and clinical staging remain essential for guiding treatment decisions and triaging patients for newer molecular classification approaches introduced in FIGO 2023 guidelines.

title: Serous Papillary Adenofibroma Cyst of the Ovary in a Young Woman: Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Serous papillary adenofibroma cysts (SPACs) are rare benign ovarian tumors arising from the ovarian surface epithelium or epithelial inclusion cysts, typically presenting in women of reproductive age with nonspecific symptoms such as pelvic discomfort and abdominal distension. These tumors are characterized by complex cystic morphology with papillary projections and solid nodules, often accompanied by elevated tumor markers (CA-125 and CA 19-9), which can complicate differential diagnosis. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI serve as primary diagnostic tools, with MRI particularly useful for identifying the fibrous stromal content through low T2 signal intensity. Intraoperative frozen section analysis is essential for confirming benignity and guiding surgical management, supported by a distinctive immunohistochemical profile including expression of WT1, ER, PR, and vimentin with absence of CD10. Risk factors for benign ovarian tumors include reproductive age, nulliparity, menorrhagia, and family history of ovarian or breast cancer, though long-term oral contraceptive use offers protective benefits. Larger cysts (>10-15 cm) carry risk for acute complications such as adnexal torsion or rupture, while hormonal influences from conditions like endometriosis may promote tumor enlargement.

title: Clinicopathological Diagnosis and Prognosis of Endometrioid Borderline Ovarian Tumors: Dual Case Reports and Literature Review
summary: Endometrioid borderline ovarian tumors (EBOTs) are rare neoplasms, representing approximately 0.2% of epithelial ovarian tumors, that frequently coexist with endometriosis and may progress along a malignant pathway toward endometrioid carcinoma. The molecular pathogenesis of EBOTs involves recurrent alterations in key genes, with CTNNB1 mutations occurring in 50% of cases and PTEN mutations in 20%, along with associated microsatellite instability; these same genetic alterations are also characteristic of uterine endometrioid carcinomas, suggesting shared molecular mechanisms. Histopathologically, EBOT cells express cytokeratins (CK, CK7), hormone receptors (estrogen and progesterone receptors), and mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), while notably lacking WT-1 expression, with a low Ki-67 proliferation index of 3-10%. Surgical resection, including adnexectomy, remains the primary effective treatment and achieves favorable prognosis, as demonstrated in two case reports where patients aged 30 and 34 years underwent successful left adnexal resection. The clinical presentation may include elevated serum CA125 levels and frequently coexists with endometrial pathology such as polyps and hyperplasia, highlighting the importance of comprehensive endometrial evaluation in EBOT diagnosis.

title: Context is key: how diagnostic and prognostic markers are reshaping gynecologic pathology
journal: The Journal of Pathology: Clinical Research
summary: Recent advances in molecular pathology are transforming the diagnosis and prognosis of endometriosis-associated malignant tumors and related gynecologic cancers through optimized biomarker panels. p53, ARID1A, and CCNE1 have emerged as critical markers for endometrial carcinomas, with p53 particularly useful for identifying serous histotypes and being nearly universal in high-grade serous carcinomas where TP53 mutations are essentially diagnostic prerequisites. Additional markers refine risk stratification: ATRX and DAXX loss associate with leiomyosarcoma through alterations in the ATRX-DAXX chaperone complex involved in telomere maintenance, while CDKN2A/p16 loss identifies unfavorable prognosis in low-grade serous carcinomas. Beyond p53 and CDKN2A, SWI/SNF complex alterations including ARID1B, SMARCA4, and SMARCB1 characterize undifferentiated endometrial carcinomas, though POLE mutations in SWI/SNF-intact cases indicate improved prognosis. Specialized markers further enhance diagnostic accuracy: GATA3 and TTF1 with absent ER/PR identify mesonephric adenocarcinomas, ERBB2 amplification associates with advanced gastric-type endocervical adenocarcinomas, and immune checkpoint markers (PD-L1, B7-H3, TIM-3) characterize HPV-independent adenocarcinomas. Integration of these molecular signatures with artificial intelligence augmentation improves diagnostic precision, particularly for challenging lesions like serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma.

title: In vitro fertilization–embryo transfer resulting in a live birth in a woman with benign multicystic peritoneal mesothelioma: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Benign multicystic peritoneal mesothelioma (BMPM) is a rare peritoneal condition affecting women of reproductive age that arises from chronic peritoneal irritation and inflammation triggered by peritoneal insults such as previous surgery, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and invasive procedures like oocyte retrieval. The pathogenesis involves proliferation and migration of mesothelial cells, with immunohistochemical markers including calretinin and cytokeratin positivity. Sex hormones, particularly estrogen, are hypothesized to regulate BMPM development and progression, with concerns that elevated estrogen during infertility treatment may exacerbate the condition. Although BMPM is benign, malignant transformation has been reported in some cases, and lesions characteristically demonstrate slow progressive growth with potential for recurrence after surgical resection. This case demonstrates successful in vitro fertilization and live birth in a woman with BMPM, highlighting the feasibility of assisted reproduction despite this rare peritoneal disorder, though careful monitoring is warranted given the potential for hormone-related progression.

title: Gastric Adenocarcinoma Resembling Submucosal Tumor: A Case Report
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary: This case report describes gastric cancer with features of a submucosal tumor (GCSMT), a rare gastric malignancy that presents diagnostic challenges due to its submucosal location and superficial mucosal coverage. A 60-year-old patient presenting with stomach discomfort was found to have a 2.0 cm poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma localized to the submucosa and muscular layer, which is the most common histological type identified in GCSMT cases. Endoscopic ultrasonography and endoscopic submucosal dissection were critical diagnostic tools, as standard gastroscopy typically misidentifies these tumors as benign lesions like gastrointestinal stromal tumors. Immunohistochemical analysis confirmed adenocarcinoma by positive expression of cytokeratins (CK, CK7, CK8/18), CDX-2, villin, and Ki-67, while excluding neuroendocrine and stromal origins through negative staining for synaptophysin, TTF-1, CD56, and CD34. Given the malignant nature of GCSMT, definitive treatment required subtotal gastrectomy with regional lymph node dissection rather than endoscopic approaches alone. This case highlights the importance of accurate diagnostic differentiation in submucosal gastric lesions to ensure appropriate surgical management and optimize patient outcomes.

title: Clinicopathological Characteristics of Atypical Polypoid Adenomyoma of the Uterus in Association With Endometrial Atypical Hyperplasia and Endometrioid Carcinoma
summary: Atypical polypoid adenomyoma (APA) is a uterine lesion predominantly affecting premenopausal women that frequently associates with atypical endometrial hyperplasia and endometrioid endometrial carcinoma, presenting clinically with abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia. Although historically considered benign, APA demonstrates malignant transformation potential, with conservative management showing recurrence and progression rates of 28.9% and 16.6% respectively. Molecularly, APA involves alterations in β-catenin/CTNNB1, PTEN, and KRAS, along with elevated phosphorylated mTOR expression in most cases and MLH1 promoter hypermethylation that may drive progression to carcinoma through mTOR pathway activation. Histologically, APA differs from endometrioid carcinoma by lacking myometrial invasion, tumor-type necrosis, and high mitotic activity, while carcinomas more frequently exhibit cribriform architecture and papillary intraluminal growth. Complete hysteroscopic resection with subsequent surveillance endometrial biopsy represents the preferred treatment for fertility preservation, significantly reducing recurrence rates, whereas hysterectomy remains a definitive option for patients with established carcinoma.

title: Outcomes and prognostic factors in patients with synchronous endometrial and ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study examined prognostic factors in patients with synchronous endometrial and ovarian cancer (SEOC), a rare malignancy typically presenting in premenopausal women with higher body mass index and nulliparity. Endometrioid carcinoma was the most common histological type, and patients typically presented with vaginal bleeding, pelvic mass, or abdominal pain. Advanced FIGO stage of ovarian cancer and platinum resistance emerged as independent predictors of worse progression-free and overall survival, respectively, while platinum-based chemotherapy achieved a 67.9% sensitivity rate when combined with surgical treatment. Key prognostic factors for overall survival included depth of myometrial invasion, tumor stage and grade of both cancers, lymph node metastasis, and platinum sensitivity. The study highlights that endometriosis is associated with SEOC development, and emerging genomic analyses including homologous recombination deficiency and ATM mutations may help stratify patients for personalized adjuvant therapy with chemotherapy, hormonal agents, or targeted approaches based on receptor status and molecular profiles.

title: Presacral Clear Cell Carcinoma of Müllerian Origin 20 Years After Hysterectomy and Bilateral Salpingo‐Oophorectomy: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents the malignant transformation of endometriosis into clear cell carcinoma of Müllerian origin in the retroperitoneal and presacral regions, occurring two decades after hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Endometriosis-associated clear cell carcinoma develops through genetic mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, and KRAS genes within endometriotic lesions, with risk factors including prolonged unopposed estrogen exposure and obesity persisting even after surgical removal of reproductive organs. The tumor displayed characteristic immunohistochemical markers including positivity for PAX8, Napsin A, CK7, and AMACR, with intact mismatch repair proteins and wild-type p53, while lacking hormone receptors in the surrounding stroma. The patient underwent surgical resection followed by adjuvant chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin, with maintenance bevacizumab treatment considered for long-term management. This case highlights that endometriosis-associated carcinomas can present as retroperitoneal masses with atypical locations, necessitating their inclusion in differential diagnoses of pelvic masses, and demonstrates the importance of considering combined estrogen-progestin therapy in post-hysterectomy patients with suspected residual endometriosis to prevent malignant transformation.

title: Ovarian mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma mimicking serous carcinoma: A case report integrating cytologic, frozen, histological, immunohistochemistry, and molecular analyses
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare ovarian mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) that mimicked high-grade serous carcinoma but was definitively characterized through integrated diagnostic analyses. The tumor exhibited distinctive morphologic features including papillary and retiform glandular architectures with nuclear grooves and psammoma bodies, while immunohistochemistry revealed a mesonephric profile positive for PAX8, GATA3, and TTF-1 but negative for estrogen and progesterone receptors and WT-1, clearly distinguishing it from serous carcinoma. Molecular analysis identified a characteristic KRAS p.G12V mutation with wild-type TP53 and absence of other common oncogenic mutations, confirming MLA biology and raising the possibility of MEK inhibitor therapy. Clinically significant was the presence of endometriotic foci in both the fallopian tube and adjacent ovarian cortex, suggesting endometriosis-associated malignant transformation. The low tumor mutational burden and microsatellite stability indicated limited benefit from immunotherapy, underscoring the importance of accurate MLA diagnosis for appropriate management, as this tumor demonstrates aggressive behavior despite "low-grade" morphology and differs fundamentally from serous carcinoma in both biology and treatment options.

title: Two Cases of Mesonephric-Like Adenocarcinoma Arising From Ovary and Peritoneal and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case series documents two cases of mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA), a rare malignancy, arising from endometriosis in the ovary and peritoneum, with one case representing the first documented primary peritoneal MLA. The evidence strongly suggests that endometriosis, particularly in the context of prior intra-abdominal surgical trauma, may undergo malignant transformation to MLA, which originates from Müllerian duct remnants or endometriotic lesions. Immunohistochemically, MLA characteristically expresses PAX8, GATA3, TTF-1, calretinin, and CD10, while lacking estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor expression, aiding diagnostic differentiation from other ovarian malignancies. Complete cytoreductive surgery is critical for prognosis, followed by adjuvant chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin, which demonstrated efficacy in the reported cases. The authors suggest that clinical awareness of endometriosis history, particularly after surgical intervention, may facilitate early detection and improved outcomes in this rare endometriosis-associated malignancy. Future therapeutic approaches under investigation include immune checkpoint inhibitors, PARP inhibitors, and antibody-drug conjugates as potential additions to standard chemotherapy regimens.

title: Misleading Lesions in Gynecological Malignancies: A Case Report of Desmoid Tumor During Pregnancy and a Narrative Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

Desmoid tumors are benign fibromatous neoplasms driven primarily by somatic CTNNB1 mutations that activate the Wnt/β-catenin pathway, leading to nuclear β-catenin accumulation and altered transcription. These tumors predominantly affect women of reproductive age and show striking associations with pregnancy, prior abdominal surgery (including cesarean section and laparotomy), and estrogenic stimulation, with pregnancy or the postpartum period present at diagnosis in 11–32% of cases. The pathogenesis involves aberrant wound healing with sustained myofibroblast activation at surgical sites, while hormonal and immunological changes during pregnancy further promote tumor growth. Contemporary management has shifted from routine surgical excision toward a watch-and-wait strategy for stable lesions, with function-preserving surgery, anti-estrogen therapy (tamoxifen), and tyrosine kinase inhibitors reserved for progressive or symptomatic tumors, recognizing that 20–30% of abdominal wall desmoids undergo spontaneous regression. Notably, abdominal wall desmoid tumors can radiologically mimic endometriosis, underscoring diagnostic challenges in reproductive-aged women with prior gynecological surgery.

title: TCGA molecular subtypes in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review examines TCGA molecular subtypes in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers (EAOC), comparing endometrioid (ENOC) and clear cell (CCOC) subtypes. Endometriosis serves as a precursor to both ENOC and CCOC, and these tumors share common mutations including PTEN, KRAS, ARID1A, and CTNNB1, distinguishing them from high-grade serous cancers which lack BRCA mutations and homologous recombination deficiency. ENOC shows higher frequencies of favorable POLE-mutated and mismatch repair-deficient subtypes, with POLE mutations conferring excellent prognosis (100% disease-free and progression-free survival), while the p53-abnormal subtype predicts poor outcomes in both ENOC and CCOC. Progesterone receptor expression stratifies outcomes in ENOC, with high expression correlating with CTNNB1 mutations and favorable prognosis, whereas low expression associates with p53 mutations and poor outcomes. The molecular heterogeneity of EAOC supports tailored treatment strategies, including potential immunotherapy benefit for MMRd and ARID1A-mutated tumors, and adjuvant chemotherapy for p53-abnormal cases.

title: Colorectal clear cell carcinoma: a literature review
summary: Colorectal clear cell carcinoma (CCC) is a rare histological subtype of colon adenocarcinoma with two distinct origins: a Müllerian type arising from endometriosis and an intestinal type originating from normal mucosa. The tumors typically present with abdominal pain and gastrointestinal bleeding, most commonly occurring in the descending colon and rectum. Immunohistochemical profiling is essential for diagnosis and differential diagnosis, with CCC characteristically showing positivity for cytokeratin 20, carcinoembryonic antigen, epithelial membrane antigen, and caudal-type homeobox 2, while being negative for cytokeratin 7, alpha-fetoprotein, and HMB-45. This marker profile helps distinguish CCC from metastatic tumors and malignant melanoma. Surgery remains the preferred treatment modality, though molecular alterations including KRAS mutations, BRAF mutations, and microsatellite instability-high have been identified in some cases, suggesting heterogeneous molecular pathways underlying this rare malignancy.

title: Association between endometriosis and prognosis of ovarian clear cell carcinoma: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: Endometriosis increases ovarian carcinoma risk nearly two-fold, with a distinct association to ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) characterized by specific molecular alterations including ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations, elevated hepatocyte nuclear factor-1b, histone deacetylase 6, vascular endothelial growth factor, and tissue factor expression. Notably, endometriosis-associated OCCC presents with a significantly better prognosis than non-associated OCCC, showing improved disease-free survival and occurring at younger ages with fewer unfavorable prognostic markers such as ascites, CA125 elevation, and lympho-vascular invasion. The superior outcomes in endometriosis-associated cases are attributed to earlier diagnosis driven by associated symptoms like pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, combined with molecular features including lower TP53 mutation rates that may enhance chemotherapy responsiveness. Key molecular events involving mutations in ARID1A, PIK3CA, and PTEN drive the transformation from endometriosis to OCCC, while oxidative stress, immune cell dysfunction, and inflammation constitute critical microenvironmental factors in tumorigenesis. These findings suggest that endometriosis-associated OCCC represents a physiologically distinct tumor subtype warranting potentially less aggressive treatment approaches to prevent overtreatment while maintaining efficacy of conventional surgery and platinum-based chemotherapy.

title: Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma presenting with malignant peritonitis and suspected to originate from the fallopian tube: A case report
summary: This case report documents a rare mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) originating from the fallopian tube that presented with malignant peritonitis and widespread peritoneal dissemination. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed a distinctive marker profile with positive expression of GATA3, TTF1, CD10, and PAX-8, while estrogen receptor, Wilms' tumor 1, and wild-type p53 were absent, which is characteristic for confirming MLA diagnosis. Genetic testing identified a KRAS G12V mutation, a notable finding that influences treatment selection for this aggressive malignancy. The patient underwent comprehensive surgical debulking including hysterectomy, radical adnexal resection, rectal resection, and omentectomy, followed by adjuvant chemotherapy with paclitaxel, carboplatin, and bevacizumab, with subsequent salvage regimens using doxorubicin, carboplatin, and irinotecan. MLAs in the uterine body and ovaries frequently coexist with endometriosis and endometrioid carcinoma, and these tumors commonly metastasize to the lungs, omentum, and liver. This case illustrates the aggressive nature of fallopian tube-derived MLA and highlights the importance of multimodal treatment and recognition of KRAS mutations for optimizing therapeutic strategies in this rare gynecological malignancy.

title: Mesonephric-like metaplasia of the endometrium in a woman treated with letrozole: morphological, immunohistochemical and molecular analysis
summary: This case report documents the first endometrial mesonephric-like metaplasia (MLA) arising from endometrial epithelium through Müllerian-to-mesonephric transdifferentiation in a patient treated with letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor that blocks estrogen synthesis. The benign mesonephric-like metaplasia showed immunopositivity for PAX8, estrogen receptor, and GATA3, with patchy p16 expression and wild-type p53 pattern, but notably lacked TTF1, CD10, and KRAS mutations characteristic of malignant mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma. While malignant MLA typically harbors KRAS mutations in over 90% of cases and is frequently associated with concurrent Müllerian lesions ranging from benign to malignant, the benign mesonephric-like proliferation described here did not show oncogenic molecular alterations. The authors suggest that prolonged aromatase inhibition may represent a novel risk factor for mesonephric-like metaplasia development, though the potential for malignant progression to MLA carcinogenesis requires further investigation. This case expands the known spectrum of endometrial metaplastic lesions and their possible etiologies in the context of hormone-deprivation therapy.

title: A giant symptomatic primary ovarian leiomyoma with prominent necrosis and degenerative changes in a 75-year-old female: a case report
summary: Primary ovarian leiomyoma (OL) is an exceptionally rare benign smooth muscle tumor of the ovary, with fewer than 100 reported cases in the literature, though it can occasionally present as a large symptomatic mass as in this 75-year-old patient. Estrogen is the primary hormonal driver of leiomyoma growth, with tamoxifen exposure further promoting both uterine and extrauterine leiomyomas, while multiparity and progestin-only contraception reduce leiomyoma risk; notably, OLs occur simultaneously with uterine leiomyomas in 78% of cases, suggesting shared hormonal etiology. Larger ovarian leiomyomas present with multiple clinical manifestations including abdominal pain, palpable masses, hydronephrosis, ascites, and elevated CA-125 levels that may mimic malignancy. Immunohistochemically, primary OLs demonstrate intense α-smooth muscle actin and widespread desmin positivity, which distinguishes them from fibromas (desmin-negative) and thecomas (α-SMA-negative), though leiomyosarcoma must be excluded by the absence of tumor necrosis, cytological atypia, and high mitotic activity. Surgical management remains the treatment of choice for symptomatic ovarian masses, with preoperative imaging by ultrasound, CT, or MRI essential for evaluation and surgical planning.

title: Primary Mesonephric‐Like Adenocarcinoma of the Ovary: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare primary mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) of the ovary in a 67-year-old patient that coexisted with endometriotic cysts, representing a notable association between this malignancy and endometriosis. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed characteristic markers including positive expression of PAX8, GATA3, and CD10, while estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and TTF-1 were absent, along with wild-type P53 expression. Molecularly, the tumor harbored KRAS mutations but lacked PTEN mutations, consistent with ovarian MLA's known genetic profile. The patient presented with gross hematuria caused by ureteral stenosis from the tumor mass and was treated with surgical resection followed by adjuvant chemotherapy with liposomal paclitaxel and carboplatin, consolidation therapy with apatinib (a VEGFR-2 inhibitor), achieving prolonged remission of 25 months. The case highlights that ovarian MLA frequently presents at advanced stages with lymph node metastasis, commonly manifests with pelvic pain and abnormal bleeding, and demonstrates sensitivity to platinum-based and anti-angiogenic therapeutic approaches, warranting consideration in the differential diagnosis of ovarian malignancies associated with endometriosis.

title: Characteristics of endometrial cancer progressed to extrauterine lesions following fertility preserving medroxyprogesterone acetate therapy for young endometrial cancer patients
summary:
# Summary

Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) is an effective fertility-preserving treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer and atypical endometrial hyperplasia, achieving complete remission rates of 90-95% in grade 1 endometrioid carcinoma. However, this study documents a critical complication: extrauterine lesions (ELs) developed in seven young patients during or after MPA therapy, with lymph node metastasis being the most common site of spread, followed by peritoneal dissemination and ovarian metastasis. Progressive disease was strongly associated with myometrial invasion, elevated tumor markers (particularly CA125), and prolonged time to complete remission exceeding six months. The findings underscore the importance of regular surveillance with imaging (CT/MRI), tumor marker monitoring, endometrial biopsy, and transvaginal ultrasonography during and after MPA treatment to detect extrauterine progression. Mismatch repair deficiency and mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma histology emerged as poor prognostic markers. These observations indicate that while MPA offers fertility preservation benefits for selected young patients, close clinical vigilance is essential to identify early signs of disease progression requiring definitive surgical and chemotherapeutic intervention.

title: Oncogenic pathway landscape of ovarian cancer and correlation with clinical prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes the oncogenic pathway landscape across ovarian cancer subtypes and correlates molecular alterations with clinical prognosis. TP53 mutations dominate high-grade serous carcinomas (92.8%), while MAP kinase signaling through KRAS, BRAF, and NRAS mutations represents the primary pathway in low-grade serous and mucinous carcinomas. Endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas, which frequently arise from endometriosis, show diverse oncogenic drivers including PI3K-AKT-mTOR and SWI/SNF family pathway alterations, with ARID1A mutations occurring in 28-59% of these cases. Prognostically, DNA damage response pathway activation associates with improved progression-free survival in high-grade serous carcinoma, while RTK signaling correlates with better overall survival. The findings support targeted therapeutic approaches: MEK/ERK inhibitors and RAF/MEK inhibitors for MAP kinase-driven tumors, PARP inhibitors for DNA damage response-deficient cancers, and PI3K/mTOR inhibitors for endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas with ARID1A inactivation.

title: Case Report: Diagnostic challenges in cervical small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of a reproductive-age woman
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a reproductive-age woman with cervical small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (SCNEC) complicated by a history of severe endometriosis and uterine leiomyomas, highlighting the diagnostic challenges associated with this rare malignancy. HPV-18 infection was identified as a significant risk factor, and immunohistochemical analysis revealed characteristic neuroendocrine markers including chromogranin A, synaptophysin, CD56, and the emerging marker INSM1, while hormonal receptors (estrogen and progesterone) were absent. The patient demonstrated elevated tumor markers (CA125, HE4, and CEA) and extensive metastatic disease involving bone, spleen, and lymph nodes at presentation, illustrating SCNEC's aggressive clinical behavior. Treatment included conventional chemotherapy (carboplatin plus nab-paclitaxel, followed by cisplatin plus etoposide) and immunotherapy with cadonilimab, a bispecific antibody targeting PD-1 and CTLA-4 checkpoints, reflecting emerging precision oncology approaches targeting recurrent mutations in RTK/RAS, PI3K–AKT–mTOR, p53, and MYC pathways. The case underscores the diagnostic difficulty of SCNEC when initial cervical biopsies appear benign and the importance of comprehensive imaging and molecular profiling for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

title: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor of the peritoneum in a young woman: A case report with molecular insights
summary: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT) is a peritoneal neoplasm of low malignant potential that arises predominantly in women of reproductive age and is frequently associated with endometriosis and adenomyosis. WDPMT pathogenesis involves recurrent somatic mutations in genes such as TRAF7, CDC42, E2F1, EHD1, and ATM, with E2F1 mutations disrupting the DNA-binding domain and increasing protein stability. Molecularly, WDPMT is distinctly characterized by retained BAP1 and MTAP expression, elevated PAX8, and enrichment of C>A transversions with COSMIC signature 24, distinguishing it from malignant mesothelioma which typically harbors inactivating mutations in BAP1, CDKN2A/B, NF2, and SETD2. Diagnosis relies on integrated immunohistochemistry (demonstrating retained BAP1, MTAP, CK17, and PAX8 expression with absent L1CAM) combined with next-generation sequencing. Management is primarily surgical through cytoreductive surgery, with active surveillance representing a safe option for appropriately selected patients, supporting excellent long-term survival outcomes with a median disease-free survival of 144 months. Chronic inflammation and tissue repair processes associated with endometriosis likely create a permissive microenvironment for WDPMT development in this patient population.

title: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma arising from extrauterine deep infiltrating endometriosis: a rare, but important differential diagnosis and short review of the literature
summary: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LG-ESS) is a rare malignant mesenchymal tumor that can arise from deep infiltrating endometriosis, occurring in approximately 50% of extrauterine cases, though malignant transformation of endometriosis overall occurs in only 0.7–1% of cases. Histopathologically, LG-ESS cells resemble proliferative-phase endometrial stroma but exhibit invasive growth and lymphovascular invasion, with characteristic immunohistochemical positivity for CD10, WT1, estrogen and progesterone receptors. The high expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors supports adjuvant endocrine therapy with aromatase inhibitors following maximal cytoreductive surgery with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, which provides optimal oncologic control. Prognosis is primarily determined by disease stage and patient age, with stage I–II tumors achieving five-year survival rates exceeding 90%, compared to approximately 50% for stage III–IV disease. Adjuvant endocrine treatment significantly reduces recurrence risk in extrauterine LG-ESS, while radiotherapy and chemotherapy provide no clear survival benefit. Long-term surveillance is essential given the potential for late recurrences in this malignancy.

title: Anti-GBM Disease Following Ovarian Mesonephric-Like Adenocarcinoma: A Unique Insight Into Paraneoplastic Autoimmunity
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents the first known instance of anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease arising as a paraneoplastic manifestation following ovarian mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA), a rare and aggressive tumor expressing PAX8, GATA3, TTF1, and hormone receptors. The patient developed anti-GBM antibodies targeting the NC1 domain of type IV collagen approximately four months after completing carboplatin-paclitaxel chemotherapy, presenting with progressive hematuria, acute kidney injury, and anemia requiring emergent dialysis. The mechanism underlying this paraneoplastic autoimmunity likely involves paclitaxel-mediated impairment of regulatory T-cell function combined with immune dysregulation caused by MLA itself, which may expose or modify glomerular basement membrane antigens and trigger autoantibody production. Treatment with plasma exchange, methylprednisolone, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab was required to manage the anti-GBM disease, highlighting the severe autoimmune consequences of this malignancy. This case raises important questions about MLA-associated immune mechanisms and underscores the need for continued investigation into how chemotherapy and malignancy-driven immune dysregulation interact to generate paraneoplastic autoimmunity.

title: Genomics of ovarian cancers and the potential of precision medicine
summary: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) exhibits a distinctive genomic landscape characterized by near-universal TP53 mutations (90–98% of cases) and frequent homologous recombination repair (HRR) gene deficiencies, including germline and somatic pathogenic variants in BRCA1/BRCA2 (18–30% combined) and other HRR genes such as RAD51C, RAD51D, PALB2, and BRIP1. Additional commonly inactivated genes include NF1, RB1, and PTEN, while gene amplifications predominantly target MECOM (~27%), MYC (9–34%), CCNE1 (7–23%), and PIK3CA (3–22%). In contrast, other endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers display distinct genetic signatures: clear cell carcinomas feature high rates of ARID1A mutations (40–67%) and PIK3CA alterations (35–55%), endometrioid carcinomas are characterized by CTNNB1, PIK3CA, and KRAS mutations with lower TP53 involvement, while mucinous and low-grade serous carcinomas predominantly harbor MAPK pathway alterations. These genomic differences have significant therapeutic implications, with PARP inhibitors such as olaparib and niraparib demonstrating clinical efficacy as maintenance therapies specifically in HRD-positive HGSOC patients, enabling precision medicine approaches tailored to individual tumor genomic profiles.

title: Misdiagnosis and fatal outcome of advanced cervical adenosquamous carcinoma in pregnancy: A case report
summary: This case report documents the fatal outcome of cervical adenosquamous carcinoma diagnosed in pregnancy, highlighting the substantial diagnostic challenges in this setting. Pregnancy-induced physiological changes, including elevated estrogen and progesterone leading to glandular hyperplasia and basal-cell proliferation, create atypical clinical presentations that are easily confused with common pregnancy-related conditions such as threatened miscarriage, thereby delaying diagnosis of advanced-stage disease. The Arias-Stella reaction further complicates diagnosis by mimicking adenocarcinoma in situ and clear-cell carcinoma on histological examination. HPV16-associated adenosquamous carcinoma was identified through immunohistochemical markers including p16 positivity, high Ki-67 proliferation indices (>80%), and expression of cytokeratins and HNF-1β, while remaining negative for estrogen and progesterone receptors. Advanced-stage cervical cancer in pregnancy carries poor prognosis due to altered immunological function, hyperdynamic circulation, and increased uterine blood supply that accelerate tumor proliferation and metastasis. This case underscores that while neoadjuvant chemotherapy represents the only pregnancy-preserving treatment option, radiation therapy and delayed diagnosis substantially increase risk of spontaneous abortion, maternal death, and congenital complications.

title: Bartholin Gland Carcinoma: A State-of-the-Art Review of Epidemiology, Histopathology, Molecular Testing, and Clinical Management
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review of Bartholin gland carcinoma (BGC) delineates distinct molecular and clinical profiles across major histologic subtypes. Human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly HPV16, is strongly associated with squamous cell carcinoma and confers a favorable prognosis with lower FIGO stage, earlier diagnosis in younger patients, and reduced nodal metastasis; p16 immunohistochemistry serves as a valuable diagnostic marker for HPV-associated disease. In contrast, adenocarcinomas are typically HPV-negative, more aggressive, characterized by higher rates of regional nodal metastasis and worse survival outcomes, and frequently harbor KRAS mutations with immunoprofiles distinct from breast malignancies (ER/PR-negative, positive for CK7/CK20/CDX2). Adenoid cystic carcinoma is distinguished by MYB/MYBL1 fusions and characteristic perineural invasion. Contemporary management integrates surgical resection with adjuvant radiation and chemotherapy, while emerging evidence supports immunotherapy for squamous variants and targeted inhibitors (mTOR/CDK4/6) for adenocarcinoma. Advanced imaging modalities including ultrasound, CT, MRI, and PET-CT, combined with molecular testing, have substantially improved diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic stratification.

title: Comparative effectiveness of paclitaxel versus cyclophosphamide in platinum-based adjuvant treatment of high-risk early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer: an Asian population study
summary: This comparative study evaluated the effectiveness of paclitaxel versus cyclophosphamide as components of platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy for high-risk early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer in an Asian population. Both agents successfully treated the disease, with cyclophosphamide demonstrating comparable disease-free survival outcomes to the more widely-used paclitaxel, addressing a gap in the literature where direct comparisons in early-stage settings were previously limited. Clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas represented the predominant histological subtypes in the cohort. Multivariate analysis revealed that FIGO stage was the only independent predictor of disease-free survival, while the choice of chemotherapy regimen was not significantly associated with survival outcomes. These findings suggest that cyclophosphamide may offer a viable alternative to paclitaxel in platinum-based adjuvant regimens for this patient population, potentially providing clinicians with additional treatment options for early-stage ovarian cancer management.

title: Analysis of the Clinicopathological Characteristics of Different Molecular Subtypes in Endometrial Cancer: A Retrospective Single Center Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study analyzes molecular subtypes of endometrial cancer and their clinicopathological characteristics using WHO classification criteria. Endometrial cancer arises from epithelial cells and develops through an estrogen-dependent pathway wherein prolonged estrogen stimulation leads to endometrial proliferation; when unopposed by progesterone, excessive estrogen triggers atypical hyperplasia that progresses to cancer. The study identifies four molecular subtypes—POLE mutations, deficient mismatch repair (dMMR), p53 abnormalities, and NSMP (no specific molecular profile)—which show distinct associations with histological types and tumor differentiation. p53 abnormal subtype predominates in aggressive serous and clear cell carcinomas (84.8% and 54.5% respectively) and correlates with poor differentiation, while POLE mutations and dMMR are more prevalent in endometrioid carcinoma. Molecular typing facilitates individualized treatment management and improves patient prognosis by providing a reference for selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies based on the specific molecular profile of each cancer.

title: Mesonephric-like Adenocarcinoma (MLA) Diagnostic Criteria and Controversies: Perspectives and Guidance From Pathologists in the MLA Consortium
summary: Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma (MLA) is a rare gynecologic malignancy arising in the endometrium, ovaries, and extrauterine sites that is strongly associated with endometriosis in approximately 60% of extrauterine cases. MLA exhibits a distinctive immunohistochemical profile characterized by consistent expression of PAX8, PAX2, and luminal CD10, along with frequent TTF1 and GATA3 positivity, while lacking estrogen and progesterone receptor expression in most cases. The tumor shows morphologic, immunohistochemical, and molecular homology with cervical mesonephric adenocarcinoma and frequently presents as mixed lesions with endometrioid carcinoma in endometrial cases or with various other tumor types in ovarian and peritoneal sites. In endometrial tumors, MLA is typically admixed with atypical endometrial hyperplasia or endometrioid adenocarcinoma, whereas extrauterine MLAs demonstrate greater morphologic heterogeneity with associated low-grade and high-grade serous components, clear cell carcinoma, and neuroendocrine features. MLAs maintain wild-type p53, intact mismatch repair proteins, and preserved PTEN expression, with KRAS mutations documented in some cases. Given the diagnostic challenges posed by morphologic overlap with conventional endometrial carcinomas and other tumor types, this consortium article supports including MLA on the list of endometriosis-associated cancers and emphasizes the importance of comprehensive immunohistochemical evaluation for accurate diagnosis.

title: Co-occurrence of peritoneal mesothelioma and genitourinary cancers: a case series with comparative outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This case series describes an uncommon clinical phenomenon: the co-occurrence of peritoneal mesothelioma (PM) with genitourinary and gynecologic malignancies, highlighting a shared embryologic origin from mesoderm and molecular pathways. Asbestos exposure and germline BAP1 mutations were identified as risk factors in affected patients, with BAP1 being particularly notable for its association with hereditary mesothelioma and renal cell carcinoma. Patients with concurrent PM and genitourinary malignancies demonstrated worse prognostic markers, including higher peritoneal cancer indices, elevated inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein-to-albumin, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte, and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratios), and reduced three-year survival compared to PM alone. The well-differentiated papillary histologic subtype was enriched in this patient subset. Cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy, supplemented by neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy, showed improved survival outcomes, while estrogen receptor signaling appeared to confer survival advantages in women. Chronic inflammatory states mediated by IL-6 signaling may represent a unifying pathobiologic mechanism linking these malignancies.

title: Case Report: A rare case of bilateral primary Müllerian adenosarcoma of the ovary with Uncommon extensive metastasis and GNAQ mutation
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare bilateral primary ovarian adenosarcoma with extensive metastasis and lymphovascular invasion in a patient under 53 years old, features associated with poor progression-free and overall survival. Histopathologically, the tumor exhibited the characteristic biphasic architecture of adenosarcoma—benign glandular epithelium with periglandular stromal condensation paired with a low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma component containing spindle-shaped stromal cells showing mild atypia. Notably, next-generation sequencing identified a novel GNAQ T96S missense mutation and positive PD-L1 expression (CPS 5.0), neither previously reported in ovarian adenosarcoma, which may inform future therapeutic strategies targeting PKC signaling or immune checkpoint pathways. The patient was treated with a multimodal approach including letrozole, radiotherapy, and sintilimab immunotherapy, though the long-term efficacy of this regimen remains to be determined. These findings highlight that lymphovascular invasion and sarcomatous overgrowth emerge as critical independent predictors of disease progression in ovarian adenosarcoma, while genomic alterations in PI3K/AKT/PTEN and MDM2/CDK4 pathways continue to drive tumorigenesis in this rare malignancy.

title: Spectrum of Histopathological Changes in Abnormal Uterine Bleeding with Estrogen and Progesterone Receptor Expression in Endometrial Hyperplasia and Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study examines estrogen and progesterone receptor (ER/PR) expression patterns across the spectrum of endometrial pathology, from abnormal uterine bleeding through hyperplasia to adenocarcinoma. Both ER and PR are highly expressed in endometrial hyperplasia and play critical roles in cellular proliferation and differentiation, with prolonged estrogen exposure identified as a key risk factor for hyperplasia and carcinoma development. In contrast, endometrial adenocarcinoma demonstrates significantly reduced ER and PR expression, with higher-grade and undifferentiated tumors typically being ER-negative, while loss of either receptor correlates with poor prognosis and decreased survival. ER and PR expression status serves as a predictive biomarker for hormonal therapy response, with PR-rich lesions showing better response to progestin treatment and higher PR positivity correlating with improved differentiation and survival rates. These findings underscore the clinical importance of ER/PR immunohistochemical analysis for risk stratification, prognostication, and therapeutic selection in endometrial disease.

title: A Case Report of Endometrioid Carcinoma of the Ovary: A Comprehensive Clinical, Pathological, and Molecular Perspective
summary: Ovarian endometrioid carcinoma is a histologically distinct malignancy that frequently arises from endometriotic cysts and shares strong associations with endometriosis, affecting younger women with infertility histories. The pathogenesis involves multiple factors including unopposed estrogen exposure and chronic inflammation, which drive dysregulation of key signaling pathways through recurrent mutations in PTEN, ARID1A, PIK3CA, CTNNB1, and mismatch repair genes that activate PI3K/AKT/mTOR and Wnt/β-catenin pathways promoting cellular proliferation. Loss of mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) generates microsatellite instability and may indicate Lynch syndrome, necessitating comprehensive molecular screening and surveillance. Immunohistochemical profiling using CK7, estrogen/progesterone receptors, and β-catenin localization helps distinguish endometrioid carcinoma from morphologic mimickers and predicts therapeutic responsiveness. Advanced-stage or high-grade tumors may benefit from chemotherapy, targeted therapies including mTOR inhibitors, hormone-based treatments, and immune checkpoint inhibitors, particularly in cases with mismatch repair deficiency. This molecular stratification approach enables personalized treatment selection while improving prognostic accuracy for this endometriosis-associated malignancy.

title: Spermatic Cord Walthard Cyst as Inguinal Mass: CT/MRI and Immunohistochemical Correlation
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of a Walthard cell nest cyst arising in the spermatic cord of the inguinal canal, a location where such lesions are exceptionally uncommon despite their frequent occurrence in gynecologic tissues including the fallopian tube, mesosalpinx, and ovarian hilum. The diagnosis was established through a characteristic immunohistochemical profile showing positivity for CK5/6, CK7, GATA3, p63, and WT1, while remaining negative for CK20, uroplakin III, PAX8, calretinin, and OCT3/4, which effectively distinguished this benign lesion from its mimics such as metastatic urothelial carcinoma and mesothelial proliferations. The low Ki-67 index (<1%) confirmed the benign nature of the lesion, and imaging with diffusion-weighted MRI excluded other differential diagnoses including abscess and epidermoid cyst. Although Walthard cell nests can occasionally occur in extragynecologic sites such as the intestines, appendix, mesentery, and inguinal canal, this spermatic cord presentation exemplifies the diagnostic challenge posed by these lesions when encountered in unexpected anatomic locations. The case was managed by hernia repair using mesh-plug technique, highlighting the clinical importance of accurate pathologic diagnosis to guide appropriate surgical management.

title: Molecular Pathology of Ovarian Endometrioid Carcinoma: A Review
summary: Ovarian endometrioid carcinoma (OEC) is strongly associated with antecedent endometriosis, which confers a two- to threefold increased risk and is identified in up to 42% of cases. The malignant transformation appears to arise through accumulation of shared somatic mutations in endometriotic lesions, particularly in ARID1A and PIK3CA genes, along with mutations in KRAS, PTEN, and TP53. Clinical risk factors include obesity and hormone replacement therapy, while protective factors include oral contraceptive use, higher parity, and surprisingly smoking. OECs characteristically express estrogen and progesterone receptors, CK7, PAX8, and CA125, with distinctive molecular features including nuclear β-catenin and PTEN loss reflecting underlying CTNNB1 mutations, plus mismatch repair deficiency or microsatellite instability in approximately 12% of cases. Recent molecular taxonomy approaches identify OEC as enriched for Lynch syndrome-associated tumors, supporting routine mismatch repair testing and opening therapeutic opportunities with immune checkpoint inhibitors and homologous recombination deficiency-directed strategies.

title: Retroperitoneal Soft Tissue Xanthogranulomatous Pseudocyst: A Report of a Rare Case and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare retroperitoneal xanthogranulomatous pseudocyst, a benign lesion characterized by accumulation of lipid-laden macrophages (foam cells) resulting from chronic infection and inflammatory processes. Xanthogranulomatous inflammation involves histiocyte accumulation accompanied by neovascularization and hemorrhage, with underlying pathogenic mechanisms including abnormal lipid transport, lymphatic obstruction, and immune dysfunction. The patient presented with abdominal pain, nausea, anorexia, and weight loss secondary to compression of surrounding structures, particularly duodenal compression and partial gastric outlet obstruction. Histopathologic examination revealed CD68-positive foamy histiocytes and absence of CD1a, S100, and endothelial markers, confirming a non-Langerhans cell xanthogranulomatous lesion distinct from other histiocytic disorders such as Erdheim-Chester disease and Rosai-Dorfman disease. Surgical excision was performed as the definitive treatment to prevent local spread, and some cases may also respond to steroid therapy. This case highlights the importance of recognizing xanthogranulomatous pseudocysts as rare differential diagnoses in retroperitoneal masses, as they can cause significant morbidity through mass effect on adjacent organs.

title: Somatically derived ovarian yolk sac tumor in a postmenopausal woman: A case report of durable remission with personalized chemotherapy
summary:
# Summary

Somatically derived ovarian yolk sac tumors (SD-YSTs) are rare malignancies in postmenopausal women that arise from somatic differentiation of endometriosis or epithelial ovarian tumors, presenting with advanced-stage disease and historically poor prognosis (30.6% five-year survival). This case report documents a 74-year-old woman with stage IIIB SD-YST harboring TP53 and AKT2 mutations who achieved durable remission with personalized cisplatin-etoposide chemotherapy despite the typical chemoresistance associated with SD-YSTs. The tumor exhibited immunohistochemical positivity for markers including Glypican-3, SALL-4, and CDX2, alongside elevated serum tumor markers (AFP, CA-125, and CEA) and characteristic histopathological features such as Schiller-Duval bodies and hyaline globules. Early normalization of alpha-fetoprotein levels by day 21 and serial AFP monitoring provided reliable indicators of treatment efficacy and favorable outcome. This case demonstrates that SD-YSTs, though traditionally associated with dismal outcomes, can achieve complete remission through personalized chemotherapy strategies tailored to individual tumor molecular profiles and careful monitoring of tumor biomarkers.

title: Immunohistochemical Expression of p53 in Epithelial Ovarian Carcinoma and Its Correlation with Clinicopathological Parameters
summary: This study demonstrates that p53 mutations are a critical molecular feature of high-grade epithelial ovarian carcinomas (EOCs), particularly high-grade serous carcinomas, where p53 alterations occur in approximately 100% of cases. The p53 tumor suppressor gene, located on chromosome 17p13.1, normally functions to repair DNA damage and trigger apoptosis, but mutations in this gene cause uncontrolled cell growth and tumorigenesis. The research reveals significant associations between p53 mutation status and multiple clinicopathological parameters including histological subtype, tumor grade, and family history of ovarian cancer, with p53 mutations being enriched in serous carcinomas while largely absent in mucinous and endometrioid subtypes. Patients with p53 mutations have worse clinical outcomes and respond more poorly to irradiation and chemotherapy compared to those with normal p53, highlighting the prognostic significance of p53 status. CA-125 levels were also found to be elevated in advanced-stage and high-grade serous carcinomas, showing a positive correlation with p53 expression. These findings underscore p53 as a fundamental driver of aggressive ovarian cancer and suggest that inherited genetic factors may influence the prevalence of p53 mutations in familial ovarian cancer cases.

title: Intestinal-Type Adenocarcinoma Is a Rare Histotype of Vulvar Neoplasm: Systematic Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

Intestinal-type vulvar adenocarcinoma (VAIt) is an exceptionally rare variant of vulvar cancer characterized by mucinous villo-glandular architecture with goblet cells and Paneth cells, resembling colorectal adenocarcinoma. Diagnosis relies on recognition of intestinal-type morphology confirmed by immunohistochemistry, with CK20 and CDX2 showing consistent positivity and CK20 expression typically exceeding CK7 in approximately 89% of cases. Unlike most vulvar cancers associated with HPV-16 and HPV-18, VAIt tumors are notably HPV-negative and may originate from multiple sources including Bartholin's glands, minor vestibular glands, endometriosis implants, or embryonic remnants such as misplaced cloacal epithelium or intestinal metaplasia. Risk factors for vulvar adenocarcinomas generally include smoking, obesity, immunosuppression, and lichen sclerosus, though VAIt appears to follow a distinct pathogenic pathway. Surgical intervention represents the cornerstone of treatment, with wide local excision being the predominant approach, supplemented in more advanced cases by radical vulvectomy with lymph node dissection and adjuvant radiotherapy or chemotherapy. The systematic review underscores the importance of recognizing this rare histotype to ensure appropriate diagnosis and management.

title: A Challenging Diagnosis of Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma in a 50-Year-Old Patient: Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma (LG-ESS) is the second most common uterine mesenchymal malignancy, typically presenting with abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and dysmenorrhea, particularly in perimenopausal and postmenopausal women. Key risk factors include obesity, diabetes, prolonged tamoxifen use, unopposed estrogen therapy, early menarche, and pelvic radiation. LG-ESS is characterized by specific genetic fusions (JAZF1, PHF1, and MEAF6-PHF1) and displays a distinctive immunohistochemical profile including strong expression of CD10, WT1, ER, PR, vimentin, IFITM1, and variably smooth muscle markers, with absent or minimal cyclin D1 expression. Although approximately one-third of cases demonstrate extrauterine spread affecting the lungs, lymph nodes, and ovaries, LG-ESS generally follows an indolent clinical course with marked sensitivity to hormonal regulation. Diagnosis can be challenging and requires immunohistochemical evaluation combined with molecular analysis to distinguish LG-ESS from endometrial stromal nodules and high-grade variants, with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy representing the standard surgical treatment. The absence of lymphovascular invasion correlates with favorable postoperative outcomes.

title: Elacridar Reverses P-gp-Mediated Drug Resistance in Ovarian Cancer Cells in 2D and 3D Culture Models
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer cells frequently develop multidrug resistance through P-glycoprotein (P-gp) overexpression, an ATP-binding cassette transporter encoded by the MDR1 gene that actively pumps chemotherapeutic agents like paclitaxel and doxorubicin out of cells. In this study, elacridar, a dual P-gp and BCRP inhibitor, effectively reversed P-gp-mediated drug resistance in paclitaxel-resistant ovarian cancer cell lines (W1PR1, W1PR1-C7, and W1PR2) by binding to P-gp and blocking its transport activity, restoring cellular sensitivity to paclitaxel and doxorubicin with fold-decreases in IC50 values ranging from 77 to 538-fold. Notably, elacridar did not reduce cisplatin resistance or alter P-gp protein expression levels, indicating selectivity for P-gp substrate drugs. The findings highlight elacridar's potential as a therapeutic strategy to overcome ABC transporter-mediated drug resistance in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancers, particularly epithelioid and clear cell subtypes, which can arise from endometriotic lesions and exhibit poor prognosis due to early chemoresistance.

title: Expanding the spectrum of AFF2 undifferentiated sarcoma associated to endometriosis: a novel ZDHHC9::AFF2 fusion sarcoma with high-grade features and poor prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a novel ZDHHC9::AFF2 fusion gene identified in a high-grade undifferentiated ovarian sarcoma arising from cystic endometriosis, expanding the known spectrum of AFF2-associated malignancies. The AFF2 gene, traditionally linked to Fragile X syndrome and recently implicated in sinonasal squamous cell carcinomas, encodes a protein involved in transcriptional regulation and chromatin remodeling through interactions with the AF4/FMR2 protein family. The tumor expressed characteristic stromal markers including estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), WT1, CD10, vimentin, and desmin, along with elevated p53 expression in 75% of cells, indicating aggressive biology. This case represents an emerging subset of endometriosis-associated malignancies driven by AFF2 fusion variants, where prior molecular understanding was limited to WWTR1::AFF2 fusions in spindle cell sarcomas. The patient received combination chemotherapy with epirubicin and ifosfamide, though the high-grade features suggest poor prognosis. These findings underscore the role of atypical endometriosis as a premalignant precursor and highlight the importance of recognizing novel fusion oncogenes in endometriosis-associated sarcomas for accurate diagnosis and potential targeted therapeutic approaches.

title: Appendiceal endosalpingiosis: a rare case with potential implications for gynecological malignancy
summary: Endosalpingiosis, a rare condition characterized by ectopic fallopian tube-like ciliated epithelial tissue, occurs primarily in the ovary, fallopian tube, omentum, uterus, and peritoneum, with appendiceal involvement being exceptionally uncommon. This case report highlights an important clinical association between endosalpingiosis and gynecologic malignancies, particularly serous borderline ovarian tumors and ovarian serous tumors of low malignant potential, as well as increased risk for ovarian and uterine cancer. Pathological examination of the appendiceal lesion confirmed endosalpingiosis through positive immunostaining for PAX8, estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and cytokeratin 7 (CK7), while remaining negative for CD10, CDX2, and CK20—a marker profile consistent with müllerian tissue origin. Unlike endometriosis, endosalpingiosis demonstrates higher incidence in postmenopausal women and shows less frequent association with pelvic pain, though appendiceal endosalpingiosis can present as an appendicitis mimic causing right lower quadrant pain without acute inflammatory changes. Although endosalpingiosis may co-occur with endometriosis, it appears to function as a distinct clinical entity with greater malignant potential. These findings underscore the clinical significance of recognizing endosalpingiosis, particularly its rare appendiceal presentation, given the potential implications for malignancy surveillance in affected patients.

title: Clear Cell Carcinoma of the Vagina in an 11-Year-Old Girl: A Case Report on Clinical, Imaging, and Immunophenotypic Features
summary: This case report documents vaginal clear cell carcinoma in an 11-year-old girl, highlighting the molecular and clinical features of this rare malignancy. Historically, in utero diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure causes clear cell carcinoma through persistence of Müllerian epithelium and abnormal epithelial-mesenchymal interactions, though the tumor can also arise from metanephric, mesonephric, or Müllerian duct abnormalities independent of DES exposure. Carcinogenesis involves overexpression of proto-oncogenes (c-jun, c-fos, c-myc), anti-apoptotic genes (bcl-2, bcl-x), growth factors (EGF, TGF-α), and estrogen-responsive genes like lactoferrin. Immunohistochemically, the tumor demonstrated diffuse positivity for CK7 and PAX8, with focal Napsin A expression, markers confirming Müllerian origin. The patient was treated with concurrent cisplatin-based external beam radiation therapy followed by high-dose-rate interstitial brachytherapy, showing symptom reduction. Prognosis depends on disease stage, tumor size, surgical margins, and nodal status, with complete surgical resection offering favorable outcomes in early-stage disease, while advanced cases require multimodal chemoradiation therapy.

title: Borderline ovarian clear cell tumor arising from endometriosis during long-term dienogest therapy: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents the development of a borderline ovarian clear cell tumor arising from endometriosis despite long-term dienogest therapy, highlighting a critical clinical limitation of hormonal treatment. Ovarian endometriosis is established as a recognized precursor to ovarian malignancies, particularly clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas, with risk factors including advanced age, menopause, and large cyst diameter. While dienogest effectively suppresses ectopic endometrial tissue growth and reduces endometriosis-related pain and dysmenorrhea, this case demonstrates that such treatment does not eliminate malignant transformation potential. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed the tumor expressed HNF1-β and PAX-8 (specific markers for ovarian clear cell neoplasms) with wild-type p53, intact mismatch repair proteins, and a Ki-67 proliferation index of approximately 40%, consistent with borderline rather than invasive disease. The findings underscore the necessity for continued surveillance of endometriomas even during effective hormonal therapy and identify imaging features—such as papillary growth, solid components, and rapid enlargement—as potential malignancy indicators warranting closer clinical monitoring.

title: Molecular and Immune Microenvironmental Changes Across Endometrial Lesions: A Comprehensive Immunohistochemical and Clinical Analysis of Progression From Benignity to Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive immunohistochemical study characterizes the molecular and immune microenvironmental progression from benign endometrial lesions to endometriosis-associated carcinoma. Cell cycle regulators cyclin D1, CDK4, and P16 show progressive overexpression across atypical endometriosis and carcinoma, driving uncontrolled proliferation through dysregulation of the G1-S phase transition, while estrogen and progesterone receptor expression progressively declines, conferring hormonal therapy resistance and increased lesion autonomy. The immune microenvironment undergoes a critical shift toward immunosuppression, with accumulating FOXP3+ regulatory T cells and M2-polarized macrophages (marked by CD163 elevation) establishing local immune tolerance that facilitates malignant progression. Concurrently, markers of genomic instability including P53 aberrations and elevated Ki67 proliferative index emerge, particularly in atypical endometriosis and carcinoma, indicating a shift toward dysplastic transformation. Notably, endometrial hyperplasia with atypia serves as a premalignant precursor, with four of six followed patients subsequently developing endometriosis-associated carcinoma, highlighting the importance of early detection in these high-risk lesions. Together, these findings delineate a multifactorial malignant progression model combining cell cycle dysregulation, hormonal dysfunction, and immunosuppressive microenvironmental remodeling.

title: Genetics of High-Grade Endometrioid Adenocarcinoma of the Ovary With Yolk Sac Differentiation: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes a rare high-grade endometrioid adenocarcinoma of the ovary with concurrent yolk sac tumor differentiation, a diagnostic challenge due to overlapping features with high-grade serous carcinomas. Genomic analysis identified pathogenic mutations in CTNNB1, ARID1A, and PIK3CA—mutations characteristic of endometrioid carcinomas and associated with endometriosis-derived tumors—confirming the endometrioid origin despite yolk sac differentiation. Immunohistochemical profiling revealed tumor cells positive for epithelial markers (AE1-AE3, PAX-8), yolk sac tumor markers (SALL-4, glypican-3, and AFP), but negative for vimentin and hormone receptors (ER/PR), further supporting the diagnosis. The identification of shared somatic mutations between the endometrioid and yolk sac components in mixed tumors indicates both components derived from the same epithelial precursor rather than from germ cells. The patient responded to a bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin chemotherapy regimen typically used for germ cell tumors, suggesting that molecular profiling should guide therapeutic selection in these rare composite tumors. This case highlights the importance of genetic profiling in distinguishing endometrioid adenocarcinomas with yolk sac differentiation from pure germ cell-derived yolk sac tumors to enable appropriate multimodal treatment strategies.

title: Primary Ovarian Poorly Differentiated Adenocarcinoma with Signet-Ring Cells: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Primary ovarian signet-ring cell carcinoma (POSRCC) is a rare adenocarcinoma subtype most commonly associated with mucinous neoplasms and mature cystic teratomas, though signet-ring cells in the ovary more frequently represent metastatic disease (Krukenberg tumors) originating from gastrointestinal sites including the stomach, pancreas, colon, and biliary tract. Diagnostic differentiation between primary and metastatic tumors relies on clinicopathologic features and immunohistochemical markers: primary ovarian neoplasms typically show unilateral involvement, large tumor size, and endometriosis association, while metastatic disease presents with bilateral involvement, lymphovascular invasion, and surface deposits. The immunophenotype is critical for classification, as primary ovarian mucinous tumors express CK7 and may express PAX8 (absent in Krukenberg tumors), whereas metastatic lower intestinal tumors demonstrate diffuse CK20 expression with absent or minimal CK7. This case report describes a patient treated with multimodal chemotherapy including paclitaxel, carboplatin, bevacizumab, belotecan, cisplatin, and pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, yet experienced disease recurrence eight months after surgery and died from progressive disease eighteen months post-diagnosis. Complete cytoreductive surgery without residual tumor represents the cornerstone of management, though POSRCC carries a poor prognosis despite aggressive treatment, highlighting the need for improved therapeutic strategies in this malignant tumor.

title: The Umbilical Clue: Recognizing Sister Mary Joseph’s Nodule
journal: European Journal of Case Reports in Internal Medicine
summary:
# Summary

Sister Mary Joseph's nodule, a rare metastatic lesion at the umbilicus, represents an important clinical indicator of advanced intra-abdominal or pelvic malignancy with poor prognosis. This case report describes an 82-year-old woman presenting with a violaceous umbilical lesion associated with asthenia, weight loss, epigastric pain, anorexia, and abdominal distension, ultimately diagnosed with serous endometrial adenocarcinoma that had metastasized to the umbilicus, peritoneum, and pelvic lymph nodes. Immunohistochemical analysis of both the umbilical and endometrial lesions revealed positive cytokeratin 7 (CK7) and PAX8 markers with negative CK20 and CDX2, confirming the genitourinary origin of the cutaneous metastasis. While Sister Mary Joseph's nodule most commonly originates from gastrointestinal malignancies in men and gynecological cancers in women, endometrial adenocarcinoma represents a particularly rare source requiring consideration in the differential diagnosis. The nodule typically signifies advanced disease with limited therapeutic options; however, aggressive combined surgical and adjuvant therapy may offer improved survival compared to palliative approaches alone. This case underscores the clinical importance of recognizing umbilical lesions as potential indicators of underlying malignancy.

title: Invasive Acantholytic Extramammary Paget's Disease of the Vulva With Mammary Carcinoma-Like Differentiation and MMR Loss: Report of an Unusual Case and Its Clinical Mimics
summary: This case report describes an unusual invasive acantholytic extramammary Paget's disease (AAEMPD) of the vulva characterized by mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency, specifically loss of MSH2 and MSH6 proteins, which resulted in microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) status and elevated tumor mutational burden. Molecular profiling identified pathogenic variants in PIK3CA (E545K) and TP53 (R248Q), with the TP53 mutation associated with aggressive tumor behavior evidenced by increased depth of invasion and lymphovascular invasion. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed characteristic markers including positivity for CK7, BerEP4, EMA, and GATA-3, while showing negativity for CK20, GCDFP-15, and hormone receptors, with an elevated Ki-67 proliferation index of approximately 70%. The patient underwent radical hemivulvectomy followed by concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin, and the case was further complicated by the discovery of a synchronous colorectal adenocarcinoma with MLH1/PMS2 loss and BRAF V600E mutation, raising concern for Lynch syndrome. This first reported case of vulvar AAEMPD with MMR loss highlights the molecular heterogeneity of this rare malignancy and suggests that MSI-H status may have therapeutic implications for immune checkpoint inhibition strategies.

title: Programmed death-ligand 1 upregulation is associated with poor prognosis in patients with epithelial ovarian cancer
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) upregulation represents a critical mechanism of platinum resistance and poor prognosis in epithelial ovarian cancer, particularly in endometrioid and clear cell subtypes. High tumor PD-L1 expression (combined positive score ≥10) was significantly more prevalent in platinum-resistant patients (38.5%) compared to platinum-sensitive patients (19.0%), and was independently associated with shorter overall survival and increased disease recurrence. Notably, cisplatin chemotherapy paradoxically induces PD-L1 expression in a dose- and time-dependent manner, with platinum-resistant cell lines (A2780R and ES2R) and tumors exhibiting substantially higher baseline PD-L1 levels than their drug-sensitive counterparts.
