This chemotherapy-induced PD-L1 upregulation enables tumor immune escape by suppressing cytotoxic T cell activity, representing a tumor-intrinsic adaptive immune resistance mechanism that perpetuates chemoresistance. The findings highlight PD-L1 as both a prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target, with potential clinical implications for combining platinum-based chemotherapy with immune checkpoint inhibitors in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer management.

title: Active surveillance of an endometriosis-related, hormone-responsive pelvic adenosarcoma during pregnancy: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis can progress to extrauterine adenosarcoma, a rare hormone-responsive pelvic malignancy characterized by expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors in its mesenchymal component. In this case report, a patient with endometriosis-associated, low-grade adenosarcoma experienced rapid tumor growth during pregnancy due to elevated pregnancy-related hormones, with the mass expanding from initial detection to 13.7 × 9.7 × 14.7 cm by the third trimester. The tumor's hormone sensitivity precluded standard chemotherapy during pregnancy due to teratogenic risks; instead, active surveillance was maintained until postpartum. Following delivery, the patient was successfully treated with dual antiestrogen therapy combining the GnRH agonist leuprolide and aromatase inhibitor letrozole, along with subsequent GnRH antagonist elagolix for endometriosis management. Low-grade adenosarcomas without sarcomatous overgrowth generally have favorable prognoses with 5-year survival rates of 60–80%, though sarcomatous overgrowth, lymphovascular invasion, and myometrial invasion are associated with poorer outcomes. This case demonstrates that hormone-receptor-positive adenosarcomas can be safely managed conservatively during pregnancy with hormonal therapy reserved for the postpartum period.

title: Two Giant Cystic Uterine Adenomyomas in a Premenopausal Woman: The Largest Case to Date With Immunohistochemical Findings
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes giant cystic adenomyomas in a premenopausal woman and elucidates the molecular mechanisms underlying adenomyosis and its malignant potential. The lesions exhibited a distinctive immunohistochemical profile characterized by loss of PTEN and ARID1A expression alongside increased HNF-1β, phosphorylated AKT (pAKT), and estrogen receptor expression, contrasting sharply with normal eutopic endometrial glands that retained these tumor suppressors. The authors propose that PTEN-deficient endometrial clones invade the myometrium to initiate adenomyosis, with subsequent ARID1A loss and enhanced pAKT signaling driving cystic enlargement and gigantism. Notably, the absence of p53 mutations appeared protective against malignant transformation despite combined PTEN and ARID1A losses, suggesting that p53 inactivation is critical for endometriosis-associated carcinogenesis. These findings highlight the multi-hit molecular model of adenomyosis progression and clarify why certain adenomyomas remain benign despite carrying genetic alterations typically associated with malignancy, providing important insights for understanding endometriosis-related tumorigenesis.

title: Rectal adenocarcinoma with a yolk sac tumor component: A rare case report and review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare rectal adenocarcinoma with yolk sac tumor (YST) component in a 66-year-old male presenting with anal bleeding and elevated carcinoembryonic antigen levels. While yolk sac tumors typically originate in the gonads and represent 3-5% of ovarian malignancies in children and young women, approximately 24% occur at extragonadal sites such as the mediastinum, retroperitoneum, and brain, with colorectal locations being exceptionally uncommon. The tumor harbored a KRAS G12S mutation and was characterized by expression of SALL4 and glypican3 immunostaining markers, though notably lacked alpha-fetoprotein production, which is unusual for this tumor type. Following surgical resection via laparoscopic abdominoperineal resection, the patient developed liver metastasis within six months, consistent with the poor prognosis typically associated with colorectal YST-like carcinomas and colon and rectal adenocarcinomas with enteroblastic differentiation (CAED). Contemporary treatment protocols for extragonadal YST include four cycles of chemotherapy with bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) or ifosfamide-based regimens, which significantly improve prognosis compared to surgery alone. This case highlights the diagnostic and clinical challenges of YST occurring outside typical anatomical locations and emphasizes the importance of recognizing enteroblastic differentiation markers in colorectal malignancies.

title: Paratubal Leiomyoma Mimicking Ovarian Malignancy: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Paratubal leiomyomas are exceptionally rare benign smooth muscle tumors arising from paratubal tissue that present a significant diagnostic challenge by mimicking ovarian malignancies. This case report describes a postmenopausal woman with an adnexal mass associated with elevated CA-125 and CEA levels, endometrial thickening, and endometrial hyperplasia without atypia, findings that initially suggested malignancy. Histopathological examination revealing smooth muscle composition with positive smooth muscle actin staining and negative calretinin and inhibin expression confirmed the benign diagnosis of paratubal leiomyoma with hyalinization, accompanied by adenomyosis. The case emphasizes the importance of recognizing paratubal leiomyomas in the differential diagnosis of adnexal masses to prevent unnecessary surgical intervention and overtreatment, and highlights that MED12 mutations may underlie the molecular basis of adnexal leiomyomas similar to uterine counterparts. Immunohistochemical profiling and standardized imaging risk stratification systems such as IOTA Simple Rules and O-RADS are essential tools for distinguishing benign paratubal leiomyomas from malignant conditions including high-grade serous carcinoma and metastatic lesions to the fallopian tube.

title: Collision tumor of endometrial hepatoid adenocarcinoma and endometrial stromal sarcoma: a rare case and literature review
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare collision tumor comprising endometrial hepatoid adenocarcinoma (HAC) and endometrial stromal sarcoma occurring in a 69-year-old postmenopausal woman with pelvic endometriosis. The endometrial HAC component demonstrated elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), expressing hepatocellular markers including AFP, HEP, and HNF-1, while the stromal sarcoma component expressed characteristic markers CD10 and Vimentin. Despite aggressive multimodal treatment with surgery, paclitaxel-carboplatin chemotherapy, external beam radiotherapy, and subsequent CAP regimen (cyclophosphamide, adriamycin, cisplatin), the patient developed pulmonary metastases within 40 days of surgery and died of disease, highlighting the poor prognosis and lung metastasis propensity of endometrial HAC. The case demonstrates that endometrial HAC exhibits marked aggressiveness and resistance to radiation therapy, underscoring the need for comprehensive preoperative staging with 18F-FDG PET/CT and aggressive chemotherapy strategies in management. This collision tumor case contributes to the limited literature on these exceptionally rare malignancies and suggests a possible association between endometriosis and endometrial HAC development.

title: Prognostic modeling of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer based on molecular signatures: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC) predominantly manifests as ovarian clear cell or endometrioid carcinomas arising from ectopic endometrium and demonstrates poor response to standard carboplatin-paclitaxel chemotherapy. Molecular classification into three subtypes—p53 wild-type (p53wt, 85.3%), mismatch repair deficient (MMRd, 4.2%), and p53 abnormal (p53abn, 10.5%)—revealed distinct prognostic outcomes, with MMRd tumors exhibiting significantly reduced progression-free survival (HR 19.58) and overall survival (HR 10.63) compared to p53wt. A prognostic nomogram incorporating pathological classification, FIGO stage, bilateral ovarian involvement, and MMRd status effectively predicted recurrence risk, with clear cell carcinoma (HR 5.66), advanced FIGO stage (HR 2.79), bilateral involvement (HR 3.49), and MMRd status (HR 8.95) emerging as independent adverse prognostic factors. This molecular signature-integrated nomogram provides a reliable clinical tool for risk stratification and treatment decision-making in EAOC management, though alternative therapeutic strategies such as PARP inhibitors warrant further investigation for chemotherapy-resistant cases.

title: Endometrial adenocarcinoma arising from adenomyosis: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis carries a significantly elevated risk for malignant transformation to endometrial carcinoma, with coexistence documented in 18-66% of cases, though malignant transformation remains rare. The pathogenic mechanisms underlying this transformation involve chronic oxidative stress, PTEN and PIK3CA mutations, epigenetic dysregulation, and impaired immune surveillance, with emerging roles for TSC2-mTOR pathway dysregulation and endometrial microbiota imbalance. This case describes a 45-year-old woman with prior adenomyotic lesion resection who developed a poorly differentiated endometrioid adenocarcinoma characterized by hormone receptor negativity, elevated proliferation (Ki-67 10-30%), and deep myometrial invasion, features associated with aggressive disease requiring systemic chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin. While adenomyosis coexistence paradoxically associates with improved prognosis through earlier detection and favorable tumor characteristics, hormone receptor negativity and p53 mutations indicate poor treatment response to endocrine therapy but potential sensitivity to immunotherapy. The case emphasizes that abnormal uterine bleeding warrants prompt imaging and histological assessment, and that patients with adenomyosis-associated malignancy require aggressive multimodal treatment and close surveillance, particularly those with early-onset disease or prolonged estrogen exposure.

title: Pelvic Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumor (GIST) Mimicking Ovarian Cancer in a Kidney–Liver Transplant Recipient Under Chronic Immunosuppression
summary:
# Summary

Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) are rare mesenchymal neoplasms driven by activating mutations in KIT or PDGFRA genes, with KIT Exon 11 being the most common mutation, though this case presented a KIT Exon 9 mutation requiring higher-dose imatinib therapy (800 mg daily). Diagnosis relies on immunohistochemical markers including DOG1 and CD117 positivity, which are critical for differentiating GISTs from mimicking malignancies such as ovarian cancer, particularly in unusual pelvic locations. This case illustrated how a pelvic GIST in a kidney-liver transplant recipient on chronic immunosuppression created diagnostic uncertainty, as solid-organ transplant recipients carry a two- to fourfold increased cancer risk. Surgical resection with complete tumor removal significantly improves prognosis, though intraoperative rupture and high mitotic rates (>5 per 50 HPF) or tumor size >5 cm substantially elevate recurrence risk. An important clinical consideration emerged regarding imatinib's inhibition of CYP3A4, which increases tacrolimus exposure in transplant patients, necessitating close therapeutic drug monitoring and interdisciplinary collaboration to optimize both immunosuppression and KIT-targeted therapy.

title: Case Report: Diagnosis and management of primary ovarian squamous-cell carcinoma: a report of two cases and systematic review of the literature
summary: Primary ovarian squamous-cell carcinoma (POSCC) is a rare malignancy most commonly associated with mature cystic teratomas (dermoid cysts), Brenner tumors, and ovarian endometriosis, with over 80% of cases arising from malignant transformation of these pre-existing lesions. The molecular landscape of POSCC involves distinct microRNA dysregulation, including upregulation of miR-151a-3p and miR-378a-3p and downregulation of miR-26a-5p and miR-99a-5p, alongside KLF5-related gene expression signatures and mutant p53 expression in approximately 80% of cases. High-risk HPV genotypes (HPV16 and HPV18) serve as established oncogenic drivers, with viral E6/E7 proteins inhibiting p53 and Rb while promoting telomerase activation and apoptosis resistance, though HPV positivity varies and can be identified by p16 overexpression as a surrogate marker. Immunohistochemical characterization shows high-molecular-weight cytokeratin expression (CK5/6, 34βE12) and p63 positivity, with treatment typically involving platinum-based chemotherapy with paclitaxel combined with aggressive surgical cytoreduction including radical hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and systematic lymphadenectomy. Prognosis is highly stage-dependent, with 5-year survival rates declining sharply from 76% in stage I to 0% in stage IV disease, with poor prognostic factors including advanced age, large tumor diameter, lymphovascular invasion, and incomplete cytoreduction. Early detection remains critical, as POSCC presents with nonspecific symptoms including chronic p

title: Adenosarcoma of the uterine cervix – a novel case report and review of the literature
summary: Adenosarcoma is a rare mixed müllerian tumor characterized histologically by benign epithelial components and malignant stromal elements, most commonly arising in the uterine corpus but occasionally in the ovaries or cervix (only 2% of cases). This disease is hypothesized to develop from long-standing endometriosis and presents clinically with abnormal vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, vaginal discharge, or abdominal mass, though cervical cytology may appear normal. Key genetic alterations including KMT2C and BCOR mutations are frequently detected, while sarcomatous overgrowth—a critical prognostic factor associated with MAGEC1, KDM6B, and MYBL1 alterations—significantly increases recurrence risk and poor outcomes. Unopposed estrogen stimulation without progesterone counterbalance promotes malignant transformation through endometrial hyperplasia and atypia, with estrogen and progesterone receptor expression in adenosarcomas enabling hormonal therapeutic approaches. Surgical treatment via radical hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy remains the primary intervention, while adjuvant radiotherapy, chemotherapy regimens (particularly doxorubicin with ifosfamide for improved progression-free survival), and hormonal therapies including leuprolide, synthetic progestins, and aromatase inhibitors are considered for advanced or recurrent disease.

title: The Influence of Molecular Factors on the Effectiveness of New Therapies in Endometrial Cancer—Latest Evidence and Clinical Trials
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer incidence is rising due to metabolic and genetic risk factors including obesity, type 2 diabetes, PCOS, and Lynch syndrome, with postmenopausal bleeding serving as a key early diagnostic indicator. The disease comprises distinct molecular subtypes—Type I tumors (80% of cases) driven by PTEN, KRAS, and CTNNB1 mutations, and Type II tumors associated with TP53 mutations and HER2 amplification—alongside prognostic subtypes including POLEmut (best prognosis), MMRd/MSI-H (intermediate), and p53abn (worst prognosis). Mismatch repair deficiency causes high tumor mutational burden and neoantigen formation, making dMMR/MSI-H tumors responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors including pembrolizumab and dostarlimab, which significantly prolong progression-free survival when combined with chemotherapy. Targeted therapies show promise across molecular subtypes: HER2-amplified cancers respond to trastuzumab deruxtecan, estrogen receptor-positive tumors to aromatase inhibitors and CDK4/6 inhibitors, and PI3K/AKT/mTOR-altered cancers to pathway inhibitors like capivasertib. Additionally, ARID1A mutations correlate with increased PD-L1 expression and immunotherapy response, while emerging biomarkers including circulating tumor DNA, dysregulated microRNAs, and long non-coding RNAs offer potential for treatment monitoring and response prediction.

title: AI-based quantification of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes with integrative transcriptomics in ovarian clear cell carcinoma: JGOG3025-TR1/A1 study
summary: Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (CCC), which frequently arises from endometriosis and harbors ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations, presents a paradoxical immune microenvironment characterized by metabolic adaptations including mitochondrial dysfunction. While CCC typically shows an immunologically cold phenotype with sparse tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, a transcriptomically defined immune-hot cluster counterintuitively associates with significantly poor prognosis, higher recurrence rates, advanced-stage disease, and residual tumors—outcomes worse than predicted by pathological assessment alone. In contrast, a pathologically-defined inflamed phenotype with increased intraepithelial CD8+ T cells, activated CD8 T cell scores, and elevated CTLA4 and interferon-gamma pathway expression emerges as a statistically significant favorable prognostic factor for both overall and progression-free survival, with particularly strong benefits in advanced-stage and residually diseased patients. These findings reveal critical discordance between transcriptomic and morphological immune profiling, highlighting that morphology-based inflammation assessment better predicts outcomes than gene expression-based clustering in CCC. The inflamed phenotype's association with improved survival, combined with CCC's responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibitors, supports immunotherapy as a promising therapeutic strategy for this endometriosis-associated malignancy.

title: DART/SWOG/NCI phase II anti-CTLA-4/PD-1 trial: clear cell carcinomas of ovary, endometrium, cervix
summary:
# Summary

This phase II DART/SWOG/NCI trial demonstrates that dual anti-CTLA-4/PD-1 checkpoint inhibition with ipilimumab plus nivolumab is effective for treating gynecologic clear cell carcinomas (ovarian, endometrial, and cervical), achieving a 33% overall response rate with improved progression-free and overall survival compared to nivolumab monotherapy. Ovarian clear cell carcinomas frequently harbor ARID1A mutations and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway alterations and are commonly associated with endometriosis, features that may contribute to increased immunogenicity and treatment responsiveness. Elevated tumor mutation burden emerged as a significant predictor of immunotherapy response in this patient cohort. Common treatment-related adverse events included fatigue, nausea, liver dysfunction, and thyroid dysfunction, with 53% of patients experiencing grade 3 or higher toxicities. These findings support checkpoint inhibitor combination therapy as a promising immunotherapeutic approach for these chemotherapy-resistant tumors, particularly in patients with higher tumor mutation burden.

title: A rare case of bilateral adnexal Müllerian adenosarcoma arising from endometriosis: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of bilateral adnexal Müllerian adenosarcoma arising from endometriosis in a young nulliparous woman who presented with abdominal bloating, pelvic pain, and significant ascites. Müllerian adenosarcomas are uncommon mixed epithelial-mesenchymal neoplasms that predominantly develop in the uterine corpus but can arise at extrauterine sites including the ovaries, fallopian tubes, peritoneum, and other locations, with extrauterine cases frequently associated with endometriosis. Ovarian adenosarcomas, as in this case, present a more aggressive clinical course with advanced disease stage, higher recurrence rates (exceeding 25%), and mortality risk over 35%, contrasting sharply with uterine adenosarcomas. The tumors demonstrated characteristic histopathologic features including biphasic growth with benign mucinous epithelium, leaf-like architecture, periglandular stromal condensation, and immunohistochemical expression of CD10, WT1, estrogen and progesterone receptors in stromal cells, along with elevated MIB1 proliferation index indicating aggressive behavior. Treatment involved multimodality approach combining neoadjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin, paclitaxel, and bevacizumab followed by surgical resection with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. This case highlights the critical link between endometriosis and adenosarcoma tumorigenesis, supporting estrogen-driven malignant transformation of endometriotic lesions.

title: A Rare Case of Primary Ovarian Mucinous Carcinoma with Signet-Ring Cells and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare primary ovarian mucinous carcinoma containing signet-ring cells, a histological finding that typically indicates metastatic disease (Krukenberg tumors) originating from the gastrointestinal tract, appendix, pancreaticobiliary system, urinary bladder, or lobular breast carcinoma. The immunohistochemical profile distinguished this as a primary tumor: conventional mucinous areas expressed Keratin 7, Keratin 20, and PAX8 while lacking CDX2, whereas signet-ring cells showed the opposite pattern with positivity for Keratin 20, CDX2, and Keratin 7 but negativity for PAX8. Metastatic tumors typically show loss of SMAD4 expression, whereas this case retained SMAD4, further supporting primary origin. Clinically significant diagnostic features for differentiating primary from metastatic mucinous carcinomas include tumor stage, presence of endometriosis or adenoma/adenofibroma background, and architectural features such as bilaterality, ovarian surface involvement, multinodularity, and lymphovascular invasion. Early-stage, low-grade primary mucinous carcinomas with localized signet-ring cell nodules appear to have favorable outcomes without requiring adjuvant treatment, distinguishing them from the typically aggressive metastatic Krukenberg tumors.

title: Pulmonary Pneumocytoma: A Benign Rarity Behind a Suspicious Nodule
summary: Pulmonary pneumocytoma is a rare benign lung tumor arising from primitive respiratory epithelium and type II pneumocytes that predominantly affects middle-aged females with a female-to-male ratio of approximately 5:1, typically presenting incidentally as a solitary pulmonary nodule. Histologically, pneumocytomas display papillary structures alternating with solid nests of spindle-shaped cells, extensive hemorrhagic areas, and minimal cytologic atypia, features that establish their benign nature. Immunohistochemical analysis characteristically shows expression of epithelial and lung-associated markers including EMA, cytokeratin 7, TTF-1, CD68, pancytokeratin, and napsin A, while lacking neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin A, synaptophysin, CD56) and endothelial markers (CD34, CD31) that would indicate alternative diagnoses such as carcinoid tumors or vascular lesions. Despite potentially exhibiting low to moderate fluorodeoxyglucose uptake on imaging that may overlap with malignant lesions, the distinctive immunophenotypic profile and histological features enable reliable differentiation from more aggressive tumors. When symptomatic, patients typically report nonspecific chest pain, and surgical resection is curative for this benign neoplasm.

title: Mesenteric and Retroperitoneal Mucinous Cystic Neoplasms: A Nongynecologic Process Commonly Managed by Gynecologic Oncologists
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Mucinous cystic neoplasms are rare tumors typically arising in the pancreas and liver, but this article describes unusual cases originating from the mesentery and retroperitoneum in patients with concurrent diverticulitis. The tumors are characterized histologically by mucinous epithelium expressing CK7, CK20, and CDX2, along with ovarian-like stroma positive for CD10, ER, PR, and WT1, with elevated Ki67 proliferation indices. The proposed pathogenesis involves metaplasia of coelomic epithelial remnants or mucinous metaplasia triggered by chronic inflammation in diverticula, a mechanism similar to endometriosis development. Complete surgical excision is essential for treatment, as incomplete resection can lead to recurrence and malignant transformation to metastatic disease. The authors note that gynecologic oncologists frequently encounter these nongynecologic tumors, which may be hormonally influenced based on their expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors in the stromal component.

title: Whole-Exome Sequencing Improves Risk Assessments of Adult Moyamoya Disease
summary:
# Summary

Whole-exome sequencing identified multiple genetic variants significantly associated with moyamoya disease (MMD), a cerebrovascular disorder disproportionately affecting East Asian populations. Mutations in RNF213 (ring finger protein 213), particularly p.R4810K with an exceptionally high odds ratio of 117.4, emerged as the strongest genetic contributor, along with variants in GAA, CHMP6, and CARD14 genes. The study validated four RNF213 mutations (p.V1195M, p.D1331G, p.S2334N, and p.R4810K) in East Asian populations and demonstrated striking population-specific differences, with p.R4810K and p.D54N absent in European-ancestry populations but prevalent in Korean patients. A nine-mutation model (MARS9) achieved 90.89% predictability for MMD risk assessment. Clinical manifestations included transient ischemic attacks (57.5% of patients), cerebral infarctions (15.6%), and hemorrhagic strokes (18.8%), highlighting the severe neurological consequences of this genetically heterogeneous disease.

title: An Immunodeficient Patient with Adenomyosis of the Uterus Complicated by Polyp-Like Endometriosis: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of polypoid endometriosis concurrent with adenomyosis in an immunodeficient patient, highlighting the critical role of immune dysfunction in endometriosis pathogenesis. Adenomyosis involves migration of endometrial glands and mesenchyme into the myometrium, while endometriosis involves similar tissue migration to organs outside the uterus, with polypoid endometriosis representing a particularly uncommon morphologic variant found across multiple sites including the ovary, bowel, bladder, and fallopian tubes. The progression of endometriosis is driven by multiple interconnected mechanisms: excessive estrogen expression and local estrogen synthesis accelerate disease, progesterone resistance impairs hormonal therapy efficacy, and elevated inflammatory factors promote chronic inflammation that facilitates microvascular angiogenesis, thereby exacerbating pain and tissue fibrosis. Immune dysfunction is fundamental to disease development, as decreased natural killer cell activity and abnormal macrophage function weaken the body's ability to eliminate abnormal endometrial tissue, while retrograde menstruation creates a permissive inflammatory environment. The patient's polypoid lesions demonstrated elevated markers including CA125, estrogen and progesterone receptors, Ki-67, and P16, with genetic mutations in KRAS commonly found in endometriotic epithelial cells. Endometriosis is strongly associated with multiple autoimmune conditions including systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren's syndrome, and rheumatoid arthritis, suggesting shared immunopathogenic mechanisms that warrant consideration in complex clinical presentations.

title: Angiomyofibroblastoma of the vulva in a 29-year-old woman: a case report of a rare entity with successful vaginal delivery
summary: Angiomyofibroblastoma (AMFB) is a rare benign mesenchymal tumor that predominantly affects premenopausal and middle-aged women, with the vulva being the most common site though cases have been documented in the uterus, vagina, and fallopian tubes. The tumor is believed to originate from perivascular stem cells that differentiate into myofibroblasts, and histopathological examination reveals bland spindle cells with characteristic immunohistochemical markers including positive staining for vimentin, desmin, smooth muscle actin, estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, B-cell lymphoma-2, and CD34, while S-100 protein is absent. AMFB exhibits benign biological behavior with slow growth, low proliferation indices (Ki-67 <5%), and minimal propensity for local recurrence, though it typically lacks a distinct capsule which may increase residual disease risk. This case is notable because the patient achieved successful vaginal delivery despite carrying the tumor during pregnancy, underscoring the tumor's compatible status with pregnancy. Simple surgical excision is curative for AMFB, making it an excellent prognostic malignancy among mesenchymal pelvic tumors.

title: Adenoid cystic carcinoma of the vulva: A case report and literature review
summary: Adenoid cystic carcinoma (ACC) of the vulva is a rare and aggressive malignancy accounting for approximately 30% of Bartholin gland carcinomas and less than 1% of all gynecological cancers. This tumor arises from glandular epithelial and myoepithelial cells, with histopathological features characterized by positive expression of cytokeratin markers (CK5/6, CK7), P63, and KI-67 (~20%), while lacking S100 and GCDFP-15 expression. Molecular drivers include the MYB-NFIB fusion gene and mutations in KRAS and KDM6A, which promote tumor development through enhanced cell proliferation, differentiation, and impaired apoptosis, while possible risk factors include chronic inflammation, abnormal hormone levels, and pregnancy-related hormonal fluctuations. Perineural invasion is a hallmark feature distinguishing vulvar ACC, leading to tumor infiltration of peripheral nerves and skeletal muscle, resulting in postoperative pain, numbness, and increased risk of local recurrence and distant metastasis, particularly to the lungs. Treatment strategies include radical surgery with negative margin achievement as the primary modality, supplemented by adjuvant radiotherapy for positive margins, with emerging targeted therapy using the tyrosine kinase inhibitor imatinib to block c-Kit signaling in CD117-expressing tumors. Prognosis is strongly influenced by surgical margin status, with patients achieving negative margins experiencing survival times 15–30 years longer than those with positive margins.

title: Brenner tumor associated with mucinous cyst and endometriosis: a case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report documents a rare concurrence of three distinct ovarian and endometrial pathologies: a benign Brenner tumor, a mucinous cystadenoma, and endometriosis within the same ovary, along with an incidental endometrioid adenocarcinoma of the endometrium. The Brenner tumor component showed characteristic immunohistochemical markers including GATA3 and CK7 positivity with low proliferative index (Ki-67 <5%), while the adjacent mucinous component expressed CK7 but not GATA3, suggesting a possible shared origin from Walthard cell nests despite their distinct epithelial differentiation patterns. Endometriosis within the ovary contributed to chronic inflammation and estrogen-driven proliferation, creating an altered microenvironment characterized by stromal remodeling and typical hormone receptor expression (ER and PR positivity in endometriotic glands and stroma). The clinical significance lies in recognizing that endometriosis is an established risk factor for malignant transformation to clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas, necessitating careful surveillance when incidentally discovered alongside other ovarian lesions. Despite the coexistence of multiple pathologies, the benign nature of the Brenner tumor and mucinous lesion was confirmed by low mitotic activity and absence of cytologic atypia, predicting an excellent prognosis following surgical excision.

title: Endometrioid endocervical adenocarcinoma in a postmenopausal woman: a case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Endometrioid-type endocervical adenocarcinoma (ECA) is a rare, non-HPV-associated cervical malignancy that arises from endometriosis rather than persistent high-risk HPV infection, distinguishing it from typical ECAs where HPV16, HPV18, and HPV45 are the primary causative agents. Unlike conventional adenocarcinomas that show diffuse p16 and CEA positivity, endometrioid ECA exhibits a distinctive immunohistochemical profile characterized by patchy p16 expression, CEA positivity, and negativity for estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and vimentin, which helps differentiate it from endometrial adenocarcinoma metastases. Endometrioid ECAs harbor specific somatic mutations in genes such as PIK3CA, PTEN, CTNNB1, KRAS, and AKT1, with some cases showing microsatellite instability (MSI-H), revealing a distinct molecular pathogenesis from HPV-driven tumors. The diagnostic challenge of endometrioid ECA is compounded by Pap smears' limited sensitivity for adenocarcinomas and the tumor's frequent location outside the easily accessible transformation zone, potentially leading to missed diagnoses even with negative recent screening. Surgical management with radical hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy followed by radiotherapy represents the standard treatment approach, demonstrating disease control at mid-term follow-up in this postmenopausal patient case.

title: Intramuscular Myxoma: Results from the Largest European Single-Center Study—Clinical and Pathological Findings and Syndromal Associations
summary:
# Summary

This European single-center study of intramuscular myxomas (IMMs) reveals that these benign soft tissue tumors are strongly associated with GNAS mutations, which also drive two syndromal conditions: Mazabraud syndrome (characterized by concurrent fibrous dysplasia) and McCune–Albright syndrome. The majority of IMMs occur in middle-aged women, with the proximal thigh, particularly the quadriceps femoris, being the predominant anatomical location. Diagnostically, MRI is the imaging modality of choice (used in 97.6% of cases), while immunohistochemistry characteristically shows CD34 positivity in approximately 90% of tumors, helping differentiate IMMs from malignant mimics such as myxoid liposarcoma and low-grade myxofibrosarcoma. Complete surgical resection is the established treatment with excellent outcomes and low recurrence risk, though patients with syndromal associations often present with multiple or recurrent tumors requiring closer surveillance for skeletal complications. Notable comorbidities include thyroid disease in nearly 30% of patients, suggesting shared molecular pathways in GNAS-associated conditions.

title: Integrated Molecular and Functional Characterization of Cervical Small-Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma Using a 3D Organoid Model
summary: This study presents an integrated molecular and functional characterization of cervical small-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (SCNEC) using 3D organoid models to understand disease pathogenesis and therapeutic responses. HPV18 infection is strongly associated with cervical SCNEC and drives tumorigenesis through integration into the host genome at the MYC locus (8q24.21), where viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate p53 and retinoblastoma protein, promoting proliferation and genomic instability. The tumors harbor recurrent somatic mutations and copy-number alterations in key oncogenes including PIK3CA (particularly the canonical p.E542K hotspot), TERT, and MYC amplification, alongside TP53 copy-number loss, which collectively drive neuroendocrine differentiation and aggressive behavior. The established organoids faithfully recapitulated the molecular and phenotypic features of the primary tumor, including HPV18 retention, neuroendocrine marker expression (synaptophysin, CD56), and high proliferative indices (Ki-67 ≥80%), providing a robust preclinical model. Functional drug screening of organoids demonstrated dose-dependent sensitivity to conventional cytotoxic agents (paclitaxel, cisplatin, etoposide) and targeted therapies including mTOR pathway inhibitors and PARP inhibitors, identifying potential therapeutic strategies for this aggressive malignancy.

title: Endometrial Dysfunction in Women with Ovarian and Uterine Tumors: What Is Known and What Should Be Learned?
summary:
# Summary

Obesity is strongly associated with chronic inflammation and hyperestrogenism, driving hyperproliferative diseases of the female reproductive system, including both benign ovarian tumors and uterine fibroids. Both uterine fibroids and benign ovarian tumors impair endometrial receptivity and can lead to implantation failure, with endometrial dysfunction serving as a critical pathogenic mechanism underlying infertility and reproductive failure. At the molecular level, benign ovarian neoplasms arise from inclusion cysts, endosalpingiosis, and inverted macropapillae, with KRAS and CDKN2A mutations predominating in mucinous tumors and BRAF mutations driving progression to serous borderline tumors and carcinomas. Uterine fibroids are driven by estrogen and progesterone signaling through mutations primarily in MED12, HMGA2, and HMGA1, which activate mitogenic pathways including AKT and Wnt ligand signaling. Immune dysfunction, characterized by loss of protective CD4+ CD8dim T cells and regulatory T cells while gaining BAFF-R expression, promotes abnormal ovarian tissue proliferation and malignant transformation. These findings underscore how metabolic dysfunction, hormonal dysregulation, and immune compromise create a multimorbid condition linking benign gynecological tumors to endometrial dysfunction and compromised fertility.

title: Endometriosis-Associated Intestinal Tumor: A Case Report and Literature Review with Comprehensive Proteomic Data
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis-associated intestinal tumors (EAITs) are rare malignancies arising from ectopic endometrial tissue, with the rectum and sigmoid colon being the most frequent sites of involvement. This case report describes a 53-year-old woman who presented with rectal bleeding and was found to have a large endometrioid adenocarcinoma of the rectum with ovarian metastasis; immunohistochemical analysis revealed the tumor was positive for PAX8, CK7, estrogen receptor, and vimentin—markers supporting an endometrioid phenotype—while being negative for CK20 and CDX2, thereby ruling out primary colorectal origin. Although EAITs carry a poor prognosis, comprehensive surgical resection including posterior pelvic exenteration with lymphadenectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin achieved favorable long-term outcomes in this patient. Proteomic analysis identified 9,294 proteins in the tumor tissue, providing biological insights for future comparative studies. The findings underscore that aggressive surgical management combined with appropriate chemotherapy and comprehensive lymph node assessment can significantly improve clinical outcomes despite the inherent aggressiveness of these rare malignancies.

title: Tumor‐Infiltrating Nociceptor Neurons in Ovarian Cancer Treatment Resistance
summary:
# Summary

High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), which accounts for over 70% of ovarian cancer deaths, is distinctly characterized by dense tumor-infiltrating nerves composed of TRPV1-expressing sensory nociceptors, whereas other ovarian cancer subtypes show minimal innervation. HGSOC cells release small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) that actively promote neurite outgrowth and recruit nerves to the tumor microenvironment, a process enhanced by carboplatin chemotherapy treatment. These tumor-infiltrating nociceptors directly contribute to treatment resistance, as evidenced by the finding that recurrent, treatment-resistant disease shows significantly higher nerve infiltration than untreated tumors, and that ablation of nociceptors improves survival when combined with carboplatin. Patients with densely innervated HGSOC tumors experience poor clinical outcomes, establishing dense innervation as a prognostic indicator. The study identifies nerve-mediated treatment resistance as a therapeutic vulnerability, demonstrating that eliminating tumor-infiltrating nociceptors sensitizes HGSOC to platinum-based chemotherapy and substantially improves survival outcomes in preclinical models.

title: Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma of the Bartholin Gland: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes adenoid cystic carcinoma (ACC) of the Bartholin gland, an extremely rare malignant vulvar tumor occurring in a 30-year-old patient presenting with moderate pain and itching in the vulvar region. The disease arises from a chromosomal translocation involving the MYB and NFIB transcription factors and is characterized by perineural invasion, a hallmark feature associated with poor prognosis and high recurrence rates despite treatment. Clinically, the tumor manifests through pelvic pain, burning sensation, bleeding, dyspareunia, and pruritus when perineural infiltration occurs. Surgical management including right hemivulvectomy, inguinal lymphadenectomy, and sentinel lymph node biopsy represents the gold standard treatment, with adjuvant radiotherapy and chemotherapy (including cisplatin for radiosensitization) recommended for cases with deep infiltration or positive surgical margins. Prognostic outcomes are significantly influenced by surgical margin status and elevated KI-67 expression, with five-year and ten-year survival rates of 77% and 60% respectively, though adenoid cystic carcinoma remains rarer than other Bartholin gland malignancies such as adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma.

title: Long-term survival in a case report with metachronous quadruple primary malignancies involving multiple organ systems
summary: This case report describes a patient who developed four metachronous primary malignancies across multiple organ systems, highlighting the complex interplay between environmental carcinogens, genetic predisposition, and field cancerization. Chronic tobacco and alcohol exposure, implicated in up to 85% of head and neck squamous cell carcinoma cases, likely contributed to the sequential development of hypopharyngeal and oropharyngeal cancers, which themselves confer elevated risk for additional primary malignancies including lymphoma and prostate cancer. The diffuse large B-cell lymphoma was characterized by typical B-cell markers (CD20, CD79a) and responded to standard R-CHOP chemotherapy with consolidative radiotherapy, while prostate adenocarcinoma was managed with external beam radiotherapy and androgen deprivation therapy based on elevated PSA and Gleason score. Key genetic alterations associated with prostate cancer risk, including PTEN inactivation, BRCA2 mutations, and RB1/TP53 mutations linked to neuroendocrine differentiation and treatment resistance, may have contributed to disease aggressiveness. The case underscores the importance of screening for hereditary cancer predisposition syndromes in patients with multiple primary malignancies and demonstrates how modern imaging modalities such as mpMRI-fusion biopsy and PSMA-PET/CT can optimize cancer detection and staging in complex cases.

title: Molecular profile and targeted therapies for ovarian clear cell ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC) is an endometriosis-associated malignancy characterized by a distinct mutational landscape dominated by ARID1A and PIK3CA mutations, each occurring in approximately 50% of cases. Loss of ARID1A, a critical SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex subunit, drives malignant progression by promoting cell-cycle advancement and is frequently associated with poor prognosis, particularly in advanced-stage disease. The disease exhibits marked chemoresistance despite platinum-based chemotherapy remaining the current standard treatment, necessitating novel therapeutic approaches. Multiple immunotherapy strategies are emerging as promising options, including pembrolizumab monotherapy and combination regimens with bevacizumab, lenvatinib, or epacadostat, with response potentially linked to biomarkers such as PPP2R1A mutations and elevated PD-L1 expression driven by PI3K pathway alterations and ARID1A loss. Targeted approaches exploiting ARID1A deficiency through ATR inhibition, alone or combined with PARP inhibition, show preclinical promise and are under clinical investigation. While early-stage OCCC generally carries favorable prognosis, the aggressive behavior of advanced-stage disease and unique immunosuppressive microenvironment, including elevated immune checkpoint molecules and paradoxically adverse CD8+ T-cell infiltration, underscore the need for refined molecular stratification and personalized treatment strategies.

title: Primary peritoneal clear cell carcinoma with metastasis mimicking ovarian carcinoma: a case report and literature review
summary: Primary peritoneal clear cell carcinoma (PPCCC) is a rare malignancy hypothesized to arise from Müllerian metaplasia or malignant transformation of endometriosis, though its precise pathogenesis remains unclear. Molecular characterization of PPCCC reveals frequent alterations in key genes including ARID1A loss and somatic PIK3CA H1047R mutations, alongside mismatch repair deficiency characterized by loss of MSH2 and MSH6 expression, which may increase tumor mutational burden and potential sensitivity to immunotherapy. Immunohistochemically, PPCCC expresses gynecologic clear cell carcinoma markers (PAX8, CK7, HNF1β) while lacking renal cell carcinoma markers (CK20, calretinin) and PD-L1, distinguishing it from other clear cell malignancies. This case presented with metastatic disease involving the peritoneum, pancreas, and spleen at diagnosis, manifesting with abdominal pain, ascites, elevated CA 125 and CA 19-9 tumor markers, and ultimately achieving FIGO stage IVB. Current treatment approaches employ cytoreductive surgery combined with platinum-based chemotherapy (gemcitabine and carboplatin), though PPCCC carries poor prognosis with frequent early recurrence and limited response to standard regimens. The identification of PIK3CA mutations in this case expands understanding of PPCCC molecular biology and suggests potential therapeutic targets through isoform-selective PIK3CA inhibitors in future treatment strategies.

title: Clinicopathology of adenocarcinoma originating from the anal gland with immunohistochemical expressions of GATA3 and TTF-1: a case report and literature review
summary: This case report describes a rare adenocarcinoma originating from the anal gland (ACAGGT) with distinctive immunohistochemical features, characterized by high GATA3 expression (90%) and low TTF-1 expression (10%), along with CK7 positivity and CK20 negativity that help establish anal gland origin. The tumor demonstrated microsatellite stability with intact mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), lacked common oncogenic mutations (KRAS, NRAS, BRAF), and expressed modest PD-L1 levels and moderate tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. Clinically, the patient presented with typical symptoms including anal pain, bleeding, and difficult defecation, conditions affecting predominantly older males (mean age 65.7 years, male-to-female ratio 2.83:1). The case emphasizes that radical surgical resection combined with chemoradiotherapy offers the best survival outcomes, and for microsatellite-stable tumors with limited immunotherapy responsiveness, combination immunotherapy approaches using agents like atezolizumab with CEA-TCB or durvalumab with tremelimumab may enhance anti-tumor activity and improve overall survival.

title: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the cervix in a perimenopausal woman: a case report and immunohistochemical study
summary: This case report describes cervical clear cell adenocarcinoma (CCAC) in a perimenopausal woman presenting with abnormal vaginal bleeding and elevated tumor markers (CA-125 and CA19-9). Immunohistochemical analysis revealed a distinctive profile characteristic of Müllerian-derived clear cell carcinoma, including positive PAX-8 and napsin-A expression, pan-cytokeratin positivity, and focal positivity for estrogen and progesterone receptors, along with a notably high Ki-67 proliferation index (70%) indicating aggressive behavior. Importantly, the tumor tested negative for high-risk human papillomavirus and demonstrated only partial, patchy p16 expression, confirming HPV-independent pathogenesis and distinguishing this case from typical cervical adenocarcinomas. The immunophenotype and clinical presentation support an etiology related to Müllerian metaplasia rather than traditional DES exposure-associated mechanisms, though possible contributions from cervical endometriosis or DNA mismatch repair deficiency are noted. The patient was treated with adjuvant chemotherapy combining paclitaxel and cisplatin following confirmation of deep stromal invasion. This case expands the incomplete understanding of CCAC immunophenotype and highlights the importance of comprehensive immunohistochemical evaluation for accurate diagnosis and classification of this rare malignancy.

title: Submucosal Colonic Endometriosis Presenting as a Sigmoid Polyp
summary: Colonic endometriosis typically involves the bowel serosa or muscularis propria, but mucosal and submucosal involvement presenting as a colorectal polyp is exceptionally rare and frequently mistaken for malignancy, creating a significant diagnostic pitfall. In this case, immunohistochemistry was critical for establishing the Müllerian origin of the lesion, demonstrating ER-positive and PAX8-positive endometrial glands with CD10-positive stromal cells, while the absence of intestinal markers (CDX2 and CK20) excluded a colonic adenoma. The combination of morphologic assessment and targeted immunophenotyping—showing endometrial markers while lacking colorectal differentiation markers—reliably distinguishes endometriosis from adenocarcinoma and prevents unnecessary staging or surgical referrals. When submucosal colonic endometriosis is completely excised endoscopically without evidence of deep infiltrative disease, conservative management with clinical follow-up is appropriate. Recognition of this diagnostic pitfall and appropriate immunological characterization are essential for avoiding over-treatment and guiding clinical decision-making in patients with mucosal bowel endometriosis.

title: Analysis of prognostic factors in ovarian clear cell carcinoma: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary: Prognostic Factors in Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma

This retrospective cohort study identifies critical prognostic factors in ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), a rare histological subtype strongly associated with endometriosis. Clinical stage emerged as the most significant predictor of both overall and progression-free survival, with early-stage disease correlating with substantially better outcomes than late-stage presentations. Notably, concurrent endometriosis was paradoxically associated with improved prognosis and earlier disease detection, likely because endometriosis symptoms prompt earlier medical consultation, resulting in more stage I diagnoses (74.3% versus 62.7% in cases without endometriosis). CA-125 normalization during treatment and final CA-125 levels ≤20 U/mL were independently associated with prolonged survival, while platinum resistance and lymph node metastasis significantly worsened outcomes. The study also highlights emerging therapeutic options including platinum-based chemotherapy and, for advanced disease, immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab and ipilimumab), with CD8+ T cell infiltration showing potential for prognostic prediction in this immunotherapy context.
